# cuekeeper — Product Research

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:10:56._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: cuekeeper

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# CueKeeper

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **7**.

## App Name

CueKeeper

## Category

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

## Target User

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

## Key Risks

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium live event operations app with clock-aware run mode, strong cue hierarchy, big-touch controls, graceful contingency handling.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Before product debate begins, infer the likely market, audience, competitors/patterns, platform expectations, risks, and native iOS opportunities from the prompt. Label assumptions clearly and turn the research into implications for the first build.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

The market is probably small-but-serious operators who live in the gap between paper run sheets and expensive show-control systems: wedding planners running ceremony/reception transitions, DJs coordinating intros and speeches, MCs keeping program order straight, stage managers for small venues, church/event producers, and freelance producers who are often alone with an iPhone in one hand. The use context is hostile to fussy UI. They are standing, moving, distracted, in bad lighting, often one-handed, with people interrupting them. That means the real product is not “event planning,” it is interruption recovery. If the app is good, the operator can lose the thread for ten seconds, look down once, and instantly know what is happening now, what is next, whether they are early or late, and what backup move exists.

The strongest comparison set is not direct app-category competitors so much as behavior patterns users already trust under pressure. Notes, Apple Reminders, Google Docs/PDF run sheets, spreadsheets, and printed timelines are the default incumbent. On the higher end, there are stage/show-cue tools and event-production platforms, but most of those are either too complex, too team-oriented, or not iPhone-native for a solo operator. The pattern worth borrowing is not “project management,” it is “mission control”: a dominant current item, unmistakable next step, drift visibility, and zero-hesitation controls. The app should feel closer to a workout live tracker, turn-by-turn navigation, or a camera capture flow than to a checklist app. If it starts looking like a database with rows, it has already lost.

The main iPhone-native opportunity is lock-screen and background resilience, but only if it stays subordinate to the core console. Live Activities make sense because this user may pocket the phone and glance at the lock screen between cues. Time-sensitive notifications are useful because silent failure under Focus would destroy trust. Haptics matter because the operator may not be staring at the screen. Widgets are only worthwhile if they support “event starts in X” or “currently running event” at a glance; otherwise they are ornamental. App Intents are useful for getting into a specific event fast, but only with confirmation because accidental launch is a real failure mode. The most native-feeling version of this app is one where entering live mode feels like entering a temporary operational state, not just opening another screen.

My biggest concern is that the product can easily fool itself into solving the planning problem instead of the live problem. Timeline building, templates, role assignment, and notes are all necessary, but they are dangerously expandable. Users already have tools for drafting a schedule. They will only switch if live mode is materially safer and faster. So the first build should optimize around a single-screen running console with four dominant actions: advance, hold, skip, and reveal contingency/jump to backup. The builder should exist to feed that console, not as a feature playground. If the first build spends too much effort on rich editing and not enough on the running state model, it will be polished but non-essential.

There are several assumptions that need to stay labeled as assumptions. One is that solo operators are willing to author a run-of-show on iPhone rather than on Mac/iPad first. That may be only partially true, so the phone builder needs to be “good enough fast,” not exhaustive. Another is that users will trust a subscription for a niche tool; that only works if the free tier proves value in a real event quickly. Another is that “contingency plan” in v1 can stay simple as notes plus a designated backup cue. That is likely enough for weddings and simple events, but it may be too thin for more theatrical operators.

The biggest risks I see are, first, state ambiguity in live mode. If the user cannot instantly tell whether a cue is upcoming, active, delayed, or skipped, the app becomes dangerous. Second, the time model can betray the product if it feels “fake.” A running timer that looks live but breaks on backgrounding will be worse than paper. Third, the UI can get seduced by elegance when this category needs blunt clarity. Small typography, subtle color coding, hidden menus, swipe-only actions, or gesture cleverness are all liabilities here. Fourth, there is a category risk that this feels too similar to a generic checklist/timeline tool unless the live console is obviously its own thing. Fifth, the premium gates can backfire badly if they are encountered anywhere near run start or mid-event.

For the first build, I would make the app revolve around three states, not many screens: setup, build, run. Setup is onboarding, sample event, and paywall boundaries. Build is a practical sequence editor with cue title, scheduled time or relative offset, role, notes, contingency note, and optional backup target. Run is the product: huge current cue, clear next cue, schedule drift, elapsed/remaining context, last action visibility, and recovery controls that do not hide under sheets. I would also preserve one design rule very aggressively: the default live screen should answer five questions without scrolling or mode switching. What is happening now? What is next? Are we on time? What can I do immediately? What is my backup if this cue breaks?

The detail the next phase must preserve is that this app wins on compressed cognition, not feature breadth. It should be designed for the moment when the operator is interrupted, slightly behind, and cannot think carefully. That means the research implication is not “add more event-management capability.” It is “design the live console as a trust instrument.” If the first build cannot make advance, hold, skip, and contingency access feel reflexive, then it is still competing with a PDF and probably losing.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Let me think through this as someone who's going to have to poke holes in whatever gets built, not just describe the market in the abstract.

The audience here is narrower and more specific than "event planners" — it's the person physically holding the phone during the live moment: a wedding planner standing at the back of a reception, a stage manager in a DJ booth or backstage corridor, an MC pacing a corporate keynote, a one-person AV operator running a school recital. That person is usually standing, often in low light or direct sun, frequently one-handed because their other hand is holding a radio, a clipboard, or literally holding a door. They are not sitting at a desk reviewing a plan — they are executing one under time pressure with adrenaline in the mix. That context should dictate almost everything about the first build: large touch targets, absurdly high contrast, no reliance on subtle color-only signaling (someone glancing at a phone in bright sun will miss a yellow-vs-orange distinction), and zero reliance on precise taps or swipes, because stress degrades fine motor control. I'd treat "can this be operated with a thumb, at a glance, without reading a full sentence" as the actual design spec, not "looks premium in a screenshot."

On comparable patterns: the honest competitor set isn't other apps, it's Notes, a printed run sheet, a spreadsheet on a phone, and stage-manager tools like Stage Timer / ShowCue-style apps or DJ set-list apps. Those printed/PDF artifacts win today for one boring reason — they never crash, never lag, never require you to find the right screen, and you can glance at any part of the sheet instantly because paper has no navigation state. Any digital tool that requires "tap into event, tap into cue list, tap into cue detail" to see the current cue has already lost to paper. The apps in this rough space that do work (timer/stopwatch-for-events tools) succeed by being almost embarrassingly simple: one big number, one or two buttons, nothing else fighting for attention. That's the pattern to learn from, not "how do I fit templates, roles, and contingency notes onto one screen" — the live console has to earn its complexity budget down to nearly zero, and everything else (building the run-of-show, assigning roles, templates) can be as rich as it needs to be because that happens beforehand, calmly, not live.

Platform-specific opportunities I'd actually bet on for v1: Live Activity on the Lock Screen / Dynamic Island is the single highest-leverage native feature here, because it means the operator doesn't even have to unlock and open the app to see "current cue, time remaining, next cue" — that's a genuine capability paper can't touch, glanceable from a locked phone in a pocket or on a stand. Haptics matter more than they'd matter in almost any other app category, because a distinct haptic pattern for "cue is running long" or "30 seconds to next cue" can be felt without looking at the phone at all — that's a real edge over any paper-based approach. Notifications with time-sensitive interruption level for "next cue in 2 minutes" are the second-highest-leverage thing. Widgets and App Intents are real but secondary — nice for pre-event countdown visibility and quick-launch, not central to the live-execution promise.

Now the risk list, and I want to be blunt about these because they're the ones that quietly sink a build like this if nobody names them upfront. First, backgrounding and Live Activity reliability is the single biggest technical risk in the whole app — iOS Live Activities have real constraints (8-hour budget, update frequency limits, they can be dismissed by the user or the system), and if the countdown showing on the Lock Screen silently goes stale or gets evicted mid-event, that's worse than not having one at all, because the operator will trust a lie. That needs an explicit fallback: the in-app live console must always be the source of truth and must always self-correct from absolute timestamps the instant it's opened, regardless of what the Live Activity was showing. Second, the free-vs-premium boundary risk: "free for simple single-event runs" is a real product tension against "must feel trustworthy" — if a wedding planner is mid-build on their one free event and discovers a template or contingency plan is gated, that's a bad moment to hit a wall, so the paywall UX itself needs to be honest and upfront (show what's free before they invest 20 minutes building a run sheet), not a bait-and-switch. Third, "role assignments" is scope creep risk in a single-operator app — if role is just a text tag on a cue ("who's responsible") that's fine and cheap, but if it starts implying who-sees-what or per-role views, that quietly reintroduces multi-device coordination through the back door, which the contract explicitly forbids. I'd flag that as something the design phase needs to nail down concretely: role is a label for accountability and print/reference purposes only, never a permissions or view-filtering mechanism.

Fourth risk, more mundane but real: cue lists for actual events (weddings routinely have 20-40 cues once you count vendor arrivals, sound checks, speeches, cake cutting, etc.) mean the live console needs a fast way to jump to an arbitrary point, not just linear next/previous — if the ceremony runs 15 minutes over and three cues get skipped, "advance" 15 times is a failure of the product's own thesis. That's really a corollary of the contingency/recovery requirement already in the contract, but I want it named specifically against list length, because a demo built with a 5-cue toy event will hide this problem completely and it won't surface until someone tests with a real 30-cue wedding timeline.

Fifth, and this is a pure testing risk rather than a design risk: because so much of this app's value proposition is background/foreground timing correctness and offline behavior, the "final review" for this app cannot be a simulator walkthrough. It needs an actual device pass — lock the phone mid-cue, wait, unlock, confirm state; toggle airplane mode and run all three core workflows; check Live Activity behavior after a forced-quit. If those checks don't happen on real hardware, "verified" is a claim with nothing behind it, and given the invalidation criterion is specifically about trustworthiness under pressure, that gap would undermine the whole premise of the app, not just be a minor testing gap.

For what the first build should actually contain, given all that: nail the live console down to something almost stupidly simple — current cue, time remaining/elapsed with clear over/under-time indication, next cue preview, and three or four unmistakable big-touch actions (advance, hold/pause timer, jump-to-backup, reveal contingency note) — before spending any real effort on template richness or role metadata. Build the timeline/cue editor to be merely competent, not beautiful, in v1; the live console is where this app either proves itself or doesn't. And I'd treat the Live-Activity-goes-stale scenario as a must-test-before-ship item, not a nice-to-have, because it's the one failure mode that would make this app actively worse than a piece of paper — paper never shows you a stale, comfortingly wrong number.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Both sides converged on the same picture — no real disagreement left, just Claude adding operational detail on top of Codex's framing. Both frame this as "interruption recovery under pressure," not event planning, and both name the same true competitor: paper/PDF/Notes, which wins today purely because it never crashes and needs no navigation. Both agree the pattern to copy isn't project-management software, it's glanceable live-tracking UX (turn-by-turn nav, workout trackers, mission-control style dashboards) — big dominant current state, unmistakable next, drift visibility, blunt controls, no subtlety or hidden menus. Both agree Live Activity/Lock Screen is the single highest-leverage native feature since it beats paper on something paper structurally can't do (glance without unlocking), followed by time-sensitive notifications and haptics, with widgets/App Intents as secondary nice-to-haves. And both land on the same implication for the first build: pour effort into a dead-simple live console (current cue, next cue, on/off-time state, advance/hold/skip/reveal-backup) and keep the builder merely competent — the console is the product, not the editor.

Claude's round added concrete risk items Codex didn't cover, but nothing that contradicts Codex — it's additive: Live Activity staleness/eviction as a named technical risk (with the rule that the in-app console must always self-correct from absolute timestamps regardless of what the Live Activity showed), the free/premium boundary needing to be disclosed honestly before someone invests time building a run sheet, "role" needing to stay a label rather than accidentally reintroducing multi-device permissioning, real event scale (20-40 cues) breaking any design that only supports linear advance, and a hard requirement that verification happen on real hardware (lock/unlock mid-cue, airplane mode, forced-quit) rather than a simulator glance.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** Solo operators physically executing a live event — wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, small production leads. They're standing, often one-handed, in bad lighting, interrupted, under adrenaline. The product isn't "event planning," it's interruption recovery: glance down for 2 seconds, instantly know now/next/on-or-off-time/backup.

**Comparable patterns:** The real competitor is paper/PDF/Notes/spreadsheets, which win today because they need zero navigation and never crash. The pattern to emulate is glanceable live-tracking UX (turn-by-turn nav, workout trackers, simple timer apps) — one dominant number/state, one or two unmistakable actions — not project-management or checklist software. If the build starts looking like rows in a database, it's already lost to paper.

**Platform-specific opportunities:** Live Activity/Dynamic Island is the top native opportunity — glanceable from a locked phone, something paper genuinely can't match — but it must never be the source of truth; the in-app console has to self-correct from absolute timestamps the instant it's opened, since Live Activities can go stale, hit update-frequency limits, or get evicted. Time-sensitive notifications and distinct haptic patterns (e.g., "running long," "cue in 30s") are the next tier of value, usable without even looking at the screen. Widgets and App Intents are real but secondary — pre-event countdown visibility and quick-launch, not core to the live promise, and App Intents must always confirm before launching a run.

**Major assumptions and risks (labeled as assumptions/risks, not verified facts):**
1. Assumption: solo operators will accept authoring a run-of-show on iPhone if the builder is "good enough fast," not exhaustive — full authoring may eventually want iPad/Mac, but that's out of scope for v1.
2. Risk: Live Activity staleness/eviction misleading the operator is worse than no Live Activity at all — must be tested and mitigated with in-app self-correction.
3. Risk: real events run 20-40+ cues; a console that only supports linear "advance" fails the moment cues get skipped or a section runs over — arbitrary jump/backup-target support is required, not optional polish.
4. Risk: paywall encountered mid-build or mid-event destroys trust — free/premium boundaries must be disclosed upfront, and gating already contracted to creation-time-only, never mid-run.
5. Risk: "role assignment" scope-creeping into permissions/per-role views would reintroduce multi-device coordination through the back door — role must stay a plain accountability label.
6. Risk: verifying timing/offline/backgrounding claims in the simulator instead of on real hardware would produce false confidence — final review must include an actual device pass (lock/unlock mid-cue, airplane mode, forced-quit of Live Activity).

**Implications for the first build:** Concentrate the build effort on a minimal, almost stupidly simple live console — current cue (dominant), next cue, elapsed/drift state with unmistakable over/under-time signaling, and 3-4 big-touch actions (advance, hold, skip/jump-to-arbitrary-cue, reveal contingency/backup) — before investing in template richness or cue-editor polish. The builder/editor should be competent, not beautiful, in v1. Live Activity + time-sensitive notifications + haptics are the native features worth building well; widgets/App Intents are real but secondary and are the first things to trim under pressure per the existing contract. This all lines up cleanly with the locked prompt contract — nothing here contradicts it, it's giving the design phase concrete texture to build against.

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** Solo operators physically executing a live event — wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, small production leads. They're standing, often one-handed, in bad lighting, interrupted, under adrenaline. The product isn't "event planning," it's interruption recovery: glance down for 2 seconds, instantly know now/next/on-or-off-time/backup.

**Comparable patterns:** The real competitor is paper/PDF/Notes/spreadsheets, which win today because they need zero navigation and never crash. Widgets and App Intents are real but secondary — pre-event countdown visibility and quick-launch, not core to the live promise, and App Intents must always confirm before launching a run.

5. Risk: "role assignment" scope-creeping into permissions/per-role views would reintroduce multi-device coordination through the back door — role must stay a plain accountability label.
6. Risk: verifying timing/offline/backgrounding claims in the simulator instead of on real hardware would produce false confidence — final review must include an actual device pass (lock/unlock mid-cue, airplane mode, forced-quit of Live Activity).

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

## Target User

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

## Key Risks

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination

Design a premium live event operations app with clock-aware run mode, strong cue hierarchy, big-touch controls, graceful contingency handling.

8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.

11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

**Claude (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

This thread is a single-app run, not a portfolio parent, and I want to say that plainly before anything else: the prompt bundle we received already has PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true, a parent portfolio name (multi-app-exp4), and one selected app slug (cuekeeper) baked in. The candidate-generation, "pick the best 7," Jira/Notion, and cross-app transcript work described in the parent portfolio prompt is real, but it is machinery that runs one level above this conversation — it already happened (or is happening in sibling threads) to produce the fact that CueKeeper is the app assigned here at build priority 7 of 7, category event-operations. If I tried to re-run "generate 10 candidates, pick 7" inside this thread, I'd be duplicating decisions that aren't mine to make and that I don't have visibility into (I can't see the other six apps' actual specs, only that Waylay and TrueScale exist as names). So no portfolio-json manifest is needed here — there's exactly one child app in play in this thread, it maps to exactly one output folder (`cuekeeper`), and nothing about the prompt asks this specific conversation to spawn siblings. I'm recording that explicitly rather than silently assuming it, per the phase rules.

With that settled, let me actually argue the product case for this one app, because "we were told to build it" isn't the same as "it deserves to exist," and the invalidation criterion in our own contract says as much.

The real user in my head is a wedding day-of coordinator, standing at the back of a reception hall, one hand on a radio, the other holding an iPhone, trying to figure out if the toasts are running four minutes long and whether that pushes the cake cutting into the band's first set. She currently uses a laminated printout or a Notes doc with times typed next to each line item. That thing has exactly one superpower: it never lags, never asks her to tap through a menu, and never needs a signal. If CueKeeper can't beat that experience at the moment she's mid-crisis, it has no reason to exist — that's not a hypothetical, it's literally the invalidation criterion baked into the spec, and I want to keep it in the front of the room rather than let it become a plaque on the wall nobody looks at again.

Where I think the real value is: not in the timeline builder — spreadsheets and Notes are perfectly fine at rest-state planning — but in the moment things go sideways live. The pain isn't "I don't have a schedule," it's "I have a schedule and reality just diverged from it, and I have three seconds to decide what to do next." A tool earns its subscription fee by making that three-second decision faster and more confident than glancing at paper. That means the product's real center of gravity is the live console, not the editor, and if a later phase spends its energy on rich template galleries or elaborate role-permission systems instead of hardening the run/recover loop, it will have built a nicer-looking version of the thing that already loses to paper.

The biggest risk to my own framing, and I want to name it against myself rather than wait for someone else to: "single operator, no team sync" might be underselling what these users actually do day-to-day. A stage manager frequently is coordinating with a DJ, a caterer, an officiant — multiple humans — even if only one phone is running the app. There's a temptation for a later phase to read "role assignments" in the spec and start building toward multi-person visibility because that's what the job actually looks like in the room. I think that temptation has to be resisted hard for v1 specifically because the contract already forecloses it (no multi-device coordination, no client portal), and because trying to be a lightweight team tool and a reflexive single-operator instrument at the same time is how you end up mediocre at both. If that constraint turns out to be wrong, it should be revisited explicitly in a future version, not smuggled in now.

The detail I want the next phase to preserve above all else: this app is being judged by whether a stressed, standing, one-handed operator can do four things — see current state at a glance, advance, recover from drift, and reach a backup plan — faster than flipping to the right page on a printed sheet. Every design and architecture decision downstream should be traceable back to that test. If a feature can't be justified against that specific person in that specific moment, it's scope creep, regardless of how reasonable it looks in a feature list.

event-operations

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics

- App Intents for quick event start

## Phase Purpose

Establish a shared, precise understanding of what this app is, who it is for, the core problem it solves, hard scope boundaries, and measurable success criteria.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

CueKeeper should be defined in one sentence as: a local-first iPhone run-of-show app for solo live-event operators that lets them see the current cue, next cue, timing drift, and backup move instantly, then advance or recover in one or two taps even offline.

The primary user is not “an event team,” it’s one person actively running the room under pressure: a wedding planner at the back of a reception, a stage manager in the wings, a DJ/MC juggling announcements and timing. The scenario to keep in the room is simple: the event is live, timing has slipped, somebody asks a question, and the operator needs to glance down and know what is happening now, what comes next, whether they are ahead or behind, and what to do if the plan just broke. If the app cannot beat a printed run sheet or Notes in that exact moment, it has failed.

The core loop should stay brutally tight. Before the event, the user creates or edits a run-of-show with cues, times or offsets, roles as simple labels, contingency notes, and an optional backup cue target. During the event, they enter a dedicated live mode that shows one dominant current cue, one clear next cue, current timing state, and large controls for advance, hold, skip, and reveal contingency or jump to backup. When reality changes, the app’s job is not to help them “re-plan” broadly; it is to help them recover immediately and keep moving.

The hard scope boundary is what makes this build viable. This is single-operator only. No team sync, no shared editing, no remote cue pushing, no client portal, no multi-device coordination. “Role assignment” is a text label, not permissions. Contingency handling in v1 is notes plus jump-to-designated-backup, not full branching logic. The builder only needs to be good enough to produce a credible event timeline on iPhone; it does not need to become a full planning suite. Live Activities, widgets, and App Intents matter only if the run loop is already excellent; they are secondary to the core console. The product is live execution, not project management, not documentation export, and not show-control for complex productions.

The measurable success criteria should be operational, not aesthetic. A first-time user can onboard, start from a blank event or template, build a usable 20-40 cue run sheet, enter live mode, and perform advance, hold, skip, and contingency reveal or backup jump from the default run screen in no more than two taps each. Timing survives backgrounding and relaunch because it is computed from stored dates, not a naive counter. The app remains usable in airplane mode across build, run, and recovery workflows. The live screen answers, without scrolling, what is happening now, what is next, whether the event is on time, and what the immediate recovery action is. If those things are true, this is a product. If not, it is just a nicer schedule viewer.

The biggest risk in this direction is that the app quietly expands sideways into “event planner with a run button” because timeline editing, templates, and metadata are easier to add than a genuinely trustworthy live console. The final phase output needs to preserve one detail above all: CueKeeper exists to reduce cognitive load during live disruption, not to maximize planning breadth. Every later decision should be judged against that.

**Claude (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Let me give my independent first pass on turning this into one precise product contract, thinking about it primarily as information architecture and edge-case exposure rather than restating the spec.

The one-sentence promise I'd lock in: CueKeeper lets a solo live-event operator build a cue sequence once, then run it under real-time pressure with a console that always shows correct elapsed/remaining state — even after the phone was locked, backgrounded, or offline for the last twenty minutes — and lets them recover from drift or a broken cue in two taps or fewer. That's tighter than "run a live event without losing the sequence" because it names the actual mechanism (self-correcting from absolute time, not an assumption that the screen was open the whole time) and the actual bar (two taps), both of which are already locked from the contract phase. If a later phase can't restate the promise that specifically, it's drifted back toward "event planning app" framing.

Primary user and scenario, picked concretely rather than left as a persona list: a wedding coordinator standing at the back of a reception, phone in one hand, radio or drink in the other, who just got tapped on the shoulder mid-toast and needs to know in under two seconds whether the room is running behind and what happens next. That's the scenario every IA decision should be tested against. Not "she's planning next month's wedding at her desk" — that's a real workflow too (the builder), but it's the calm-state workflow and should be designed for competence, not reflex speed. The live console is designed for the opposite: no calm, no time to read, no patience for a menu.

Core loop, stated as three states with a specific transition model, because I think this is where ambiguity will actually bite: Build (add/reorder/template cues, assign role labels, attach contingency notes and a designated backup target per cue) → Run (enter live mode, which locks the sequence and starts deriving state from absolute timestamps) → Recover (advance, hold, skip, or jump-to-backup when reality diverges from plan). The detail I want preserved is what "jump to backup" actually does structurally: it's a pointer relocation, not a branch. The original sequence still exists and is still walkable — jumping to a backup cue doesn't fork the show into an alternate timeline, it just says "the current pointer is now here" while every other cue's scheduled time and existence is untouched. That matters because if a later phase implements this as true branching, we've silently violated the non-goal of contingency logic being lightweight, and if it's implemented as a destructive replace, the operator loses the ability to jump back to where they actually were. I'd also flag now, before it's built, that the console needs an explicit answer for what happens after the very last cue completes or is skipped — is there an "event complete" terminal state with its own screen, or does the console just... stop? That's a real state a first-time user will hit at literally every event and it can't be an afterthought discovered during implementation.

Hard scope boundaries I want stated plainly, on top of what's already locked: single device, single sequence per event (no parallel tracks — a stage manager coordinating both a ceremony track and a reception track is a real use case but it's a v2 problem, not v1), no editing the cue list from inside live mode beyond the recovery actions already named (advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup/note-edit) — if someone wants to insert a brand-new cue mid-run because the caterer showed up early, that's explicitly out of scope for v1 and should surface as a graceful "you can't restructure mid-run, but you can jump to a backup or add a note" rather than a dead end or a crash. That's a real gap users will hit, and naming it now as an intentional non-goal is better than discovering it as an unplanned limitation during build.

Measurable success criteria, kept concrete rather than aspirational: the enumerated tap count for advance/hold/skip/contingency-jump from the default running live-console state must each be ≤2, and that count has to be written down as an actual enumerated path (screen → tap → resulting state), not asserted. State must survive a real lock-screen/background/relaunch cycle mid-cue with the displayed elapsed/remaining time correct to the second, verified on device, not in the simulator. A cold-start operator must be able to go from onboarding to a running live console using only a template in under two minutes, without reading documentation. All of that is falsifiable, which is the point — "feels trustworthy" isn't a success criterion, "recovers correctly after being locked in a pocket for eleven minutes" is.

The biggest risk to my own framing: by putting so much emphasis on the live console as the entire product, I'm implicitly telling the next phases to underinvest in the builder, and if the builder is too crude, operators won't get a plausible cue sequence built at all — the console has nothing to run if authoring a 25-cue wedding timeline takes fifteen frustrated minutes of fiddly reordering. So "builder should be merely competent" needs a floor, not just a ceiling: fast add, drag-reorder, and duplicate-from-template have to work well even though they don't need to be beautiful. I'd rather name that tension now than let "console is the product" get used later to justify a genuinely bad authoring experience.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where things stand after round one: both Codex and Claude landed on basically the same shape for CueKeeper — a solo-operator run-of-show app whose whole reason to exist is the live moment when timing has slipped and someone needs to glance down and instantly know what's happening, what's next, and what to do about it. Same primary user (the standing, one-handed, mid-crisis operator), same core loop (build calmly, run under pressure, recover in one or two taps), and the same non-negotiable scope fence: single device, single operator, role is just a label, contingency is notes-plus-jump-to-backup, and the builder just needs to be "good enough fast," not a planning suite.

Where Claude pushed further than Codex did, in ways Codex hasn't weighed in on yet: 

- A specific mechanical definition of "jump to backup" — it's a pointer relocation (the live cursor moves), not a branch or destructive replace, so the original sequence stays fully intact and walkable.
- An explicit question about what happens after the last cue: is there a real "event complete" screen, or does the console just stop? Claude flagged this as something that can't be discovered mid-build.
- A harder boundary than Codex stated: no parallel tracks (one sequence per event, v2 problem), and no restructuring the cue list from inside live mode — inserting a new cue mid-run is explicitly out of scope, with a defined graceful message rather than a dead end.
- A self-flagged risk: "builder should be merely competent" needs a floor, not just a ceiling — fast add/reorder/duplicate-from-template must genuinely work well, or there's nothing for the console to run.

None of that contradicts Codex's framing, but it's new and specific enough that it hasn't actually been confirmed by the other voice yet — it's Claude putting proposals on the table, not something both sides have already shaken hands on.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex to react directly to Claude's four additions above — especially the terminal "event complete" state (does it need its own screen, yes/no) and the "no mid-run restructuring" boundary — so those land as agreed contract lines rather than one-sided proposals before this phase locks.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Claude’s additions are mostly right, and I don’t think we should soft-pedal them into “nice clarifications.” They actually make the product definition safer. The strongest one is the pointer-relocation definition for backup jumps. That is exactly the kind of thing that seems minor in discussion and becomes product-shaping once built. If “jump to backup” branches the run into some alternate sequence model, we’ve blown the v1 scope. If it destructively replaces the current path, we’ve made recovery brittle and confusing. The right behavior is simpler and more operator-trustworthy: the run has one canonical sequence, the live pointer can move to a designated backup cue, and the operator can still see and reach the original surrounding cues afterward. That fits the actual user moment too. A wedding planner doesn’t want to enter some abstract alternate timeline because the toast went long; they want to say “skip to cake cutting” and keep the rest of the night legible.

I also agree with Claude that we should stop leaving the end-of-event behavior fuzzy. “What happens after the last cue?” is not an edge case. It happens every single run. I’d make that a real terminal state, not just an empty console. The app should explicitly say the event is complete, show final timing status, last completed cue, and a couple of calm post-run actions like review notes or return to event overview. That matters because the product promise is trust under pressure, and trust includes not feeling like the app falls off a cliff when the run ends. On the mid-run editing boundary, I’m with Claude too: no restructuring the cue list in live mode. That sounds restrictive, but it is exactly the kind of restriction that keeps the app fast and safe. If someone needs to insert a brand-new cue live, that is really a planning-tool behavior, and it drags the run console back toward being a tiny spreadsheet. For v1, recovery means advance, hold, skip, reveal contingency, jump to backup, maybe add a quick note. Not re-author the show in the middle of it.

Where I want to sharpen Claude’s point is the builder floor. “Merely competent” can’t become permission to ship a frustrating authoring flow. Our real user will tolerate a narrower builder, but not a tedious one. If creating a 30-cue reception run sheet on iPhone feels like pecking through forms one row at a time, the operator may never reach the part of the app that’s actually good. So I’d lock the builder promise more concretely: blank event or template start, very fast cue add, easy drag reorder, duplicate cue, simple role label, contingency note, backup target, done. No elaborate metadata, no dependency graphs, no multi-track views. But those few actions have to feel quick enough that a first-time user can build a plausible event without resentment. So I think the contract we should converge on is: one-sequence, single-operator live execution tool; backup jump is pointer relocation; live mode is operationally locked except for recovery actions and notes; event completion is a real state; and the builder is intentionally narrow but must still be fast enough to produce a credible 20 to 40 cue run without friction becoming the story. That keeps the product honest to the user we keep naming instead of letting it sprawl into “event planner with a live tab.”

**Claude (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Codex's line "the app exists to reduce cognitive load during live disruption, not maximize planning breadth" is exactly the north star I want carried forward, and I think it actually strengthens the case for locking down the four things I raised rather than treating them as extra scope — because every one of them is really a "what happens when disruption hits" question, which is the whole point of the product.

Take the event-complete terminal state. Codex's core loop describes build→run→recover, but recover implies there's always a next thing to recover toward. Every real event ends. If the console's answer to "last cue just finished" is ambiguous — does it show the last cue frozen forever, does it silently fall through to some undefined screen, does it crash on an out-of-bounds index — that's not an edge case, it's a certainty that happens at 100% of events, at the exact moment the operator is winding down and probably showing the screen to a curious client or vendor. I want to nail this down as a real screen state: when the last cue is marked done or skipped, the console transitions to an explicit "event complete" state — final elapsed time, a wrap-up affordance, no more advance/hold buttons since there's nothing left to advance to. That's cheap to build and expensive to retrofit once someone's already coded the console as "current cue index + 1, no bounds check."

On "jump to backup is a pointer relocation, not a branch" — I want to push harder on why this isn't just an implementation nicety, it's actually load-bearing for a scenario Codex's own framing predicts: timing drift recovery. Say cue 14 of 30 breaks (officiant runs long) and the operator jumps to backup cue 22 (skip the receiving line, go straight to dinner service). If cues 15-21 still exist as walkable, skippable, still-visible-in-the-list entries, the operator can glance at the full picture and consciously decide "I'm also skipping the toasts, mark those done" one at a time, with full visibility into what got abandoned versus what's still pending. If instead the backup jump is implemented as "replace the remaining sequence with the backup branch," the operator loses the ability to see or recover any of cues 15-21 — and worse, if it turns out cue 19 (something like "cut the cake") still needs to happen, there's no path back to it. Pointer relocation preserves optionality under exactly the kind of pressure this app is designed for; branching removes it. I don't think this is a minor technical detail, I think it's the difference between the app being trustworthy and being another rigid tool that assumes it knows better than the person on the ground.

On no-mid-run-restructuring — I want to be more precise here than I was last round, because "out of scope" alone isn't enough; the UX response matters. If a caterer shows up 20 minutes early and the operator wants to slot in an unplanned cue, the wrong failure mode is a dead tap or a buried "you can't do that" toast. The right failure mode is: the contingency-note field on the current cue becomes the release valve — "caterer arrived early, doing informally" typed into the note, and the operator either holds the current cue longer or jumps to a nearby backup. That's not a workaround I'm inventing on the fly, it's actually forced by the non-goal Codex and the locked contract already agreed to (no branching, no mid-run restructuring) — I just want it made explicit that the contingency note is the intended pressure-release mechanism for "something happened that isn't in the plan," so a later phase doesn't build a UI that leaves the operator with no in-app move at all in that moment.

And on the builder floor — I'll go further than "needs to work well." I think there's a concrete, testable line: duplicating a template into a new event and reordering an existing cue must each be effectively one gesture (tap "use template," drag to reorder), not a form-heavy multi-screen flow. If building a 25-cue timeline from a template takes more than a couple minutes of taps, the operator's actual behavior in the field will be to keep using their old PDF for the next event too, because they never trusted the builder enough to invest the setup time — which quietly kills the whole product before the console ever gets used. So I'd write this into the success criteria alongside the tap-count and backgrounding tests: applying a template and reaching a "ready to run" event takes under two minutes for a first-time user, measured the same way we're measuring the live-console tap counts — not vibes, an actual timed walk-through.

One more thing I want on the table since nobody's raised it yet: what does "hold" actually freeze? If hold pauses the on-screen countdown but the underlying stored timestamps keep marching forward in real time (which they have to, since we're deriving state from absolute dates, not a counter), then the moment the operator taps hold again to resume, there needs to be a clear rule for what "resume" means — does the whole remaining schedule shift forward by the hold duration, or does only the current cue's target time shift while everything after it stays anchored to its original wall-clock time? I think the sane answer, and the one consistent with "recompute from absolute timestamps," is that hold shifts everything downstream by the held duration — otherwise "hold" is cosmetic and the drift indicator will show the event wildly behind two cues later even though the operator explicitly told the app "we're waiting on purpose." That's a real design decision, not an implementation detail, and it needs to be written down now so build doesn't quietly pick whichever behavior falls out of the first shipped data model.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Good round — Codex came back and explicitly signed off on all four of Claude's proposals from last round, not just tolerated them: pointer-relocation for backup jump (not branching, not destructive replace — the full sequence stays visible and walkable), a real "event complete" terminal state instead of an undefined fall-through, no mid-run restructuring, and a sharpened builder floor. They even converged on wording for a locked contract sentence covering all of it.

Claude then took another pass and pushed each point one level more concrete: the event-complete screen needs specific contents (final elapsed time, wrap-up affordance, buttons that make sense when there's nothing left to advance to); the pointer-relocation argument got a worked example (jump from cue 14 to backup cue 22, cues 15-21 stay visible/walkable so nothing gets silently lost); the "no mid-run restructuring" rule got its missing piece — the contingency note is explicitly the pressure-release valve for "something unplanned happened," so operators aren't left with zero in-app move; and the builder floor became testable (template-to-ready-to-run in under two minutes, timed, not vibes).

But Claude also raised something brand new that nobody's answered yet: what does "hold" actually do to the schedule? Since timing is derived from absolute timestamps, pausing the countdown doesn't stop time — so when the operator resumes, does the rest of the night's cue times shift forward by however long they held, or does only that one cue move while everything after it stays anchored to its original clock time? Claude's instinct is "shift everything downstream," because otherwise "hold" is just cosmetic and the drift indicator will falsely show the event way behind two cues later. That's a real, no-small-print design decision and it's sitting open — Codex hasn't weighed in.

CONSENSUS: NO

Everything from round 1 is now locked and mutually confirmed. The one loose thread for next round is the "hold" semantics question — does resuming from hold shift all downstream cue times forward by the held duration, or only the current one — and getting Codex (or whoever closes this out) to actually take a position on it before this phase can call itself done.

### Round 3


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 3**

Claude’s right to force the “hold” question into the contract, because if we leave it vague the build will accidentally choose a behavior and then we’ll rationalize it afterward. I think the answer is: hold is an intentional schedule shift, not a cosmetic pause. When the operator hits hold, the current live state freezes visibly, and when they resume, the app pushes all downstream planned times forward by the held duration. That is the only behavior that matches the mental model of the person actually using this thing. If a wedding coordinator pauses because the bride’s father disappeared for six minutes, she is not saying “pretend the schedule is still anchored to the original clock and just show me as late forever.” She is saying “we are deliberately waiting; move the rest of tonight with us.” Anything else makes the hold button dishonest. The app can still show drift against the original plan somewhere if we want that historical truth, but the primary live schedule has to rebase forward or the operator will stop trusting the controls.

I also want to connect that back to the rest of what Claude and I already aligned on, because it all forms one coherent product now, not a grab bag of rules. Jump-to-backup as pointer relocation, no mid-run restructuring, contingency note as the pressure-release valve, real event-complete state, and hold shifting the downstream schedule are all really the same philosophy: don’t make the operator model branching logic in their head during a live event. Give them one sequence, one live pointer, one honest notion of “where the night is now,” and a few explicit recovery moves that preserve legibility instead of creating hidden state. That keeps the product small enough to build and strong enough to matter. So the contract I’d lock is: CueKeeper is a local-first iPhone run-of-show app for one live-event operator; they build a single cue sequence with role labels, notes, contingency notes, and optional backup targets; live mode shows current cue, next cue, timing state, and big recovery controls; advance, hold, skip, and contingency reveal or backup jump all stay within two taps; hold rebases the remaining schedule forward by the held duration; backup jump moves the live pointer without destroying the original sequence; the event ends in an explicit completion state; and anything that smells like multi-user coordination, parallel tracks, branching show logic, or mid-run re-authoring stays out. If we say it that plainly, a developer can build it, a tester can falsify it, and a real operator can tell whether it beats their printed sheet.

**Claude (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 3**

Since Codex didn't take a position on hold semantics and the coordinator's flagging it as the one open thread, let me actually push this through to a decision rather than leave it dangling for a fourth round, because I think there's a wrinkle in my own round-2 proposal that's worth surfacing before we lock "shift everything downstream" as the rule.

The reason "shift downstream" is right isn't just "otherwise hold is cosmetic" — it's that hold is a *deliberate, acknowledged* pause, and the whole product's value proposition is distinguishing intentional decisions from accidental drift. If the officiant runs long and the operator does nothing, that's drift, and the app should absolutely show them falling behind — that's the honest signal the product exists to give. But if the operator taps hold because the flower girl needs a minute and everyone in the room knows it, that's not drift, that's a choice, and the app punishing them for it by showing "12 minutes behind" the second they resume would actively erode trust in the drift indicator itself — the operator would start ignoring it, which defeats the entire premise Codex and I both keep hammering on (the console has to be more trustworthy than paper, and paper doesn't guilt-trip you for pausing). So I want to go one step further than just "shift downstream": when the operator taps resume, the on-time/drift baseline should recalibrate to zero at that moment. The hold duration gets baked into every downstream cue's target time as a permanent offset, and "drift" starts measuring fresh from the moment of resume, not from the original pre-hold schedule. That way hold behaves exactly like the operator telling the app "yes, I know, this was on purpose" — and any further lateness after that is new information they actually need to react to.

There's a real edge case this surfaces that I think is worth naming now rather than letting the architecture phase discover it by accident: are cue times, under the hood, absolute clock anchors set at build time (band starts at 8:00 PM sharp because that's literally in the vendor contract), or are they relative offsets computed forward from event start / previous cue? Those are different products in a subtle but real way. If everything is relative offsets, "shift downstream on hold" is trivially correct and consistent — the whole schedule is just a chain of durations, and delaying one link delays everything after it, full stop. But real wedding timelines have both kinds of cues — most things are elastic ("toasts, however long they run"), but some things are genuinely hard-anchored to a external commitment the operator can't move ("band is contracted 8 to 11, full stop," "venue kicks everyone out at midnight"). If hold silently pushes a hard-anchored cue later along with everything else, that's not a recovery tool, that's the app quietly lying to the operator about something that can't actually move.

I don't think we should solve hard-anchored cues in v1 — that's a real feature (a per-cue "locked to wall clock" flag) that adds real complexity, and it wasn't in anyone's spec. But I think we have to name it explicitly as a known limitation rather than let "shift downstream" get written down as if it's universally correct: for v1, all cue times are relative offsets recomputed forward through the chain, hold/resume bakes its duration into that chain permanently, and drift resets to zero at resume. If an operator has a genuinely fixed external commitment later in the night, the app will represent it as just another cue that also shifts under hold — which is a real, honest limitation of a v1 that treats the whole show as one elastic chain, and it should be reported as such rather than silently glossed over, consistent with how we've been treating every other honesty requirement in this build (offline claims, timing claims, paywall claims). That gives us a decisive, buildable answer instead of an open question, and it's consistent with everything Codex and I have converged on rather than introducing new scope.

With that, I think this phase is actually done — Codex's round 2 message signed off on all four of my prior points with matching language, I've now closed the one loose thread the coordinator flagged, and nothing here contradicts the locked prompt contract or the product research. I'd call consensus this round unless Codex sees a problem with the relative-offset-only limitation.

**Gemini — Round 3 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

This one actually landed. Codex took a clear position on the hold question — hold is a deliberate schedule shift, not a cosmetic pause, so resuming pushes every downstream cue's time forward by the held duration, because a wedding coordinator pausing on purpose isn't "drift," it's a choice, and the app shouldn't punish her for it. Claude then took that one step further rather than contesting it: not only does the schedule shift, the drift/on-time baseline should reset to zero the moment the operator resumes, so lateness only gets flagged again from new information going forward — otherwise the drift indicator would guilt-trip people for a pause they explicitly chose, and they'd learn to ignore it, which defeats the whole point. Claude also surfaced one honest limitation that needs to be on the record rather than discovered later: v1 treats every cue time as a relative offset chained forward from event start, not a hard clock anchor — so something genuinely fixed to the outside world ("band contracted 8–11 sharp") would still slide if a hold happens earlier in the night. That's named explicitly as a known v1 limitation, not solved now, consistent with how everything else in this build (offline, timing, paywall) gets treated — real gaps get written down, not glossed over.

Nothing here contradicts anything Codex said — it's the same "one sequence, one honest live pointer, no hidden state" philosophy Codex was already arguing for, just carried one notch further. That closes the one loose thread from last round, and everything from rounds 1–2 (pointer-relocation for backup jump, real event-complete terminal state, no mid-run restructuring with the contingency note as the release valve, and the timed builder floor) was already mutually confirmed.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** CueKeeper is a local-first iPhone app that lets a solo live-event operator build a cue sequence once, then run it under real pressure with a console that always shows the correct, self-correcting elapsed/remaining state — even after the phone was locked, backgrounded, or offline — and lets them recover from drift or a broken cue in two taps or fewer.

**Primary user and scenario:** One person physically running the room — a wedding coordinator, stage manager, DJ, or MC — standing, often one-handed, interrupted, under time pressure. The defining scenario: the event is live, timing has slipped or something just broke, and the operator has about two seconds to glance down and know what's happening now, what's next, whether they're on time, and what to do about it. If the app can't beat a printed run sheet or Notes in that exact moment, it fails its own invalidation test.

**Core loop:** Build (calmly, beforehand: add/reorder cues, apply a template, attach role labels, notes, contingency notes, and an optional backup target) → Run (enter live mode; the sequence locks and all state derives from absolute timestamps, never an accumulating counter) → Recover (advance, hold, skip, or jump-to-backup when reality diverges from plan). The whole loop rests on one sequence and one live pointer — never a branching alternate show, never hidden state the operator has to reconstruct in their head.

**Hard scope boundaries:**
- Single device, single operator, single sequence per event — no parallel tracks, no team sync, no multi-device coordination.
- "Role" is a plain accountability label, never permissions or per-role views.
- Contingency handling in v1 = free-text notes per cue + jump-to-designated-backup. No true branching show logic.
- "Jump to backup" is a pointer relocation, not a branch and not a destructive replace — the full original sequence stays visible and walkable afterward (e.g., jump from cue 14 to cue 22, cues 15–21 remain visible/reachable, nothing silently disappears).
- No restructuring the cue list from inside live mode. Recovery actions are limited to advance/hold/skip/reveal-contingency/jump-to-backup/edit-note. If something unplanned happens (caterer shows up early), the contingency note is the explicit release valve — that's the intended in-app move, not a dead end.
- The event has a real, explicit completion state once the last cue finishes or is skipped: final elapsed time, last completed cue, calm wrap-up actions — not an undefined fall-through or an out-of-bounds console.
- Hold is a deliberate, honest schedule shift: resuming pushes all downstream cue times forward by the held duration, and the on-time/drift baseline resets to zero at the moment of resume — hold means "we chose this," and only new lateness after that gets flagged.
- Named v1 limitation: all cue times are relative offsets chained forward through the sequence, not hard clock anchors — a cue genuinely fixed to an external commitment (e.g., a band contracted 8–11) will still shift if an earlier hold pushes the chain. This is disclosed as a known limitation, not solved in v1.
- The builder is intentionally narrow (no elaborate metadata, no dependency graphs, no multi-track views) but must be fast, not tedious.

**Measurable success criteria:**
- Advance, hold, skip, and contingency-reveal/jump-to-backup are each ≤2 taps from the default running live-console state — enumerated as an actual tap path, not asserted.
- Applying a template and reaching a "ready to run" event takes under two minutes for a first-time user, timed.
- Timing state survives an actual lock-screen/background/relaunch cycle mid-cue, correct to the second, verified on real hardware.
- The live console answers, without scrolling: what's happening now, what's next, are we on time, what's the immediate recovery action.
- The app remains fully usable in airplane mode across build, run, and recovery workflows.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 3**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase well. All five required outputs are present and concrete: one-sentence promise names the actual mechanism (self-correcting from absolute timestamps, ≤2-tap recovery) rather than restating marketing copy; primary user/scenario is a specific person in a specific moment, not a persona list; core loop names the three states and explicitly forecloses hidden state or branching; hard scope boundaries are unusually precise for this stage (pointer-relocation semantics for backup jump with a worked example, contingency note as the named release valve for unplanned events, explicit event-complete terminal state, and a fully worked-through hold/resume rule); measurable success criteria are falsifiable (timed builder flow, enumerated tap paths, on-device backgrounding test, airplane-mode pass).

The round 2→3 disagreement (hold semantics) was real and got resolved with reasoning, not asserted: Codex proposed shifting downstream times, Claude extended it to also reset the drift baseline at resume and gave the product rationale (don't let the app guilt-trip the operator for a deliberate pause), and then proactively surfaced a related honest limitation (relative-offset-only timing, no hard clock anchors) rather than overclaiming universality — consistent with how the locked prompt contract already treats offline/timing/paywall honesty. Nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, product_research, or portfolio_selection; if anything it operationalizes them further (e.g., the tap-count and timed-builder criteria map directly onto decision rule 8 from prompt_contract).

Acceptance checks pass: a developer could restate this in one sentence, the scope is deliberately narrow (single device/operator/sequence, narrow builder, no branching), and out-of-scope items are explicit and specific rather than generic. No repair needed — ready to move to design phase.

CueKeeper

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination
