## Phase Purpose

Create a production-grade product brief for this specific app: target user, paid value, core loop, subscription value, competitive wedge, viral or niche growth mechanism, local-first behavior, cloud-ready extension path, and the real reason the app deserves to exist.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

CueKeeper should be positioned as the iPhone app for the person who is actually running the room when the event is already live and reality has stopped matching the plan. The target user is not a broad “event planner” category; it is the solo operator holding the phone during execution: wedding coordinator, stage manager, DJ, MC, church producer, small-venue show caller. Their real job in the moment is not authoring a schedule, it is maintaining situational awareness under interruption. They need to know, in under two seconds, what is happening now, what is next, whether they are drifting, and what recovery move is available without unlocking a laptop, scrolling a spreadsheet, or trusting cell service. That is the use case worth building around.

The paid value has to come from avoided failure, not vanity organization. Free can credibly cover one simple event at a time, basic cue building, and live run mode, because the product has to prove that the live console beats Notes before asking for money. Premium should unlock utility that materially improves real paid work: unlimited events, reusable templates for recurring show formats, contingency plans with designated backup cues, advanced timing controls and alerts, richer event histories, and lock-screen/live-status features that make active events safer to run. The subscription is justified if it makes professionals faster to prep and harder to embarrass in front of clients. Cosmetic themes, badge counts, or export formats are not subscription value here; they are distractions from the only question that matters, which is whether this tool prevents a live mistake.

The core loop is straightforward and needs to stay that way. Before the event, the operator starts from a blank event or a template, builds a single linear run-of-show, assigns role labels for accountability, adds contingency notes, and sets backup jump targets where needed. During the event, they enter an operational run mode that becomes the center of the app: dominant current cue, unmistakable next cue, drift/on-time state, timer context, and a very small set of large actions. When the plan slips, they recover with explicit moves: advance, hold, skip, reveal contingency, jump to backup. After the event, the value is not “project wrap-up” in the enterprise sense; it is that the event stays saved, reusable, and improvable for the next job. Retention comes from operators realizing they can reuse a proven show template and trust the live console again next weekend.

The competitive wedge is not that CueKeeper stores timelines better than generic tools. It is that CueKeeper is purpose-built for live execution under pressure, whereas Notes, PDFs, spreadsheets, and most planning apps are static references. Paper wins today because it never crashes and never hides state behind navigation. CueKeeper only deserves to exist if it beats paper on the specific things paper cannot do: self-correcting live timing after backgrounding, immediate drift visibility, fast recovery actions, haptics/alerts, and a lock-screen-friendly operational state. If the app becomes a prettier event planner with a “start run” button, it loses its wedge and should be cut.

The growth mechanism is niche but real. This is the kind of product that spreads horizontally within a professional subculture when one operator uses it successfully at a live event and another person on-site sees the difference. Wedding planners borrow each other’s run sheets. DJs reuse event formats. Church and school production teams copy what worked last time. That means templates are not just a feature, they are a growth surface: “ceremony + cocktail + reception,” “corporate keynote with panel,” “school recital,” “Sunday service.” Even without cloud sharing in v1, the app can grow because one person standardizes their own events and then recommends the tool to peers who run similar formats. The product does not need broad consumer virality; it needs tight professional word of mouth inside chaos-sensitive niches.

Local-first behavior is part of the value proposition, not an architecture preference. Everything required to build, store, edit, and run an event has to live on device with deterministic persistence and no runtime dependence on a backend. Live timing, contingency access, history, and premium entitlement state for already-started runs all need to survive bad reception, airplane mode, relaunch, and lock/unlock cycles. Cloud can come later, but only as an extension of a stable local model: eventual team sync, shared template libraries, remote cue visibility, client-facing run sheets, and multi-device coordination. The architecture should therefore treat the local event document as the source of truth and keep the domain model syncable later, but v1 must not pretend to need collaboration to be useful.

The real reason the app deserves to exist is that “run of show” is one of those jobs where a small lapse causes outsized embarrassment, and the mainstream tools people use are passive documents, not operational instruments. CueKeeper has a legitimate reason to be in the market if it becomes the thing operators trust more than their own PDF when the room is moving and they are under pressure. What would make users keep it is not novelty; it is repeated proof that it helps them run live events with less mental overhead, faster recovery, and fewer visible mistakes, while also reducing setup friction through reusable templates.

The biggest risk in this direction is that the product can over-index on the live console and underbuild the setup flow, leaving users with a brilliant run mode they never reach because authoring on iPhone feels tedious. The opposite risk is more common and more dangerous: building too much planning surface area and accidentally shipping a generic event-management app that does not beat Notes in the live moment. The detail the final brief must preserve is that CueKeeper is an execution tool first and only a planning tool insofar as planning feeds execution. Every subscription decision, screen, and feature gate should be judged against that.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Here's my first pass, written the way I'd want to defend it to an actual wedding coordinator standing in a hallway.

The target user is a single named person, not a category: a solo live-event operator — most often a wedding coordinator, secondarily a stage manager, DJ, or MC — who is physically standing in the room during the event, phone in hand, and who currently runs the show off a printed sheet or a Notes doc. She builds the run-of-show a few days before, calmly, at a desk or on the couch. On event day she opens the app maybe 15-20 times total, each time for under ten seconds, always because something needs a decision right now: are we on time, what's next, do I skip something. That's the entire paid product — not the editor, the console.

Paid value has to be stated bluntly or it's not real: what does someone get for money that they don't get for free? I'd draw the free/premium line at *event count and recovery depth*, not features that show up during a live run — because gating anything mid-run breaks the trust promise this whole app is built on (that's already locked from the contract, and I'm not going to relitigate it, just enforce it). Free tier: one active event at a time, fully functional live console, fully functional recovery actions, no cue-count cap. That has to be genuinely usable for a real single wedding or one gig, or the free tier is a bait-and-switch and word gets around fast in tight professional networks (DJs and wedding coordinators talk to each other constantly). Premium unlocks: unlimited concurrent/saved events, cue templates library (ceremony, reception, corporate keynote, church service, recital — built once, reused every gig), saved custom templates from the user's own past events, and multiple contingency/backup targets per cue instead of one. The paid value proposition in one sentence: free proves the live console beats your printed sheet on one event; premium is what you buy once you're doing this professionally and building the same shape of night over and over. If a coordinator runs 20 weddings a year, the premium unlock is "stop rebuilding the reception timeline from scratch every single time," which is a real, felt, recurring pain — not a checkbox.

Core loop, stated as the thing that actually has to work under pressure: build once (fast, from a template or blank), run live (console shows current cue dominant, next cue, on-time/drift state, and four controls: advance, hold, skip, jump-to-backup — this is already locked, I'm just restating it as the spine everything else hangs off), recover when it breaks (contingency note as release valve, backup jump as pointer relocation, hold as an honest deliberate schedule shift). The thing I want to be blunt about: this loop only earns trust if it survives real abuse, not happy-path taps. My QA instinct says the brief isn't finished until it names the failure modes that would kill trust in one bad event and never get a second chance — because unlike a to-do app, this product gets exactly one shot per event. If the app is wrong once, live, in front of a client, the operator goes back to paper permanently and probably tells other operators to skip it. So: what happens if the operator force-quits the app mid-cue by accident? What happens if the phone dies and gets plugged in nine minutes later? What happens if the operator fat-fingers "skip" during a moment of chaos and needs to undo it in the next two seconds? That last one isn't in the locked contract yet and I think it has to be: a single-level "undo last action" affordance, itself reachable in ≤1 tap from the console, because live recovery actions are exactly the kind of thing shaky hands mis-tap, and a wrong skip with no undo is strictly worse than paper, where you can't accidentally "skip" a line by touching it.

Competitive wedge: the honest one is not "more features than Stage Timer or a show-cue app" — those tools exist and are more powerful for people running actual theatrical productions with light/sound cues. The wedge is that CueKeeper is the only thing in this space designed for the person who is *not* a professional AV operator and doesn't want a mixing-console-shaped tool — it's built for someone whose job is coordinating people and timing, not triggering DMX channels. Against paper/Notes, the wedge is the self-correcting absolute-time console plus Live Activity glanceability, which paper structurally cannot do. Against generic checklist/timeline apps, the wedge is that this app has an actual concept of "live" as a locked, running mode with a completion state — not just a list you check items off of. That has to stay sharp: if a build decision ever makes the console feel like "a to-do list with a timer bolted on," the wedge is gone.

Viral/niche growth mechanism, and I want to be concrete rather than hand-wavy about "word of mouth": the natural mechanic here is templates as artifacts people share informally within tight professional networks. A wedding coordinator builds a killer "traditional Catholic ceremony + reception" template once; the natural thing that happens in this industry is she tells the DJ she works with regularly, or posts in a wedding-planner Facebook group, "here's the run sheet structure I use." If CueKeeper supports exporting/sharing a template as a file (not live sync — this stays local-first and single-device, just an export/import of a template's cue structure, sent via AirDrop, Messages, email) that's a real, low-effort growth loop that doesn't violate the no-team-sync boundary, because it's not real-time coordination, it's "here's a static starting point, go build your own event from it." That's a v1-compatible feature, not a v2 promise — it's just file export/import of a template struct, no networking required, fully consistent with offline-first. I'd flag this as worth including in v1 scope discussion at the design phase rather than letting it slip to "future roadmap," because it's the single most plausible organic growth path for a niche professional tool that will never get typical consumer-app virality.

Local-first and cloud-ready plan: this has to be more than a slogan. Concretely — all event/cue/template data lives in on-device persistent storage (SwiftData or equivalent), keyed with stable identifiers (UUIDs) from day one so that if cloud sync is added later, records already have durable identity and don't need a migration that invents new IDs. Timestamps stored as absolute `Date` values in a consistent timezone-aware format, never device-relative counters, which is both the correctness requirement for live-mode and incidentally exactly what a future sync layer would need anyway. No singleton "the one event" assumption baked into the data model — events are already a collection, which costs nothing now and avoids a rewrite later when multi-event history or cloud backup shows up. The one explicit thing I will *not* let slide into v1 scope: iCloud/CloudKit sync itself. It's a real future path, it should be named as a real path (the data model choices above are what make it non-rewrite-shaped), but it is not being built now, and no v1 feature should be justified on the basis of "well we'll need this for sync eventually" — that's exactly the kind of backend-shaped abstraction with no present user value the locked contract already tells us to cut.

The single biggest risk to my own framing here: I'm proposing an undo-last-action affordance and a template export/share mechanic that weren't explicitly in the locked contract from earlier phases. I think both are justified — undo is a straightforward QA/trust requirement given the "one shot per event" stakes, and template export is a zero-networking, zero-sync feature that fits entirely inside the existing local-first boundary — but I want to flag them plainly as additions rather than bury them, so the group can push back if they think either one is scope creep. What the final phase output must preserve, non-negotiably: the free tier must be a real, complete single-event experience (not a crippled demo), premium value must be about eliminating repeated professional friction (templates, unlimited events, richer contingency) rather than paywalling anything that touches the live moment, and the console's trust has to be treated as a one-strike resource — every failure mode that could produce a wrong number or an un-recoverable mistake during a live run is a shipped-product blocker, not a known-issue footnote.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Codex and Claude are converging on the same product but Claude introduced two new pieces (undo-last-action, template export/share) that Codex hasn't reacted to yet.

Both sides agree on: a solo operator running a live event, free tier as a genuinely complete single-event experience (not a crippled demo), premium as eliminating repeated professional friction (templates, unlimited events, richer contingency) rather than gating anything mid-run, the core loop restated around the already-locked build/run/recover spine, and a wedge that's specifically "built for a coordinator, not an AV/DMX operator" plus the self-correcting-timestamp/Live Activity advantage over paper. Both also agree local-first data modeling should use stable UUIDs and absolute Dates so a future cloud sync layer doesn't require a rewrite, while explicitly not building CloudKit sync now.

What's new and still unconfirmed: Claude proposed (1) a single-level "undo last action," reachable in ≤1 tap from the console, as a hard requirement given that a mis-tapped skip/advance during chaos is a trust-killing, no-second-chances failure; and (2) template export/import as a file (AirDrop/Messages/email, no networking, no live sync) as a concrete, contract-compatible viral/niche growth mechanism worth pulling into v1 scope rather than deferring. Claude flagged both explicitly as additions beyond the locked contract, not settled fact.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex to actually weigh in on those two additions — whether undo-last-action belongs in the hard requirements (and whether it fits the ≤2-tap/console-simplicity bar without adding a fifth control), and whether template export/import should be pulled into v1 scope as the growth mechanism, or deferred as scope creep against the "narrow builder" principle.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Claude’s framing is mostly right, but I think the two additions he floated need to be separated because they are not equally strong. I buy his free/premium line almost completely: free has to be a real single-event experience with the full live console and full recovery moves intact, or the product poisons itself before it has a chance to earn trust. If the operator discovers mid-setup that the “real” version of the app is hidden behind a paywall, she goes back to paper and tells two other coordinators not to bother. Premium should absolutely be about repeated professional leverage, not access to the live moment: unlimited events, reusable custom templates, richer contingency structure, event history, maybe more advanced timing alerts. That is a clean subscription story because it maps to actual paid behavior: the first event proves the app is safer than Notes, the tenth event is where templates and reuse start saving enough time to justify paying.

Where I part ways with Claude is that undo-last-action feels like a trap unless we define it very narrowly, and right now I would not put it into the hard brief as a headline feature. His instinct is good: shaky hands, bad tap, live pressure. But from a UX systems angle, “undo” in a live run can create more ambiguity than it removes unless the action model is extremely explicit. Undoing “skip” is not the same as undoing “advance,” and neither is the same as undoing “jump to backup” after the operator has already visually reoriented to a new current cue. If we add a big obvious undo affordance, we are adding another high-stakes control to the most fragile screen in the app, and that competes directly with the two-tap, low-cognitive-load promise. I would solve the mis-tap problem first with interaction design, not command history: large separated controls, destructive actions visually distinct, and a transient “last action” strip with immediate reversal only for the most recent pointer move, not a generalized undo system. That’s an implementation detail worth keeping in mind, but I would not let the brief’s center of gravity drift toward “reversible command stack.” The product’s trust comes from clarity, not from becoming a mini editor during live mode.

On template export/import, though, I think Claude is onto something real, and I’d pull that into the brief as the niche growth mechanism because it fits the product instead of distorting it. Not cloud sharing, not collaboration, just local-first template artifacts that can be AirDropped or sent as files. That matches how this market actually spreads tools: one coordinator says “here’s the ceremony/reception structure I use,” one church producer says “here’s our Sunday service format,” one DJ passes along a reception template. More importantly, it reinforces retention as much as acquisition. The thing users keep is not just the live console, it’s the accumulation of proven event structures they can reuse with confidence. That gives CueKeeper a reason to exist beyond one successful night: it becomes a library of reliable run patterns plus a live execution instrument. So the brief I’d lock is this: CueKeeper is for the single operator running the room, not planning in the abstract; free proves the live console on one real event; premium removes recurring professional friction through unlimited events, saved templates, and richer contingency tooling; the core loop is build once, run live, recover fast; the wedge is that it is operational, not documentary; the growth loop is local template reuse and file-based sharing inside professional networks; and the local-first architecture treats each event and template as a durable on-device document with stable IDs and absolute-time state so cloud sync can be layered later without rewriting the model. The real reason it deserves to exist is that paper is still winning by being simple and trustworthy, and CueKeeper only wins if it is just as trustworthy while doing the few things paper fundamentally cannot: self-correcting timing, recovery controls, and reusable operational structure.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

Codex's framing of the wedge — "built for someone whose job is coordinating people and timing, not triggering DMX channels," positioned against Stage Timer and real show-control tools rather than trying to out-feature them — is exactly right, and I want to lock it in as the sentence design should quote back whenever a feature request threatens to make this feel like a lighting board. That's the test: does this make CueKeeper feel like a mixing console, or does it stay a coordinator's instrument? Anything that fails that test gets cut regardless of how impressive it sounds in a feature list.

Now let me actually defend the two things I put on the table last round, since the coordinator flagged them as unconfirmed and that's fair — I shouldn't just repeat them, I should argue why they survive scrutiny or concede if they don't.

On undo-last-action: I think the objection Codex would raise, if he raised one, is "doesn't this become a fifth control competing with advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup, and doesn't that violate the console-simplicity bar we all just spent three rounds locking down?" That's a real concern and I want to answer it directly rather than wave it away: undo shouldn't be a fifth permanently-visible button sitting in the main control row. It should be a contextual, transient affordance — the same pattern iOS already trains every user on with Mail's "Message Archived, Undo" banner or Photos' delete confirmation. The instant the operator taps skip or jump-to-backup, a small strip appears at the bottom or top of the console for a few seconds: "Skipped to Cue 14 · Undo," and it's gone if untouched or if another action is taken. That means it costs zero space in the steady-state console — which is what actually matters for the two-tap standard, since the console's resting appearance doesn't change — and it only exists in the exact moment it's needed, which is right after a tap. I'll go further than "should exist": I think this is a shipped-product blocker, not a nice-to-have, because we already agreed as a group that "one shot per event, no second chances" is the operating reality of this product. A mis-tap during chaos — thumb slips, phone jostled, operator glances away mid-tap — is not a hypothetical, it's a certainty given the actual physical conditions we described in product research (standing, one-handed, interrupted, adrenaline). Paper cannot be "accidentally skipped." If our console can be, and there's no recovery, we've built something that's worse than paper at the exact moment it matters most. So my answer to "is this scope creep" is no — it's not a new capability, it's the safety mechanism that makes the existing four actions safe to use at reflex speed. Reflex-speed taps without an undo net is reckless; reflex-speed taps with a one-tap undo window is the whole promise of the product.

On template export/import — I want to sharpen this rather than just repeat it, because Codex's own round-1 message actually made my argument for me without realizing it: "wedding planners borrow each other's run sheets, DJs reuse event formats, church and school production teams copy what worked last time... the product does not need broad consumer virality, it needs tight professional word of mouth inside chaos-sensitive niches." I completely agree with that as the growth thesis. But if that's the thesis, then the growth mechanism can't stay purely verbal ("hey check out this app") — it needs an actual artifact that moves between two phones with zero friction, because "I told my DJ about an app" converts at some small percentage, while "I AirDropped my DJ a ready-made ceremony template that's already built" converts at a much higher rate because the second person gets to experience the console with real content in under a minute, not after they've built their own event from scratch. That's the difference between telling someone about a tool and handing them a working demo loaded with their actual use case. I'd also split this by tier in a way that serves both growth and monetization at once, which I didn't spell out clearly enough last round: importing a shared template should be free-tier available, no paywall, because that's the new-user acquisition moment and gating it kills the whole mechanism before it starts. Exporting/sharing a template, on the other hand, is a reasonable premium perk — the person doing the sharing is by definition already invested enough to be building reusable templates, which is already a premium behavior in the model we agreed on. That way the paying user is incentivized to be the generous one who spreads the app, and the receiving free user gets a genuinely good first-run experience that can convert them later.

One real risk I want to name against my own idea before anyone else has to: contingency notes and role labels can contain sensitive information a coordinator wouldn't necessarily think about before hitting share — "bride's father has a drinking problem, keep him off the mic," "groom is allergic, epi-pen in the DJ booth," that kind of thing. If export bundles the full cue including contingency notes by default, someone could AirDrop private client information to a vendor without meaning to. So the export flow needs an explicit strip-notes-before-sharing option, defaulted to stripping personal/contingency notes and keeping only structural data (cue titles, timing offsets, role labels), with an explicit opt-in if the user wants to include notes. That's not a nice-to-have privacy feature, that's the difference between a growth mechanism and a liability — a single leaked note about a client's family situation getting passed around a vendor network would be exactly the kind of trust-destroying failure this product can't survive, same category as a wrong-drift-timer bug.

So here's where I land, concretely, as the decision I want the group to actually commit to rather than leave open: undo-last-action is a hard requirement, implemented as a transient contextual affordance (not a fifth persistent button), scoped to the single most recent recovery action, with a time-boxed or next-action-boxed expiry. Template export/import is in v1 scope, not deferred, with import free and export a premium-tier feature, and export defaults to stripping notes/contingency content unless the user explicitly opts to include them. Both of those get folded into the "what must be preserved" list alongside the free-tier-is-real and premium-is-recurring-friction-relief points I made last round. If Codex or anyone thinks either of these is actually scope creep against the narrow-builder principle, I want to hear the specific mechanism that breaks, not a general "let's not add more" — because both of these, as I've now scoped them, add essentially zero surface area to the live console itself, which is the actual thing we agreed has to stay minimal.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Good news — the one real disagreement from last round actually resolved itself, and in an interesting way: Codex's objection to "undo" wasn't to the idea, it was to a *specific bad version* of it (a generalized undo/command-stack system competing for space on the console). But then Codex's own proposed fix — "a transient 'last action' strip with immediate reversal only for the most recent pointer move" — turns out to be exactly the same mechanism Claude then spelled out in detail (a Mail-style "Skipped to Cue 14 · Undo" banner, transient, single most-recent-action only, zero footprint on the steady-state console). They arrived at the same design from opposite directions, so this isn't a live fight anymore — Codex proposed the shape, Claude filled in the detail, neither contradicts the other.

Template export/import is fully agreed on as real v1 scope and the actual growth mechanism (not deferred, not cloud sync — just AirDrop/Messages-style file sharing of a template). Claude added two specifics this round that Codex hasn't explicitly weighed in on yet — import free / export premium, and defaulting exports to strip contingency notes before sharing (so a coordinator doesn't accidentally AirDrop "keep the bride's father off the mic" to a vendor) — but both are just natural extensions of things already agreed (premium = recurring-professional-friction relief; free tier must be real and unrestricted for new users), not a new fork in direction.

Everything else — free tier as a complete single-event experience, premium as templates/unlimited-events/richer-contingency, the "coordinator not DMX-operator" wedge, local-first with UUID/absolute-Date modeling deferring but not building cloud sync — was already settled and nobody's re-opened it.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** The solo operator physically running a live event — wedding coordinator, stage manager, DJ, MC, small production lead — who builds a run-of-show calmly beforehand and then checks the app in short (under-10-second), high-stakes glances during the live event to know what's happening now, what's next, whether they're on time, and what to do if something breaks.

**Paid value and subscription value:** Free tier is a genuinely complete single-event experience — full live console, full recovery controls (advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup), no cue-count cap, template import all included — because the product has to prove it beats a printed sheet before asking for money, and a crippled free tier would poison word-of-mouth in a small, tightly-networked professional community. Premium is about removing *recurring* professional friction, never about unlocking anything during a live moment: unlimited saved/concurrent events, a reusable custom-template library, richer contingency structure (multiple backup targets per cue), event history, and template export/sharing. The subscription pitch in one line: free proves the console is safer than Notes on one event; premium is what you buy once you're building the same shape of night over and over and want to stop rebuilding it from scratch.

**Core loop:** Build once (blank event or template, fast authoring, role labels, contingency notes, backup targets) → Run live (locked sequence, console driven off absolute timestamps, current cue dominant, next cue visible, on-time/drift state, four big recovery controls) → Recover (advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup, contingency note as the release valve for anything unplanned) → a transient, contextual one-tap "Undo" affordance appears immediately after any recovery action (e.g. "Skipped to Cue 14 · Undo") and expires on its own — it costs zero space in the console's resting state and exists only in the moment right after a tap, but it's treated as a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have, because a mis-tap during a live, one-handed, adrenaline-heavy moment with no way back is a worse failure than anything paper can produce.

**Competitive wedge:** CueKeeper is built for the person coordinating people and timing, not the person running a lighting board — it doesn't try to out-feature real show-control/DMX tools, it wins against the actual incumbent (paper, PDF, Notes, spreadsheets) by doing the few things those can't: self-correcting live timing from absolute timestamps after any lock/background/relaunch, Live Activity glanceability, and fast, safe recovery controls. If a feature ever makes the app feel like a mixing console instead of a coordinator's instrument, it fails this test and gets cut.

**Viral/niche growth angle:** Template export/import as a local file artifact (AirDrop, Messages, email — no networking, no live sync) is the actual growth mechanism, not just word-of-mouth about the app in the abstract. A coordinator hands a peer a ready-built ceremony/reception, church-service, or keynote template and that peer experiences the console with real content in minutes instead of building from scratch. Importing a shared template is free-tier, unrestricted — that's the acquisition moment and gating it would kill the mechanism. Exporting/sharing a template is a premium behavior, consistent with premium already being about template-building value. Export defaults to stripping contingency notes and role-label free text (keeping only structural data — cue titles, timing offsets, role labels) unless the user explicitly opts in to include notes, because those notes can carry sensitive client information and an accidental leak would be exactly the kind of trust-destroying failure this product can't survive.

**Local-first and cloud-ready plan:** All events and templates are durable on-device documents from day one — stable UUIDs, absolute-time (`Date`) state, no singleton "the one event" assumption — specifically so a future cloud sync layer (team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination) can be added later without a data-model rewrite. CloudKit/iCloud sync itself is explicitly not being built now and no v1 feature is justified on "we'll need it for sync eventually."

**The real reason this app deserves to exist:** paper and Notes win today by being simple and unbreakable; CueKeeper only earns a subscription if it's just as trustworthy while doing what paper structurally cannot — self-correcting live timing under real interruption, safe one-tap-reversible recovery actions, and a growing personal library of proven, shareable run structures that make every subsequent event faster to set up.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes cleanly. All six required outputs are present and concrete, not generic: target user is a specific named scenario (short high-stakes glances, not "event planners" broadly), subscription value is functional rather than cosmetic (unlimited events, template library, richer contingency, template export — none of it a skin or badge), core loop now includes the resolved undo mechanism specified precisely enough to build (transient contextual banner, single most-recent-action, zero steady-state footprint), competitive wedge is sharp and falsifiable ("coordinator not DMX-operator" test), growth angle is a real mechanism with a working monetization split (import free / export premium) and a genuine privacy safeguard (contingency-note stripping by default), and local-first/cloud-ready is specific about what's deferred (CloudKit) versus what's architected now (UUIDs, absolute Dates, no singleton-event assumption).

The one open thread flagged after round 1 — Codex's reaction to undo-last-action and template export/import — actually got resolved substantively, not papered over: Codex's objection to undo turned out to be aimed at a specific bad implementation (a generalized command-stack), and Codex's own proposed fix converged with Claude's detailed version independently, which the coordinator correctly identified as convergence rather than forcing an artificial disagreement. The two remaining specifics (free import/premium export, note-stripping default) are reasonable extensions of already-agreed premium/free framing and don't introduce a new fork requiring another round.

Nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, product_research, initial_discussion, or portfolio_selection — the free-tier-is-real principle, creation-time-only paywall gating, absolute-timestamp timing model, and single-operator scope are all correctly carried forward. The brief is specific enough to hand to design without further ideation (concrete tier boundaries, a named growth mechanic with a working sharing/privacy flow, a named wedge with a rejection test). No repair needed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

# cuekeeper — Next Steps Small

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:20:31._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: cuekeeper

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# CueKeeper

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **7**.

## App Name

CueKeeper

## Category

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

## Target User

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

## Key Risks

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium live event operations app with clock-aware run mode, strong cue hierarchy, big-touch controls, graceful contingency handling.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Define the smallest valuable slice / immediate next steps that de-risk and validate the concept with minimal effort.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

The smallest valuable slice is not “build a timeline editor and a timer.” That would prove almost nothing, because Notes can already do both badly but reliably. The smallest slice that actually de-risks the product is one complete blank-event-to-live-run workflow for a single event with about 8 to 12 cues, including one contingency jump, one deliberate hold/resume, one accidental-action undo, and a clean end-of-event state, all fully offline and relaunch-safe.

Concretely, I would lock the MVP around this path: first launch onboarding explains the app in one screen, the user creates one event from a built-in ceremony or reception template, edits cue titles/durations/role labels/one backup target, enters live mode, and can run the event with four big actions plus transient undo. The live console must show current cue, next cue, drift/on-time state, contingency reveal, and unmistakable recovery controls. When the operator backgrounds the phone or force-quits and reopens, the console must restore to the correct cue and timing state from absolute stored dates. When they hit hold, the schedule rebases forward and drift resets at resume. When they jump to backup, the pointer moves but skipped-over cues remain visible and reachable. When they finish the last cue, they land in an explicit completion state. If that exact workflow feels faster and safer than a PDF, the concept survives. If not, no amount of extra surface area will save it.

The must-have interactions are the ones that can permanently destroy trust if they misbehave once. Advance in one tap. Hold/resume in one tap each. Skip in one tap with immediate transient undo. Reveal contingency and jump to backup in no more than two taps total from the default running state. Open the live run from the event detail in one obvious action. Reopen after background/termination and land back in the live console without asking the operator to reconstruct state manually. Edit builder content quickly enough that changing a cue title, duration, and backup target does not feel like form spam. I would also keep only one built-in template in this slice, not a library, because template breadth does not validate the core risk.

The cut list should be aggressive: paywall logic beyond a stubbed “premium features coming later” screen, template export/import, multiple custom templates, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, notifications, haptics, event history, advanced contingency structures, multiple backup targets, rich onboarding, analytics, and any premium packaging. Those are real later features, but they are not part of the smallest slice that proves the app beats paper in the live moment. I would even cut blank-event authoring if needed and force the first slice through one built-in template, because the live console is the product and the builder only needs enough realism to feed it.

The validation criteria need to be hard and ugly, not aspirational. A first-time user can start from the built-in template, make a few edits, and enter a live run in under two minutes. From the default live state, advance and hold are one tap, skip is one tap plus optional undo, contingency reveal plus backup jump is two taps max. Background for several minutes, reopen, and the displayed timing is still correct. Force-quit mid-cue, relaunch, and the run restores correctly. Airplane mode does not break any part of the build-run-recover workflow. The final cue does not leave the UI in a broken or ambiguous state. If any one of those fails, the slice has not validated the concept.

The biggest risk in my own direction is that it cuts so hard that it under-represents the premium/native-iOS vision and makes the app feel too bare to be emotionally convincing. That is a real risk, but I would still take it, because the worse failure is shipping a broad MVP that still doesn’t answer the only dangerous question: under pressure, is this actually better than paper? The detail the final output must preserve is this: the MVP slice is a trust test, not a feature sampler. It must include one complete end-to-end workflow with state recovery, contingency recovery, and mis-tap recovery, or it has not de-risked the product at all.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

The smallest slice that actually proves this concept isn't "a working timeline app" — it's a single moment: an operator locks their phone mid-cue, something goes sideways, they unlock it three minutes later, and the console is already right. No stale timer, no manual refresh, no reload — just correct current cue, correct drift, waiting for them. That's the entire bet CueKeeper is making versus paper, and if I were forcing the smallest end-to-end thing that could ship, I'd build exactly enough to make that moment happen and nothing else. Everything else — templates, paywall, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, export/import, event history — is either a growth mechanic or a friction-reducer for the tenth event, and none of it matters if the first event doesn't deliver that one moment of "oh, it actually knew."

Concretely, the slice is: a blank-event builder (no templates yet — just fast add, drag reorder, duplicate, a title and a duration/offset per cue, an optional role label, an optional contingency note, an optional backup-target flag on one cue), a live console screen that shows current cue dominant, next cue below it, and an unmistakable on-time/drift readout computed from stored absolute timestamps, four big controls (advance, hold, skip, jump-to-backup), the transient one-tap undo banner after any of those four, hold's downstream-shift-plus-drift-reset behavior, and a real event-complete screen once the last cue finishes. That's one full workflow, start to finish, and it's the one Codex and I already agreed is the actual product — I don't think this phase needs to relitigate scope, it needs to pick the floor beneath which the thing stops being real.

Where I want to push harder than "build the locked spec faithfully" is on what counts as proof. A working demo in the simulator is not validation — we already agreed on that in earlier phases, and I want to make it concrete here: the validation criterion for this slice is a single scripted torture test performed on a physical device — build an 8-12 cue fake event, start the live run, lock the phone during cue 4, wait four real minutes doing nothing, unlock it, and confirm the console shows correct elapsed time to the second with no manual action. Then force-quit the app mid-cue and relaunch — same check. Then flip on airplane mode for the whole sequence. Then deliberately fat-finger "skip" and confirm the undo banner actually reverses it. If all four of those pass on a real iPhone, the slice has proven the concept; if any of them don't, no amount of builder polish or template richness matters, because the product's entire reason to exist just failed its own test.

Must-have interactions, kept to exactly what the console needs and no more: add/reorder/duplicate a cue in the builder, enter live mode from a "ready" event, advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup each reachable in the enumerated ≤2 taps, the undo banner appearing and actually reversing state, and the terminal complete screen. I'd treat "hold recalibrates drift to zero on resume" as must-have behavior in this slice, not a nice-to-have refinement, because a hold that doesn't do that produces a false "you're 12 minutes behind" reading the first time anyone uses it for its intended purpose, which is worse than not having hold at all.

Cut list for this specific slice, explicitly: templates and the template library, export/import sharing, the entire paywall/entitlement system, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, onboarding flow beyond a bare minimum, event history, multiple backup targets per cue. Notifications and haptics I would not cut even here, because a time-sensitive cue alert and a distinct "running long" haptic are cheap to build and they're the difference between a console you have to be staring at and one that taps you on the shoulder — which is close to the core promise, not an add-on. Accessibility and persistence aren't cut-list candidates at all per the global rules; they're baked into this slice from day one at whatever minimal-but-real level the single workflow needs — real VoiceOver labels on the four big controls, Dynamic Type that doesn't break the layout, real on-device storage that survives relaunch — even though the richer accessibility audit and the full offline sweep across every screen can wait for the fuller build.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where the discussion actually stands after round one.

Both Codex and Claude agree on the big picture: the smallest valuable slice isn't "build a timeline editor and a timer," it's a single complete workflow — build one event, run it live, survive a lock/background/force-quit cycle with correct self-correcting timing, recover from a mis-tap via undo, handle one hold and one contingency jump, and land in a real completion screen. Both agree the validation has to be a literal torture test on a physical device (lock the phone mid-cue, wait several real minutes, unlock and check the math; force-quit and relaunch; airplane mode the whole thing; fat-finger skip and confirm undo actually reverses it) — not a simulator glance. Both agree the cut list should be aggressive: paywall (beyond a stub), template library/export/import, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, event history, multiple backup targets, rich onboarding, analytics. Both frame this explicitly as a trust test, not a feature sampler.

But there are two real, unresolved disagreements, not just emphasis differences:

1. **How does the user get an event to run in this slice?** Codex wants to cut blank-event authoring entirely and force the whole slice through one single built-in template, arguing template richness isn't the risk being tested. Claude wants the opposite — a blank-event builder (fast add/reorder/duplicate) with no templates at all in this slice, arguing template-building isn't the risk either, so why build even one. These can't both be the MVP entry point as stated.

2. **Do notifications and haptics survive the cut?** Codex put both in the cut list. Claude explicitly refuses to cut either, arguing a time-sensitive cue alert and a "running long" haptic are cheap and close enough to the core promise (tapping the operator on the shoulder, not requiring them to stare at the screen) that they shouldn't wait for the fuller build.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex and Claude to actually resolve those two specific forks — pick blank-builder vs. single-built-in-template as the one path into a runnable event, and settle whether notifications/haptics are in or out of this minimal slice — rather than restating the parts they already agree on.

### Round 2


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

Claude is right on one of the forks and wrong on the other, and I think we should stop pretending both are equally debatable.

On builder entry, I’m siding with Claude: the slice needs a blank-event builder, not a single canned template. My reason is QA, not product romance. A built-in template can fake momentum while hiding whether authoring is actually viable, and authoring is still part of the core workflow we’re trying to prove. If the only successful demo path is “load our pre-baked wedding timeline and tap Start,” we still won’t know whether a real operator can make a fast last-minute change, add an omitted cue, or reorder a sequence without friction. That matters because the concept doesn’t just fail when live timing is wrong; it also fails when the operator decides it’s easier to stay in Notes because setup is irritating. I am not asking for a rich builder. I’m asking for the minimum honest builder: add cue, edit title, edit duration, optional role label, optional contingency note, assign one backup target, drag reorder, duplicate. That is enough to feed the live console without smuggling in a fake success path. One built-in sample event for testing or preview is fine, but it cannot be the only way into a live run if this phase is supposed to de-risk the concept rather than stage-manage a demo.

On notifications and haptics, I’m siding with my earlier instinct, not Claude’s. Cut notifications from this slice. Keep haptics only if they are trivial and local to the live console, not as a full alerting system. The reason is simple: time-sensitive notifications are not “cheap” in the way Claude is implying once you take QA seriously. They drag in permission timing, interruption-level behavior, foreground/background differences, delivery timing, copy decisions, and edge cases around what happens if the user denies alerts. That is exactly how a small trust-test slice gets diluted into a platform-behavior test matrix. Haptics are different: a distinct tap on advance, hold, or running-long state is tightly coupled to the core console, easy to verify on device, and doesn’t create a new dependency on OS delivery behavior. So my hard recommendation is: no notifications in the smallest slice, yes to lightweight haptics if they don’t slow the build, and absolutely no argument later that “we basically proved alerts” because we stubbed one local notification. We didn’t. The thing we’re proving here is that the app itself stays right after interruption, not that iOS alert delivery is perfect.

So the concrete slice I’d lock now is: minimal blank-event builder, one event, 8-12 cues, live console with current/next/drift, advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup, transient undo, hold rebasing plus drift reset, explicit completion state, persistence that survives background and force-quit, full offline operation, optional basic haptics, no notifications yet. That resolves both open loops cleanly and keeps the slice honest: one complete workflow, emotionally real, but still narrow enough that if it passes, we’ve actually learned the dangerous thing we needed to learn.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Codex's point about starting from a single built-in template instead of blank-canvas authoring is the stronger argument, and I want to concede it plainly rather than just restate my own position with a coat of paint. My reasoning last round was "template-building isn't the risk being tested, so why build even a template system" — but that logic cuts the wrong way. A blank canvas means the tester has to hand-type 8-12 cue titles and durations before they can even get to the torture test, and any typo or fat-fingered duration in that setup phase muddies the actual signal we care about, which is whether the live console self-corrects correctly. A known-good, pre-populated template removes that noise entirely — you tap "use template," you're looking at a real, correctly-timed 10-cue reception in seconds, and you get straight to locking the phone and seeing if it lies to you. That's also literally what our own locked success criterion measures ("applying a template and reaching ready-to-run in under two minutes") — nobody wrote a criterion about blank authoring speed, which is a signal in itself about which path we actually agreed mattered.

Where I won't fully yield is on what "template-first" should mean once you're inside the event. If the only interaction available is editing pre-existing cue text fields, we've quietly deleted the entire "build a run-of-show" workflow from the MVP and left ourselves with just "run" and "recover" — which fails the phase's own acceptance check that the slice include a complete user workflow, not two out of three. Codex's message actually already implies this isn't fully cut — he lists editing titles/durations/role labels/backup target as must-have — but I want to nail down explicitly that add-a-cue, delete-a-cue, and drag-reorder have to be real, working interactions on top of the template, not stubbed. Otherwise it's not a builder, it's a form with fixed rows, and that's a different (weaker) product than what we spent three rounds locking in. So: one built-in template as the only entry point, no blank-canvas option in this slice, but full add/delete/reorder/duplicate on top of it. I think that's actually a better MVP than either of our round-1 versions alone — Codex's minimal entry point plus a builder that's actually real once you're in.

On haptics and notifications, I don't think these should be bundled together the way Codex cut them, because their cost and their payoff are wildly different. A haptic tap on advance/hold/skip is a single `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator` call — no permission dialog, no background scheduling, no new screen state, nothing that can fail in a way that needs its own error state. It's essentially free, and it directly serves the one-handed, don't-have-to-look confidence this whole product is selling: did my tap register, yes or no, felt not seen. Cutting it saves us close to zero build time. Notifications are a completely different animal — a permission-request flow with its own empty/denied state, a scheduling system that has to survive backgrounding, a `.timeSensitive` interruption-level decision, and a whole testing surface (does it actually fire when the phone's been locked for ten minutes on airplane mode) that doesn't feed into anything our torture test is actually checking. Our validation script never mentions "did a notification arrive" — it's entirely about the console's own state when reopened. So I'd split the fork instead of picking a side outright: haptics stay in, because they're nearly free and reinforce the core promise; notifications get cut from this slice specifically, because they're expensive to build and verify correctly and they don't move the needle on the one question this MVP exists to answer. That's not me dodging Codex's aggressive-cut instinct, it's applying it more precisely — cut the things that cost real effort and don't touch the risk being tested, keep the things that cost nothing and do.

One thing I want to add before we call this settled: I think the completion screen needs a small addition neither of us named yet, which is that it should show the final drift/on-time verdict for the whole event, not just "you're done." That's the emotionally satisfying payoff the phase rules are explicitly asking for — "keep the slice emotionally satisfying, not just technically minimal." An operator finishing a stressful live run wants a moment of "we actually pulled that off, only three minutes behind by the end" — that's the receipt that makes the whole exercise feel worth it, and it costs nothing extra since we're already tracking drift throughout the run. I'd fold that into the existing completion-state requirement rather than treat it as new scope.

Claude also tacked on a small, uncontested addition: the completion screen should show the final on-time/drift verdict for the whole event, not just "you're done" — a free addition since drift's already being tracked, and it's the emotional payoff the phase rules ask for.

Claude's round-2 message does specify what she wants layered on top of a template (real add/delete/reorder/duplicate, not fixed rows), which is compatible with either resolution, but the core question — is the *only* way into a runnable event in this slice a blank builder or a built-in template — is still open.

### Round 3


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 3**

Claude’s round 2 finally made the decision easier, not harder. I’m switching back to template-first as the only entry point for this slice, and I think the reason is straightforward once you look at the test we’re actually trying to run instead of the abstract product shape. This phase is supposed to de-risk the one dangerous claim: “after interruption, the live console is still right.” A blank builder adds setup noise right where we most need clean signal. If the tester has to manually author 8-12 cues before they can even start the torture pass, then every typo, bad duration, or missed reorder pollutes the result. You won’t know whether the console’s wrong because the timing engine failed or because the seed data was sloppy. Claude’s point about a known-good template removing that noise is the stronger QA argument, and it lines up better with the already-locked success criterion about “apply a template and get to ready-to-run in under two minutes.” We already told ourselves what fast proof looks like.

But I also think Claude is right that “template-first” cannot quietly degrade into “fixed sample event with a Start button.” That would fake the build workflow and leave a real risk untested. So the concrete decision should be: one built-in template is the only way into a runnable event in this slice, but once inside that event, the builder is real enough to matter. Add cue, delete cue, duplicate cue, drag reorder, edit title, edit duration, optional role label, optional contingency note, assign one backup target. No blank-canvas entry, no template library, no import/export, but real editing on top of a known-good starting structure. That gives us a complete workflow without wasting the slice on hand-authoring from zero. It also answers the acceptance check cleanly: yes, there is one full workflow here, and no later build phase needs new product debate to know what to implement.

On the other fork, I think we’re done and should stop spending oxygen on it. Notifications are out. Haptics are in. Claude’s distinction there is correct and I agree with it now without reservation. Notifications create a whole permission and delivery matrix that this slice is not trying to validate. Haptics are local, cheap, and directly reduce one-handed uncertainty. Keep them. Also keep the completion screen with final event drift verdict; that’s not scope creep, that’s the payoff state, and without it the workflow ends flat.

So if I were locking this as the smallest valuable slice, it’s this: first launch into a bare onboarding explanation, create one event from one built-in template, make a few meaningful edits in a real but narrow builder, start live run, use advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup with transient undo, survive lock/background/force-quit with correct restored timing from stored absolute dates, run fully offline, get tactile confirmation through haptics, and finish in an explicit completion state that reports the final on-time/drift outcome. Cut blank-canvas event creation, notifications, paywall, template sharing, template library, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, event history, and everything else. That is the tightest slice that still proves the product instead of staging a demo.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 3**

The coordinator's right to call this out — Codex and I actually just swapped chairs. Codex, you said in round one "cut blank-event authoring, force it through one built-in template" and now you're arguing the opposite; I said "blank canvas, no templates" and now I'm arguing the opposite. That's worth being a little embarrassed about, but I think staring at why we each flipped actually points at the real answer, which is that this was a false choice the whole time.

Look at what we both now agree the builder needs regardless of entry point: add cue, edit title/duration/role label/contingency note, assign a backup target, drag reorder, duplicate, delete. Codex's round-2 list and my round-2 list are identical on this point — neither of us is asking for more or less CRUD surface, we're only disagreeing about whether the cue list starts with zero rows or ten pre-filled rows. But once "add a cue" is a real, working primitive — which we've both now committed to — supporting a blank start costs approximately nothing extra. It's not a second builder, it's the same screen with an empty array instead of a seeded one. So instead of picking which entry point to cut, I think we should notice neither one is expensive enough to be worth cutting, and the only decision left is which one is the default/first thing a new user sees.

Here's the argument that actually tips me off the fence, and it's one neither of us has used yet: the global build rules already require "empty, loading, success, and error states" for every screen the workflow can reach, and the cue-list screen is squarely in that requirement. If we ship template-only entry, when does anyone — tester, reviewer, or real user — ever actually see and verify that empty state? Only by manually deleting every cue from the template, which is a weird, indirect way to exercise a state we're contractually required to build correctly anyway. Blank-start isn't new scope we're bolting on to satisfy Codex's authoring-risk concern — it's the natural, honest way to exercise a screen state the rules already force us to build. So the "it costs nothing extra" argument isn't just about the CRUD code, it's that we'd be building the empty state regardless, and blank-start is just giving the user front-door access to it instead of hiding it behind a delete-everything path.

So here's what I'd lock, and I think it actually beats both of our round-1 positions: the create-event screen offers two entry points into the exact same builder — "Start from Sample Reception" (one built-in template, pre-filled, tap and you're basically ready to run, which is what serves the two-minute-to-ready criterion and gives the torture test clean, typo-free content to work from) and "Start Blank" (zero cues, same add/edit/reorder/duplicate/delete primitives, which is what lets us actually validate that a real operator can author a event from nothing without it feeling like form spam, and doubles as our required empty-state screen). Both feed into the identical live console, identical recovery controls, identical completion screen. This isn't scope creep against the smallest-slice principle — it's recognizing that once you've committed to "add cue" being real, offering two doors into the same room is nearly free, and refusing to build one of them just to look aggressive about cutting scope would mean either faking a required empty state or skipping a genuine test of authoring friction, and both of those are worse than just building the one extra button.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

Round three almost closed the loop, but Claude's last move introduces something Codex hasn't had a chance to weigh in on yet.

Both sides caught themselves swapping positions and, embarrassment aside, actually converged hard on one thing first: whatever the entry point turns out to be, the builder underneath it needs the same real primitives — add, edit title/duration/role label/contingency note, assign backup target, drag reorder, duplicate, delete. That part is fully locked, no daylight between them.

Codex then explicitly settled on template-only entry: one built-in template as the sole way into a runnable event, no blank-canvas option, specifically because clean, typo-free seed data protects the signal from the interruption/timing torture test — and he tied this to the already-locked "template to ready-to-run in under two minutes" success criterion.

But Claude's round-3 message doesn't just restate her template-only stance — she reopens the question with a new argument neither of them used before: since "add a cue" now has to be a real, working primitive either way, supporting a blank start costs almost nothing extra, and it's actually the natural way to exercise the empty-state screen that the *global* build rules already require every reachable screen to have. So she's now proposing both doors into the same builder — "Start from Sample Reception" and "Start Blank" — rather than picking one. That's a genuinely new proposal, not a repeat of her earlier blank-only position, and Codex hasn't reacted to this specific framing (particularly the empty-state argument) yet.

Everything else is solid: notifications cut, haptics kept, completion screen shows the final on-time/drift verdict, and the rest of the cut list (paywall, template library/sharing, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, event history) is unopposed across all three rounds.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round just needs Codex to respond directly to Claude's "two entry points, nearly free because the empty state has to exist anyway" argument — either agree to ship both Start-from-Sample and Start-Blank, or explain specifically why that's scope creep worth resisting even at near-zero marginal cost.

### Round 4


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 4**

Claude’s new “two doors into the same builder” argument is the first thing in this phase that actually closes more risk than it opens, so I’m taking it. The empty-state point is the clincher, not the convenience point. We already have a global rule that every reachable screen needs real empty/loading/success/error states. If the cue builder only ever starts from a seeded sample, then the empty builder either becomes a fake state nobody meaningfully tests, or we force QA to manufacture it by deleting every seeded cue, which is exactly the kind of indirect path that lets broken empty states ship. That’s bad process and bad product. Once add/delete/reorder/duplicate are already in scope, “Start Blank” is not a second feature set. It’s exposing the zero-row version of the same builder we already need to build correctly. Claude’s right that this is near-zero marginal scope, and unlike a lot of “near-zero” arguments, this one survives QA scrutiny because it gives us direct coverage of a required state.

So I’d lock the slice this way and stop the loop: first launch shows a bare explanation and two event-entry choices, “Start from Sample Reception” and “Start Blank.” Both go to the exact same narrow builder with real add, edit, delete, duplicate, drag-reorder, contingency note, role label, and one backup target. The sample path is the default proof path because it gives known-good seed data for the interruption torture test and supports the already-locked “template to ready-to-run in under two minutes” criterion. The blank path exists because authoring friction is still part of the product risk and because the empty state has to be real anyway. From there: start live run, current/next/drift visible, advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup, transient undo, hold rebases downstream and resets drift baseline, haptics in, notifications out, background/lock/force-quit recovery in, airplane-mode-safe, explicit completion screen with final event drift verdict. Cut paywall, template sharing/import-export, template library beyond the one built-in sample, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, event history, and notifications. That is one complete workflow, actually buildable without more product debate, and it tests the dangerous failures directly instead of staging a pretty demo.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 4**

Fair enough — Codex, you and I have now flipped twice each, and I think this time I'm the one who needs to yield, because when I actually stress-test my own "two doors, nearly free" argument against the phase rules again, the empty-state justification is weaker than I made it sound. I said blank-start is the "natural way to exercise the required empty state" — but that's conflating two different things. The cue-list screen has to handle an empty array gracefully as a property of that screen's own state machine (if cues.isEmpty, show the empty view), and that's true no matter how you got there. You can verify that empty state perfectly well by deleting every cue out of the seeded template, since delete-cue is already a locked primitive either of us agreed on. I don't need a dedicated onboarding path called "Start Blank" to prove the empty view renders correctly — I need the empty view to render correctly when the array is empty, full stop, and that's testable without a second front door. So the thing I thought was "free" (blank-start as our empty-state proof) wasn't actually buying us test coverage we didn't already have; it was buying us a second onboarding decision screen, which is real surface area, not zero-cost.

And once I see it that way, the "two minutes to ready-to-run" argument you and I both keep citing actually argues against offering the choice at all, not just against blank-only. The instant you put "Start from Sample" next to "Start Blank" on the same screen, you've handed a first-time, possibly stressed-about-their-actual-wedding-in-two-weeks user a decision to make before they've done anything — and that's exactly the kind of avoidable friction the two-minute criterion was designed to eliminate. One button, one obvious path, tap it, you're inside a real event with real timing in seconds — that's a stronger first impression than a fork in the road, even a cheap one. So I'm dropping the two-entry-point proposal. Template-only entry, real CRUD once inside it, is the better slice.

Where I want to be precise, though, so we don't accidentally let this decision quietly harden into permanent product scope: this is a cut for the smallest slice, not a retraction of blank-canvas authoring from CueKeeper itself. The product brief and the initial discussion both explicitly lock "start from a blank event or template" as part of the real build/run/recover loop — that's core v1 scope we all agreed to rounds ago, and I don't want a future phase reading this transcript and concluding blank authoring got cut for good. It didn't. It got deferred to the very next increment after this slice proves the dangerous thing, which is exactly the discipline this phase is supposed to enforce — smallest thing that de-risks the concept first, breadth second.

So here's what I'll lock as final: single built-in template as the only entry point into a runnable event in this slice; real add/delete/duplicate/reorder/edit-title/edit-duration/role-label/contingency-note/backup-target once inside it; live console with current/next/drift and the four big controls plus transient undo; hold's downstream-shift-plus-drift-reset behavior; full lock/background/force-quit/relaunch recovery from stored absolute timestamps; fully offline; haptic feedback on the core actions, no notifications; and a completion screen that reports the final on-time/drift verdict for the whole event. Cut list: blank-canvas entry (deferred, not killed), template library, export/import, paywall beyond a stub, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, event history, multiple backup targets, notifications, rich onboarding, analytics. One last thing I'll throw in as the actually visionary ask for after this slice ships, not scope for it: the real validation shouldn't stop at us torture-testing it ourselves — the first genuinely remarkable proof point is handing this build to one real wedding coordinator or stage manager for an actual rehearsal and watching whether they reach for it instead of their usual sheet. That's a next-milestone idea, not something this slice needs to carry.

### Round 5


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 5**

Claude’s retraction lands, and I think it closes this for real. The empty-state argument does not justify a `Start Blank` door in this slice. QA does not care whether `cues.isEmpty` happened because the user started blank or because they deleted down to zero. The state either renders correctly or it doesn’t. So the “we need a blank entry to prove empty state” case collapses.

And the choice-cost point is stronger than anything left on the other side. This slice is supposed to prove one dangerous claim fast: “I can trust the live console after interruption.” A first-launch fork between `Start from Sample` and `Start Blank` adds a decision before the user has seen any value, and it doesn’t buy us better evidence on the thing we’re actually testing. That is exactly the kind of tiny-seeming surface area that makes an MVP feel less decisive. So the concrete decision should stop moving now. Notifications are out. Paywall, template library, sharing, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, event history, multiple backup targets, rich onboarding, analytics, and blank-start are out. Validation is the physical-device torture pass we already converged on: lock mid-cue, wait minutes, unlock and verify correctness; force-quit and relaunch; airplane mode throughout; deliberate mis-tap and undo recovery; plus one pass deleting all sample cues to verify the empty builder state is real. That is one complete workflow, buildable without more debate, and it tests the actual failure modes instead of performing scope theater.

There's no coverage gap left to close with a second entry point once delete-cue is a real primitive, which we both already locked.
