## Final Output

**MVP slice:** A single complete build→run→recover workflow, entered through one built-in sample event (a pre-filled reception timeline), not a blank canvas. Blank-canvas event creation is explicitly *deferred to the next increment*, not cut from the product — it's already locked v1 scope from earlier phases and will return once this slice proves the dangerous claim.

**Must-have interactions:**
- Create an event by tapping "Start from Sample Reception," landing on a known-good, pre-timed ~8-12 cue event.
- A real builder on top of it: add cue, edit title/duration, delete cue, duplicate cue, drag-reorder, optional role label, optional contingency note, assign one backup target — not a fixed-row form.
- Enter live mode; console shows current cue (dominant), next cue, and on-time/drift state computed from stored absolute timestamps.
- Four big recovery controls — advance, hold, skip, jump-to-backup — each within the locked ≤2-tap standard.
- A transient, contextual one-tap "Undo" banner after any recovery action.
- Hold rebases all downstream cue times forward by the held duration and resets the drift baseline to zero at resume.
- Jump-to-backup relocates the live pointer only — skipped cues remain visible and reachable, never destroyed.
- State survives phone lock, backgrounding, force-quit, and relaunch, recomputed correctly from absolute `Date` values.
- Fully offline — no networking anywhere in this workflow.
- Haptic feedback on core actions (advance/hold/skip).
- An explicit event-completion screen reporting the final on-time/drift verdict for the whole run, not just "done."

**Cut list (for this slice only):** blank-canvas event entry (deferred), template library beyond the one built-in sample, template export/import, paywall beyond a stub, notifications, Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, event history, multiple backup targets per cue, rich onboarding, analytics.

**Validation criteria (physical device, not simulator):**
- Start from sample, make a few real edits (change a duration, add a cue, reorder one), reach ready-to-run in under two minutes.
- Lock the phone mid-cue, wait several real minutes, unlock, and confirm elapsed/drift time is correct to the second with no manual action.
- Force-quit the app mid-cue, relaunch, and confirm the same correctness.
- Run the full workflow in airplane mode with no breakage.
- Deliberately trigger a wrong skip and confirm the undo banner actually reverses it.
- Trigger one hold, resume it, and confirm downstream cue times shift forward and drift resets to zero at that moment.
- Trigger one jump-to-backup and confirm the skipped-over cues remain visible and reachable.
- Run the last cue to completion and confirm the completion screen shows a real final drift/on-time number.
- Delete every cue from the sample event and confirm the empty builder state renders correctly (this is the agreed substitute for a dedicated blank-start entry point).

If every one of those passes, the slice has proven the one thing this product's entire existence depends on — that it's more trustworthy than a printed sheet in the exact moment reality diverges from plan — and the later build phase can execute this without reopening product debate.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 5**

QUALITY: PASS

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: cuekeeper

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# CueKeeper

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **7**.

## App Name

CueKeeper

## Category

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

## Target User

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

## Key Risks

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium live event operations app with clock-aware run mode, strong cue hierarchy, big-touch controls, graceful contingency handling.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.

## Phase Purpose

Deeply analyze requirements, edge cases, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions, resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

The biggest thing this phase needs to lock is that CueKeeper is not a scheduler with a live skin; it is a state machine for recovering trust under interruption. That means every requirement should be judged by one brutal question: when the operator looks down after chaos, does the app make the next correct move more obvious than paper would? If not, it is either deferred or cut. The remarkable version of this app is not “pretty run-of-show builder.” It is “I can unlock my phone in a bad moment and the app is already telling me the truth.”

So I would resolve the core requirements this way. One active run at a time, always resumable, always derived from persisted absolute timestamps plus persisted operator actions, never from a free-running counter that can drift. Live mode is a locked execution surface, not an editable canvas. The only allowed run-time mutations are pointer moves and schedule-state moves: advance, hold/resume, skip, reveal contingency, jump to designated backup, undo last action, and maybe edit the contingency note text if we already said that is allowed. Everything else stays out of live mode. Builder mode owns structure; live mode owns execution. That boundary matters because once you let people restructure cues mid-run, you turn a trustworthy instrument into a tiny project-management app exactly when cognitive load is highest.

The tap-path requirement needs to be preserved in literal terms, not just as an aspiration. From the default running console state: advance is one tap; hold is one tap, resume is one tap; skip is one tap with transient undo available in one tap; reveal contingency is one tap and jump-to-backup is the second tap; viewing next cue is zero taps because it is already visible. If any essential recovery move requires navigation, sheet stacking, or hunting through a menu, the product has failed its own reason to exist. I would also explicitly require that the console state answer five questions without scroll: what is happening now, what is next, are we ahead/on time/behind, are we currently on hold, and what is the fastest recovery action available.

The edge cases that matter are not exotic, they are the normal shape of live failure. The operator starts a run and immediately backgrounds the app to answer a text. The phone locks mid-cue. The app is force-quit while a run is active. The user skips the wrong cue. The user jumps to backup and then wants to resume the original linear flow later. The user holds for six minutes, resumes, and expects downstream times to move while drift resets to zero at that resume moment. The event runs past midnight. The device clock changes because the user manually edits time or crosses DST boundaries during a long event. The last cue is skipped rather than advanced normally. The operator deletes all cues before starting and the builder must become a valid empty state, not a dead end. The backup target points to the current cue or to an invalid/deleted cue. A role label is blank. A contingency note is blank. Two cues have the same title. None of these should crash the model or create ambiguous live state.

I would convert several open questions into explicit exclusions right now. No parallel tracks. No per-role views. No auto-advance in v1 unless there is a very strong implementation reason and a hard off switch, because auto-advance that fires incorrectly is worse than no automation. No externally anchored hard-clock cues in v1; we already know the timing model is relative and shifts downstream on hold, so that limitation should be written plainly. No mid-run paywall checks, ever. No dependence on notification delivery for correctness. No assumption that Live Activity is current; it is a convenience mirror, not the source of truth. No silent Quick Start from App Intent; confirmation screen first, always.

On data and privacy, the main thing is to treat event notes as sensitive operational data even if the app never touches the network. Contingency notes can absolutely contain embarrassing, medical, family, or vendor-sensitive information. So local-first is not enough as a slogan; the app should minimize casual leakage. Shared templates should strip contingency notes and free-text role notes by default. The sample event should contain no realistic personal data. If analytics ever arrive later, they should never collect cue titles or note content. The app also needs a clear answer on local persistence failure: if a save fails, the user needs an explicit error state and a last-known-good run state, not silent optimism. Data loss is one of the few failures that can permanently kill trust in this category.

Offline implications also need to be stated more sharply than “no networking.” The app must behave identically in airplane mode for build, run, recover, reopen, and complete. Permissions cannot be prerequisites for the core workflow. If notification permission is denied, the run is still fully valid. If Live Activities are unavailable, the app is still fully valid. If haptics are unavailable, the app still works. The remarkable product here is resilient by subtraction: fewer dependencies, fewer hidden conditions, fewer ways to surprise the operator.

The risk register I would carry forward is pretty specific. The largest product risk is false confidence: the app looks operationally serious but is wrong after interruption. Mitigation is absolute-time recomputation, persisted state transitions, and physical-device verification, not simulator comfort. The second risk is interaction ambiguity in live mode: a control row that looks clean in design review but is unclear under adrenaline. Mitigation is brutally limiting actions, labeling them in human terms, and enforcing the tap-count audit. Third is mis-tap harm: skip/jump actions are powerful and dangerous. Mitigation is spatial separation, visual distinction, and the transient undo banner as a hard requirement. Fourth is builder drag: if setup feels tedious, operators never reach the live value. Mitigation is sample-first entry for the MVP slice, aggressive defaults, duplication, drag-reorder, and a narrow field set. Fifth is scope creep into team coordination by accident through “roles,” sharing, or future sync-shaped abstractions. Mitigation is explicitly modeling role as plain text only and keeping all collaboration out of v1. Sixth is privacy leakage through exported templates. Mitigation is structural export by default with notes excluded unless explicitly opted in. Seventh is platform feature overreach: Live Activities, widgets, and App Intents can consume disproportionate effort and still not improve the run loop. Mitigation is to treat them as secondary surfaces that may be cut or stubbed without apology.

The final assumptions should be written clearly enough that design and architecture stop arguing later. The operator is solo. The run is a single linear sequence with optional pointer relocation to a backup cue, not branching logic. Time is modeled as relative offsets that can be rebased by a hold. Live mode is execution-only except for the narrow recovery actions already named. The builder can be modest as long as the live console is exceptional. The sample event is the MVP entry point for the smallest slice, but blank event creation remains true v1 scope later. Premium value comes from repeated professional leverage, not unlocking live reliability. And the app wins only if the operator feels that unlocking it mid-chaos is safer than glancing at a PDF.

The biggest objection to my own direction is that it is ruthless enough to under-serve the “premium app” ambition. If you focus this hard on truthfulness and recovery, you risk shipping something that feels austere rather than delightful. I still think that is the right trade. In this category, delight comes after trust, not before it. The detail the final phase output absolutely must preserve is this: CueKeeper is only valuable if its live state remains obviously truthful after interruption, and every feature, edge case, privacy choice, and scope cut should be traceable back to protecting that trust.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Let me stress-test this from the failure-mode side, since most of the product shape is already locked and what's left is exactly the stuff that quietly breaks a "trustworthy" claim in the field.

Start with the one I think is most dangerous and least discussed so far: what does cold relaunch actually land the operator on? We've locked "force-quit mid-cue, relaunch, confirm correct state" as a validation step, but nobody has specified where in the app that relaunch lands them. If iOS kills the app for memory pressure during a live run and the operator taps the icon again, do they land back on the live console automatically, or on some app-root/event-list screen requiring them to tap back into the event and back into live mode? Every extra screen between "app icon" and "correct live state" is exactly the kind of friction paper doesn't have — a printed sheet never makes you navigate to find your place. I'd treat "cold launch during an active live event routes directly to the live console, not the event list" as a hard requirement, not an assumption, because getting this wrong quietly fails the entire invalidation criterion in the one moment it matters most.

Second, system clock integrity. The whole design rests on absolute `Date` values, which is right, but that assumption breaks if the underlying clock itself is unreliable mid-event — automatic time zone sync kicking in after a dead zone, a user who's fat-fingered their clock, or an NTP correction jumping the device clock by several minutes. If that happens mid-run, drift math could suddenly show something nonsensical — "47 minutes behind" out of nowhere, or negative elapsed time. I don't think we need to solve this fully, but I do think we need to name it: the app should treat implausible instantaneous jumps in device time (e.g., current time moved backward, or forward by more than some large threshold, between two consecutive foreground computations) as a signal to flag "time may have changed" rather than silently rendering a wildly wrong drift number as if it were confident fact. An operator trusting a false "you're 40 minutes behind" reading badly enough to panic-skip cues is a worse outcome than the app admitting uncertainty.

Third — dangling references in the builder. We've locked that backup-target assignment and jump-to-backup are core mechanics, but what happens when the cue designated as someone's backup target gets deleted or reordered during editing? Nothing in the discussion so far specifies this. My position: deleting a cue that is currently referenced as another cue's backup target should either clear that reference with a visible warning at delete-time, or block the delete with an explanation — silently leaving a phantom backup pointer that resolves to nothing (or worse, to the wrong cue after reindexing) is a live-mode failure waiting to happen, and it would happen exactly when the operator is trying to use the safety feature we spent two phases designing.

Fourth, terminal-state edge cases beyond the "last cue via advance" case we've already locked. What happens if the operator hits skip on the last cue? Hits hold on the last cue and never resumes? Jumps to a backup that happens to be the last cue? Every one of the four controls needs a defined terminal behavior, not just advance — otherwise QA will find at least one console state that's reachable but undefined, which is the exact kind of thing that reads as "broken" the first time a real operator hits it under pressure.

Fifth, accessibility interactions that are more than a checkbox. Two specific ones: the transient undo banner is a genuinely good design, but a fixed-duration auto-dismiss is a known anti-pattern for VoiceOver users, who need more time to navigate to and activate a control than a sighted user does — if the banner disappears on a timer regardless of accessibility state, VoiceOver users structurally lose the safety net the rest of us get, which is a real equity and correctness problem, not a nice-to-have. And drag-to-reorder in the builder, which we've locked as a required interaction, is not accessible to VoiceOver out of the box — SwiftUI's default `onMove` gesture needs explicit accessibility custom actions ("move up," "move down") or VoiceOver users literally cannot reorder cues at all, which would be a real accessibility failure sitting inside a workflow we've called a hard requirement.

Sixth, a concurrency rule we haven't stated: what happens if the operator (or the App Intent) tries to start a second live run while one event is already live? Single-operator/single-sequence is locked at the per-event level, but nothing says what happens across events. I'd want a rule that starting a new run while another is active either blocks with "Event X is currently live — resume it?" or forces an explicit end-current-run step first, especially since the App Intent path is required to always show a confirmation screen anyway — that's the natural place to surface this conflict rather than silently letting two events think they're both "live."

Seventh, data-at-rest for contingency notes. We already agreed these can contain sensitive client information (allergies, family conflicts) sensitive enough to justify stripping them from template exports by default. That same sensitivity means local storage shouldn't be an afterthought — I'd want the on-device store using standard iOS data protection (file encrypted while locked) rather than whatever a default SwiftData container happens to give you unconfigured, and I'd flag the app-switcher snapshot (iOS captures the visible screen when backgrounding, shown in the task switcher) as a real, if minor, leak surface for a screen showing a contingency note — worth at least naming even if we decide not to build a snapshot-blur for v1.

Eighth, paywall-and-offline interaction that could contradict the "creation-time-only, never mid-run" gating rule we've already locked: what happens if a creation-time entitlement check fires while the device has no way to verify a cached StoreKit transaction (freshly reinstalled, receipt not yet refreshed, or just genuinely offline)? If the app can't confirm premium status, it must fail open toward the free tier's real capabilities, never fail closed in a way that blocks a first event from being built — a false "you need premium" gate on a fully offline app would be an embarrassing, avoidable trust failure on day one.

Last, a smaller but real product question nobody's resolved: can a cue have zero duration — an instant marker like "cut the cake" or "rings exchanged" that's a point in time rather than a span? If yes, the builder needs validation logic and the console needs a rendering rule for a cue with no meaningful "elapsed" state; if no, we need a stated minimum duration and a validation error state in the builder for anyone who tries to enter zero. Either answer is fine, but leaving it unspecified means the design phase will improvise it under time pressure, which is how inconsistent behavior slips in.

The thing I don't want to see happen in this phase: all of the above getting hand-waved as "the design phase will figure it out." Several of these are architecture-shaping (cold-launch routing, data protection class, App Group needs if Live Activity is ever attempted) and need to be resolved as assumptions or explicit exclusions now, not discovered mid-build.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at this round: Codex laid out a strong overall framing (state machine for recovering trust, locked tap-paths, exclusions, risk register), and Claude's response doesn't contradict any of it — instead she adds nine concrete, previously-undiscussed edge cases that Codex's framing implies but never actually specified. This is additive, not yet reconciled, so the phase isn't done.

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's the honest state: Codex set the philosophical frame (every requirement judged against "is the next correct move more obvious than paper") and it's solid, unopposed, and consistent with everything locked in earlier phases — locked tap-paths, execution-only live mode, the exclusion list (no parallel tracks, no auto-advance, no hard-clock anchors, no mid-run paywall, Live Activity as mirror not source-of-truth), and a risk register naming false-confidence-after-interruption as the top risk. Nobody's disputing any of that.

But Claude's message is really nine separate, concrete gaps that this phase's own rules ("enumerate edge cases," "convert open questions into assumptions or exclusions") say need an actual answer, not just an acknowledgment:

1. Where does a cold relaunch during an active run land the operator — live console directly, or the event list? Nothing's specified this yet.
2. What happens if the device clock itself jumps (NTP correction, timezone resync) mid-run — does drift math silently show nonsense?
3. What happens when a cue currently set as someone's backup target gets deleted or reordered — does the reference get cleared with a warning, or is delete blocked?
4. What's the defined terminal behavior for hold, skip, and jump-to-backup landing on/at the last cue (we've only actually locked "advance past the last cue")?
5. Does the transient undo banner respect VoiceOver's need for more time, or does a fixed timer strand accessibility users without the safety net?
6. Is `onMove` drag-reorder actually wired for VoiceOver custom actions, since it isn't accessible by default?
7. What happens if a user tries to start a second live run while another event is already live?
8. Should contingency-note storage use explicit file data protection, and is the app-switcher snapshot of a note-bearing screen worth naming as a minor leak surface?
9. If a creation-time entitlement check can't verify purchase state (offline, fresh install), does it fail open toward free capabilities, or could it wrongly block a first event?

Plus one open product question: can a cue have zero duration (an instant marker), and if not, what's the builder's validation error for that?

None of these have been answered yet — they're just on the table. Next round needs Codex to actually take a position on each (agree/refine/reject-with-reason), so they can get folded into the phase's required resolved-requirements/edge-cases/risk-register output rather than left as unresolved QA flags.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Claude’s list is the right pressure test, and I don’t think any of it should be left for “design will sort it out,” because half of those decisions change the product contract, not just the UI. The biggest one is her first point: cold relaunch during an active run cannot dump the operator at an event list. If CueKeeper is serious about beating paper in the exact panic moment, tapping the app icon while a run is active has to route straight back to the live console for that run, with a plain “Resume Live Event” state already reconstructed. If we make them re-enter the event and then re-enter live mode, we’ve quietly admitted that the app is a document browser first and a live instrument second. Same on the “second active run” question: absolutely no silent parallel run state. If another event is already live, every start path, including App Intent confirmation, has to collapse to one blunt decision: resume the active event or explicitly end it and start this one. One live run at a time, globally, not just per event.

On clock integrity, I think Claude is also right that “absolute Date” is necessary but not sufficient. If device time jumps backward, or jumps forward by some implausible amount between foreground recalculations, the app should not render a confident drift number as if nothing happened. That’s a product trust issue, not just a math issue. I would make the rule: the console stays resumable, but it surfaces a visible warning that device time changed and timing may be unreliable until confirmed. Don’t try to get clever and auto-heal a bad clock in v1. Admit uncertainty rather than fake precision. Same philosophy on dangling backup targets: no silent corruption. If a cue that is referenced as someone else’s backup target is deleted, clear that reference immediately and tell the user exactly what changed. Blocking delete is too fussy for the builder we want; silent retargeting is dangerous; clearing with an explicit warning is the clean middle. Reordering should not matter if backup targets are stored by stable cue ID, which they should be. That decision needs to be locked now because it’s the difference between a real pointer model and an index-based footgun.

I also want to resolve the terminal-state and accessibility pieces concretely instead of admiring them. Every control needs defined last-cue behavior. Skip on the last cue ends the event and lands in completion, just like advance would. Jumping to a backup that happens to be the last cue is valid; completing or skipping it ends the run normally. Hold on the last cue is allowed, but if the operator never resumes, the event remains actively on hold, not “complete”; completion only happens through a deliberate final cue completion/skip path, not passage of time. That keeps the mental model honest. On the undo banner, Claude’s right again: a hard auto-dismiss timer that behaves the same for VoiceOver users is not acceptable because it makes the safety net conditional on vision and reaction speed. So the requirement should be functional, not implementation-shaped: the most recent recovery action must remain reversible long enough to be meaningfully accessible, and VoiceOver mode may need a different dismissal behavior than the default few-second banner. Same with reorder: drag handles alone do not satisfy the builder requirement. If reorder is core, VoiceOver needs explicit move up/down actions. Otherwise we’re pretending accessibility is in scope while cutting the exact part that makes the builder usable.

The privacy and entitlement points should also be promoted from “nice caution” to actual assumptions. Contingency notes are sensitive operational notes. That means local persistence should use standard iOS protected storage, and the app should treat app-switcher snapshots of the live console as a real leak surface worth mitigating if it’s cheap enough. I wouldn’t let snapshot-blurring become a build blocker for the MVP slice, but I would name it as a known privacy hardening task rather than ignore it. On entitlements, Claude’s rule is exactly right: if creation-time premium verification is uncertain offline, the app fails open to the free tier’s guaranteed capabilities, never fail-closed in a way that blocks someone from building or running their one free event. Otherwise “offline-first” is marketing fiction. Last thing: zero-duration cues should be allowed. Real operators absolutely have marker moments that are points, not spans: “doors open,” “kiss,” “cake cut,” “intro begins.” Forcing fake one-minute durations would make the model less truthful. The product rule should be that zero-duration cues are valid markers in the builder and render in live mode as instant cues with no meaningful progress bar, just a current/next state. That’s a cleaner product than inventing arbitrary minimums.

So I think the phase should now lock these as resolved requirements, not open questions: cold relaunch goes directly to the active live console; only one live run can exist globally; clock jumps trigger a visible uncertainty warning instead of silent bogus drift math; backup references are stable-ID based and get cleared with warning if their target is deleted; last-cue behaviors are defined for skip/hold/jump; undo and reorder must have real accessibility accommodations; protected local storage is assumed and app-switcher leakage is a named privacy concern; offline entitlement uncertainty fails open to free capabilities; and zero-duration cues are explicitly supported. That set actually sharpens the product instead of bloating it. It makes CueKeeper feel more like a trustworthy instrument and less like a nice-looking list app that happens to have timers.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Codex's framing — "every requirement judged by: does this make the next correct move more obvious than paper" — is the right filter, and I want to use it right back on my own list to make sure I'm not just being exhaustive for its own sake. A couple of my nine points fail that test if left as open questions, so let me actually commit to answers instead of just re-flagging them, since the coordinator's right that round two needs positions, not more flags.

On cold-relaunch routing — I'm not softening this one. Codex's "false confidence is the top risk" point makes my case for me: if the app relaunches to an event list and makes the operator navigate back into live mode, that's not just friction, it's a moment where the operator has to trust their own memory of where they were instead of trusting the app, which is precisely the failure mode Codex is naming as risk #1. So the concrete resolution I want locked: the app persists an "active live event" pointer independent of any single view's state, and app launch checks that pointer before rendering anything else — if a live, non-completed run exists, you land in the live console, full stop, zero navigation. This is a routing decision that belongs in this phase because it shapes the app's root view architecture, not something design can improvise later.

On the clock-jump problem, I want to go from "flag it as a risk" to an actual buildable answer, because "the app should know something's wrong" isn't testable as stated. Concretely: alongside the wall-clock `Date` timestamps we're already storing, the app also samples `ProcessInfo.processInfo.systemUptime` — a monotonic clock that keeps advancing regardless of what the user does to the wall clock — at the same moments. If, between two consecutive foreground checks, the wall-clock delta and the monotonic delta disagree by more than a few seconds, that's a real, cheap, on-device signal that something moved the calendar time out from under us. When that fires, the console shows a small, non-alarming inline note — "device time changed, verify manually" — rather than either hiding the problem or blasting a scary modal. That's a two-clock comparison, not a research project, and it directly protects against the exact panic-skip scenario I raised.

On dangling backup references, I want to tighten this into a data-modeling requirement, not just a UX one: backup targets have to be stored as a stable identifier reference to the cue, never an array index, so reordering never silently breaks anything — that part should just be correct by construction. The only real decision left is what happens on delete, and I'll commit to an answer: deleting a cue that's currently someone's backup target shows a specific confirmation ("Cue 14 is Cue 6's backup — deleting it removes that assignment. Delete anyway?") rather than either silently orphaning the reference or flatly blocking the delete forever. That keeps the builder from becoming a maze of undeletable cues while making sure the operator finds out about the broken safety net at edit time, not live, mid-event, when they tap jump-to-backup and it goes nowhere.

Terminal states — Codex named "last cue skipped instead of advanced" as an edge case but didn't resolve it, so let me actually propose the rule: any of the four controls that would move the pointer past the last cue lands in the same completion state, no matter which control got you there. The one asymmetric case worth calling out explicitly is hold: if the operator holds on the last cue, resuming from that hold should not auto-complete the event — it should return them to "last cue, running" and require an explicit advance to declare it finished. I think that's the right call because completion is a meaningful, deliberate moment for the operator (Codex, your own framing about the app answering "what's happening now" — completion is itself a state the operator should consciously arrive at, not something that happens to them because a hold timer ran out).

I'm not backing off the two accessibility points, and I'd actually push them from "should preserve" to "hard requirement, testable" language, because "we did an accessibility pass" is exactly the kind of vague claim the prompt contract already told us not to accept for timing and offline — I don't see why accessibility gets a lower bar. Concretely: check `UIAccessibility.isVoiceOverRunning`, and when it's true, the undo banner does not auto-dismiss on a wall-clock timer — it stays until the user acts or performs another recovery action, because a VoiceOver user navigating to a banner takes meaningfully longer than a sighted glance-and-tap, and a timer that doesn't know that just quietly removes their safety net. Pair that with an actual `UIAccessibility.post(.announcement, ...)` call so VoiceOver users are told the banner exists at all rather than having to discover it by swiping around. And for drag-reorder, SwiftUI's `onMove` genuinely doesn't expose itself to VoiceOver without custom accessibility actions — "Move up" / "Move down" actions on each cue row are a known, small, well-documented fix, and I want it written down now as required, not left as a maybe for whoever builds the list view.

On the concurrent-live-event question, I'll commit to a specific rule rather than leaving it as "needs a decision": exactly one event can be live app-wide at any time, not per-event. If the operator tries to start a second run — from the event list or from the App Intent — they get a blocking choice naming the currently-live event and offering resume-that-instead, never two events silently believing they're both the source of truth. Codex, this dovetails with your own "no silent Quick Start" exclusion — the App Intent confirmation screen you already want mandatory is exactly the natural surface to present that conflict, so this doesn't cost us a new screen, just a new branch on one we're already committed to building.

Data protection and the app-switcher snapshot I'll hold as real but smaller asks. I'd want us to explicitly confirm the persistence layer isn't accidentally using a weaker protection class than the sensitivity of the content warrants — this is a one-line configuration check, not new engineering, so there's no excuse to skip verifying it. The snapshot concern I'll admit is more marginal — banking apps solve it with a blur overlay on `scenePhase` transition to background, and it's cheap enough to include, but I won't die on this hill if the group wants to name it as a documented limitation instead of building it; I just don't want it to disappear from the transcript unmentioned.

Last thing, and I want to actually answer my own zero-duration question instead of leaving it open: I think the right call is to allow zero-duration cues as legitimate instant markers — "cut the cake," "rings exchanged" — because forcing someone to type a fake "1 minute" duration for a milestone that isn't a duration is exactly the kind of tedium that makes operators bail on the builder, which Codex flagged as risk #4. The builder should still hard-block negative durations as a validation error, and the console should render a zero-duration cue without a progress/over-time visual (there's no "elapsed" to show), immediately advance-eligible. That's a small, clean rule that avoids inventing fake data just to satisfy a schema constraint.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

This round actually closed it. Codex took a position on every one of Claude's nine open points instead of just gesturing at them, and Claude then tightened each of Codex's answers into something concrete and buildable rather than re-flagging anything as still-open. The only place where their exact wording differs — Codex initially framed the backup-reference-deletion fix as "delete, then clear the reference and warn," while Claude framed it as "show a confirmation dialog before the delete completes" — isn't a real disagreement, it's the same principle (never silently orphan a reference, never block deletion forever) expressed as two flavors of the same UX, and Claude's confirm-first version is the stricter, safer read of what Codex already called "the clean middle." Nothing else has any daylight between them.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements (new this phase, on top of everything already locked):**
- **Cold relaunch routing:** the app persists an "active live event" pointer independent of any single view's state. On launch, before rendering anything else, if a live/non-completed run exists, the app routes directly to that event's live console — never to an event list requiring manual re-navigation.
- **One live run globally, not per-event:** only one event can be "live" across the whole app at any time. Attempting to start a second run (from the event list or the App Intent) surfaces a blocking choice naming the currently-live event and offering to resume it instead of silently starting a second one. The App Intent's already-mandatory confirmation screen is the natural surface for this — no new screen required, just a branch on the one already planned.
- **Clock-jump detection:** alongside stored absolute `Date` timestamps, the app also samples `ProcessInfo.processInfo.systemUptime` (a monotonic clock unaffected by wall-clock changes) at the same moments. If the wall-clock delta and monotonic delta disagree by more than a few seconds between two consecutive foreground checks, the console shows a small inline "device time changed, verify manually" note rather than either hiding the discrepancy or rendering a falsely confident drift number.
- **Backup-target integrity:** backup targets are stored as a stable cue identifier, never an index, so reordering can never silently break them. Deleting a cue that's currently assigned as another cue's backup target triggers an explicit confirmation ("Cue 14 is Cue 6's backup — deleting it removes that assignment. Delete anyway?") — never a silent orphan, never a permanent delete-block.
- **Terminal states, defined for all four controls:** any control (advance/skip/hold-resume/jump-to-backup) that moves the pointer past the last cue lands in the same completion state. The one asymmetric case: holding on the last cue and never resuming does not auto-complete the event — the event stays "on hold" until the operator explicitly resumes and advances. Completion is a deliberate moment the operator arrives at, not something a timer produces for them.
- **Accessibility, treated as testable requirements, not polish:** the undo banner does not auto-dismiss on a fixed timer when `UIAccessibility.isVoiceOverRunning` is true — it persists until the user acts or takes another recovery action, and its appearance is announced via `UIAccessibility.post(.announcement:)`. Drag-reorder in the builder gets explicit "Move up"/"Move down" accessibility custom actions, since SwiftUI's `onMove` doesn't expose itself to VoiceOver by default.
- **Data protection and privacy surfaces:** the persistence layer's data-protection class is explicitly verified to match the sensitivity of contingency-note content (a one-line config check, not new engineering), given earlier phases already established these notes can carry sensitive client/medical/family information. App-switcher snapshot leakage of a note-bearing live console is named as a real but minor privacy concern — worth a cheap blur-on-background mitigation if time allows, but explicitly not a blocker for the MVP slice if it's cut; it must appear in the final report either way, not silently vanish.
- **Entitlement fail-open:** if a creation-time premium check can't verify purchase state (offline, fresh install, unrefreshed receipt), the app fails open to the free tier's guaranteed capabilities — it must never block a first event from being built or run due to an unverifiable entitlement check.
- **Zero-duration cues are valid:** instant markers ("cut the cake," "rings exchanged") are legitimate cues with no meaningful elapsed/progress state, rendered in live mode as instant, immediately-advance-eligible cues. The builder still hard-blocks negative durations as a validation error, but doesn't force a fake minimum duration onto point-in-time moments.

**Risk register additions:** false confidence after an undetected clock anomaly (mitigated by the dual-clock comparison above); accessibility as a silent trust-net failure specifically for VoiceOver users on undo/reorder (mitigated by the two concrete fixes above); accidental multi-event "live" state corrupting the single-source-of-truth promise (mitigated by the global one-live-run rule); paywall fail-closed behavior undermining the offline-first claim (mitigated by explicit fail-open).

**Final assumptions:** the operator is solo and only one event is ever "live" app-wide; all pointer references (current cue, backup target) are stable-ID based, never index based; timing correctness depends on an absolute-time model that now includes a monotonic-clock sanity check, not blind trust in wall-clock `Date` alone; accessibility for undo and reorder is a hard, testable requirement carried into the build phase, not a later polish pass; any native-feature or hardening item that gets cut under time pressure (snapshot blur, in particular) must be named explicitly in the final report rather than dropped silently.

No open ambiguity remains for the design/architecture phase to inherit — every edge case raised this round has a concrete, stated resolution.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase well. All five required outputs are present and concrete: resolved requirements directly resolve every one of Claude's nine round-1 edge cases with buildable, testable specifics (persisted active-live-event pointer for cold-relaunch routing, global one-live-run rule branching off the already-mandatory App Intent confirmation screen, a dual wall-clock/monotonic-clock comparison for clock-jump detection, stable-ID backup references with an explicit delete confirmation, defined terminal behavior for all four controls including the hold-on-last-cue asymmetric case, VoiceOver-conditional undo persistence plus announcement, explicit move-up/move-down accessibility actions for reorder, a named data-protection check plus an honestly-scoped app-switcher snapshot concern, entitlement fail-open, and zero-duration instant-marker cues). Edge cases are enumerated with resolutions, not just listed. Data/privacy implications are concrete (protected storage class check, snapshot leakage named even though its mitigation is optional). Risk register entries each carry a stated mitigation. Final assumptions are specific enough to bind design/architecture (solo/single-live-event globally, stable-ID pointers throughout, dual-clock timing model, accessibility as hard requirement, cut items must be named not dropped).

The one wording difference between Codex and Claude on backup-deletion handling is correctly characterized as two compatible expressions of the same principle rather than papered-over disagreement, and the coordinator explains why the stricter version was adopted. Nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, product_research, initial_discussion, per_app_product_brief, or next_steps_small — it builds on and tightens them (e.g., extending the already-locked App Intent confirmation into the concurrency-conflict surface, extending the already-locked absolute-Date model with the monotonic-clock sanity check). Acceptance checks pass: no major ambiguity remains for design/architecture, and every risk has either a concrete mitigation or is named as an explicit, non-blocking limitation that must surface in the final report. No repair needed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

This round actually closed it. Codex took a position on every one of Claude's nine open points instead of just gesturing at them, and Claude then tightened each of Codex's answers into something concrete and buildable rather than re-flagging anything as still-open. The only place where their exact wording differs — Codex initially framed the backup-reference-deletion fix as "delete, then clear the reference and warn," while Claude framed it as "show a confirmation dialog before the delete completes" — isn't a real disagreement, it's the same principle (never silently orphan a reference, never block deletion forever) expressed as two flavors of the same UX, and Claude's confirm-first version is the stricter, safer read of what Codex already called "the clean middle." Nothing else has any daylight between them.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements (new this phase, on top of everything already locked):**
- **Cold relaunch routing:** the app persists an "active live event" pointer independent of any single view's state. On launch, before rendering anything else, if a live/non-completed run exists, the app routes directly to that event's live console — never to an event list requiring manual re-navigation.
- **One live run globally, not per-event:** only one event can be "live" across the whole app at any time. Attempting to start a second run (from the event list or the App Intent) surfaces a blocking choice naming the currently-live event and offering to resume it instead of silently starting a second one. The App Intent's already-mandatory confirmation screen is the natural surface for this — no new screen required, just a branch on the one already planned.
- **Clock-jump detection:** alongside stored absolute `Date` timestamps, the app also samples `ProcessInfo.processInfo.systemUptime` (a monotonic clock unaffected by wall-clock changes) at the same moments. If the wall-clock delta and monotonic delta disagree by more than a few seconds between two consecutive foreground checks, the console shows a small inline "device time changed, verify manually" note rather than either hiding the discrepancy or rendering a falsely confident drift number.
- **Backup-target integrity:** backup targets are stored as a stable cue identifier, never an index, so reordering can never silently break them. Deleting a cue that's currently assigned as another cue's backup target triggers an explicit confirmation ("Cue 14 is Cue 6's backup — deleting it removes that assignment. Delete anyway?") — never a silent orphan, never a permanent delete-block.
- **Terminal states, defined for all four controls:** any control (advance/skip/hold-resume/jump-to-backup) that moves the pointer past the last cue lands in the same completion state. The one asymmetric case: holding on the last cue and never resuming does not auto-complete the event — the event stays "on hold" until the operator explicitly resumes and advances. Completion is a deliberate moment the operator arrives at, not something a timer produces for them.
- **Accessibility, treated as testable requirements, not polish:** the undo banner does not auto-dismiss on a fixed timer when `UIAccessibility.isVoiceOverRunning` is true — it persists until the user acts or takes another recovery action, and its appearance is announced via `UIAccessibility.post(.announcement:)`. Drag-reorder in the builder gets explicit "Move up"/"Move down" accessibility custom actions, since SwiftUI's `onMove` doesn't expose itself to VoiceOver by default.
- **Data protection and privacy surfaces:** the persistence layer's data-protection class is explicitly verified to match the sensitivity of contingency-note content (a one-line config check, not new engineering), given earlier phases already established these notes can carry sensitive client/medical/family information. App-switcher snapshot leakage of a note-bearing live console is named as a real but minor privacy concern — worth a cheap blur-on-background mitigation if time allows, but explicitly not a blocker for the MVP slice if it's cut; it must appear in the final report either way, not silently vanish.
- **Entitlement fail-open:** if a creation-time premium check can't verify purchase state (offline, fresh install, unrefreshed receipt), the app fails open to the free tier's guaranteed capabilities — it must never block a first event from being built or run due to an unverifiable entitlement check.
- **Zero-duration cues are valid:** instant markers ("cut the cake," "rings exchanged") are legitimate cues with no meaningful elapsed/progress state, rendered in live mode as instant, immediately-advance-eligible cues. The builder still hard-blocks negative durations as a validation error, but doesn't force a fake minimum duration onto point-in-time moments.

**Risk register additions:** false confidence after an undetected clock anomaly (mitigated by the dual-clock comparison above); accessibility as a silent trust-net failure specifically for VoiceOver users on undo/reorder (mitigated by the two concrete fixes above); accidental multi-event "live" state corrupting the single-source-of-truth promise (mitigated by the global one-live-run rule); paywall fail-closed behavior undermining the offline-first claim (mitigated by explicit fail-open).

**Final assumptions:** the operator is solo and only one event is ever "live" app-wide; all pointer references (current cue, backup target) are stable-ID based, never index based; timing correctness depends on an absolute-time model that now includes a monotonic-clock sanity check, not blind trust in wall-clock `Date` alone; accessibility for undo and reorder is a hard, testable requirement carried into the build phase, not a later polish pass; any native-feature or hardening item that gets cut under time pressure (snapshot blur, in particular) must be named explicitly in the final report rather than dropped silently.

No open ambiguity remains for the design/architecture phase to inherit — every edge case raised this round has a concrete, stated resolution.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

# cuekeeper — App Features

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:30:54._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: cuekeeper

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# CueKeeper

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **7**.

## App Name

CueKeeper

## Category

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

## Target User

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

## Key Risks

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium live event operations app with clock-aware run mode, strong cue hierarchy, big-touch controls, graceful contingency handling.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:

- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.

## Phase Purpose

Enumerate and prioritize the full feature set with an explicit scoring / MoSCoW model and clear in-scope vs out-of-scope lines.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

The feature list needs to stay brutally aligned to one test: does this make live execution faster and safer than a printed run sheet when the operator is already under pressure. A lot of plausible features sound useful in planning mode and still weaken the app, because every extra concept dilutes the live console. I would treat the run loop as the product and everything else as support.

My must-haves are the ones without which the promise breaks. The sample-event entry path is a must, because the build slice already locked that as the fastest path to proof and it keeps the experience focused. The real builder on top of that sample is a must too, but only the narrow set already justified by live execution: add cue, edit title, edit duration including zero duration, delete, duplicate, reorder, optional role label, optional contingency note, and one backup target. The live console is obviously must-have, but I want that stated as a concrete surface, not a vague screen: dominant current cue, persistent next cue, clear on-time/behind/hold state, and the four recovery actions with the locked tap-count rules. The transient undo affordance is must-have because these controls are high-risk; without reversal, the app is less forgiving than paper. Persistence and cold relaunch routing are must-have because a run-of-show app that loses place on reopen is dead on arrival. Offline operation is must-have because it is part of the product claim, not a technical preference. Accessibility accommodations for undo and reorder are must-have, because otherwise we're pretending the workflow exists for everyone when it doesn't. Haptics on core actions stay must-have in this build size because they support one-handed confidence without introducing system complexity. The explicit completion state with final drift verdict is also must-have; otherwise the experience just falls off a cliff at the end.

Each of those needs acceptance criteria tight enough that the build phase cannot blur them. The builder passes if a first-time operator can start from the sample, make a few meaningful edits, and reach ready-to-run in under two minutes without hidden menus. Live mode passes only if advance, hold, skip, and contingency reveal plus backup jump all stay within the locked tap budget from the default running state. Timing passes only if the app recomputes correctly from stored timestamps after lock, background, relaunch, and force-quit, and surfaces the clock-change warning when wall clock and monotonic time disagree. Backup behavior passes only if jumping relocates the pointer without destroying skipped cues, and deleting a referenced backup target forces an explicit warning/confirmation path. Undo passes only if the last recovery action is actually reversible and remains accessible under VoiceOver. Completion passes only if every path past the last cue lands in a defined done state except “held on the last cue and never resumed,” which must remain on hold rather than auto-finishing. Offline passes only if the full build-run-recover loop works in airplane mode. Accessibility passes only if big controls are labeled correctly, reorder works with VoiceOver custom actions, and the undo banner does not silently disappear for VoiceOver users.

My should-have list is the next layer that strengthens the product but is not required to validate the core claim in this build size. Blank-event creation belongs here now: it is real v1 product scope, but not essential to this MVP slice. Template import should be here too, because it supports acquisition and real-world usefulness, but the current slice can prove itself without it. A lightweight onboarding pass should be here, not must-have, as long as the sample entry path is already obvious. Snapshot blur on background is a should-have privacy hardening item: good to include if cheap, but not worth destabilizing the run loop. Basic premium gating scaffolding is also should-have, provided it stays strictly at creation-time boundaries and fails open.

Could-have is where I would put the native surfaces and expansion hooks that are attractive but easiest to over-invest in. Live Activities, widgets, App Intents, notifications, event history, richer template management, multiple backup targets, and contingency-note export options all fit here conceptually, but for this chosen build size most of them should actually be treated as deferred. They only become worth touching after the core console is unquestionably trustworthy.

The won’t-build list for this phase needs hard edges, not “maybe later” mush. No team sync, no multi-device coordination, no shared runs, no client-facing views, no branching show logic, no parallel tracks, no permissions model hidden inside role assignment, no auto-advance, no mid-run structural editing, no hard-clock anchored cues, no notification-dependent correctness, no mid-run paywall checks, and no feature that turns the live console into a tiny project-management app. I would also explicitly exclude any “analytics insight” surface, because it adds product noise without helping the operator in the stressful moment this app is built for.

The biggest design risk in my own direction is that it can make CueKeeper feel too narrow or even austere during calm setup. That is a real cost. A planner comparing this to a flexible spreadsheet may initially think the app is underpowered. I still think that trade is correct, because the app wins or loses in live execution, not in how many planning fields it exposes. If we stuff extra planning features into the MVP to compensate emotionally, we will almost certainly make the run mode less legible.

The detail the final output must preserve is this: prioritization is not just about implementation effort, it is about protecting the console’s mental model. Anything that introduces ambiguity about “where am I, what’s next, what happens if I tap this, and can I recover if I tapped the wrong thing” should be demoted or cut even if it sounds commercially useful. If that line gets fuzzy, the whole app becomes easier to compare to Notes, and that is exactly what it cannot afford.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Before scoring anything, I want to name the trap this phase can fall into: we've spent five prior phases converging on a very disciplined MVP slice, and app_features is exactly the moment where all the deferred stuff (blank-canvas entry, notifications, Live Activities, template library, export/import, paywall, widgets, App Intents) comes back onto the table at once and there's a temptation to wave all of it into "must" because it's already been discussed at length and feels earned. I don't think that's right. The smallest-slice phase and the detailed-discussion phase were about proving the concept and closing edge cases; this phase has to separately answer "what does production-ready v1 actually contain," and the two lists are not the same size just because a lot of words were spent on the first one.

Here's where I land on must-have, and I'm scoping it around one test: if this is missing or broken, does the app fail its own invalidation criterion (obviously better than Notes/PDF) or become dishonest about its own state. Blank-canvas event creation is a must — it was deferred only for the smallest validation slice, but prompt_contract's core workflows explicitly include "build a run-of-show" as a first-class workflow, and a production app that only ever lets you fork off one sample event isn't a run-of-show builder, it's a demo. Acceptance criteria: a user can create a zero-cue event, add cues one at a time with title/duration/role/note/backup-target, reach a valid live-ready state, with the empty-builder state rendering correctly (this is the same code path the smallest slice already validated by deletion, so there's no new risk here, just a new front door). The full builder CRUD set (add/edit/delete/duplicate/reorder, zero-duration cues, stable-ID backup targets with the delete-confirmation flow) is must, already fully specified in the prior phase, and I'd fail the build if a real device pass shows reordering ever silently breaks a backup reference.

The live console and its four controls, transient undo (with the VoiceOver-persistence carve-out), hold's rebase-and-drift-reset, jump-to-backup's pointer-relocation-not-deletion behavior, the defined terminal states for all four controls, and the completion screen with a real final drift verdict are all must, non-negotiable, already locked with concrete acceptance tests from the prior phases (I won't re-litigate them, but they need to survive into this phase's must list verbatim, not get diluted). Persistence that's relaunch-safe, the cold-relaunch-routes-to-live-console requirement, the global one-live-run rule, and the clock-jump sanity check are must — these are architecture-shaping and the kind of thing that's cheap to get right now and expensive to retrofit later. Offline verification across all three core workflows is must, and I want the acceptance criterion stated as a literal airplane-mode pass on a physical device covering build, run, and recovery, not a code review confirming no import of Network.framework.

Accessibility is must, not should, per the global rules treating it as a product requirement — and I want the acceptance criteria to be the specific, testable ones already locked: VoiceOver custom move-up/move-down actions on reorder, the undo banner not auto-dismissing under VoiceOver, Dynamic Type not breaking the console layout, and contrast actually checked against the high-contrast design bar, not eyeballed once in Xcode's preview canvas. Paywall is must at the "real, locally testable" bar prompt_contract already set — StoreKit 2 configuration file, creation-time-only gating, entitlement fail-open when unverifiable — with live App Store billing explicitly named as a limitation, not quietly implied as done. Notifications are must, and I want to be precise about why they survived the smallest-slice cut but belong in full v1: prompt_contract's hard requirements explicitly call for `.timeSensitive` cue alerts, separately from the Live Activities/widgets/App Intents bucket that decision rule 6 names as first-cut-under-pressure. That's a real distinction other phases already drew, and I don't want it flattened here — notifications are core-loop-adjacent (they're how the app taps you on the shoulder when you're not looking at it), Live Activities/widgets/App Intents are glanceable conveniences. Acceptance criteria: a scheduled cue alert fires with time-sensitive interruption level even under Focus, and if notification permission is denied, the entire live-run workflow remains fully correct and usable — permission denial can never degrade correctness, only convenience. Haptics are must, already settled, cheap, and I'd add one concrete acceptance test: verify on a physical device that advance/hold/skip each produce a distinguishable haptic pattern, not three identical taps that all feel the same under adrenaline.

Now for what I'd actually push down to should, and this is where I expect friction: Live Activities. Product research called it the single highest-leverage native feature, and I don't disagree with the value story — but value story and buildable-without-risk are different questions. ActivityKit involves an App Group, a widget extension target, push-to-update or timeline-refresh budget limits, staleness/eviction behavior we already flagged as a named technical risk, and it directly touches the exact trust question this whole product depends on (if the Live Activity shows stale state and the operator trusts it instead of opening the app, that's worse than not having one). Prompt_contract's own decision rule 6 already puts it in the first-cut bucket. I'm treating it as should: build it if the core loop is airtight and there's real time left, and if it ships, the in-app console must self-correct from absolute timestamps regardless of what the Live Activity last displayed — that self-correction requirement is a must regardless of whether Live Activities themselves ship. App Intents quick-start is should for the same reason — genuinely nice, but rule 6 already blesses cutting it, and if it's cut, that's fine as long as it's named in the final report rather than silently dropped. If it does ship, the confirmation-screen requirement and the one-live-run-conflict-surfaces-here behavior are non-negotiable, not optional add-ons to the feature. Widgets for event countdown are should-leaning-could — pre-event visibility is real value but it's the furthest thing from the live-execution promise the app exists to prove. Template library beyond the one built-in sample, and template export/import, are should — they're the premium value story and the actual growth mechanism per the product brief, genuinely important commercially, but they don't affect whether the app beats paper during a live event, which is the one invalidation test that matters most. Event history and multiple backup targets per cue are should as premium depth features, not required for a first production release to be honest and complete.

Could-bucket: snapshot-blur-on-background mitigation for the app-switcher leak (already named as optional in the prior phase), any richer contingency structuring beyond free-text-note-plus-one-backup, template metadata like tagging/categorization, in-app onboarding beyond a single explanatory screen (a full tutorial/interactive walkthrough is nice but this is an app whose entire value prop is "understand it in one glance," so an elaborate onboarding flow would almost be a bad sign about the design).

Won't-build, and I want these stated as hard lines rather than soft aspirations: team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination, any shared/live editing — all explicitly out per the original spec's future roadmap and per_app_product_brief. True branching show logic beyond jump-to-backup pointer relocation. Live App Store billing (StoreKit config testing only). True Critical Alerts (no entitlement, and I'd flag this explicitly rather than let time-sensitive notifications get casually mislabeled as critical alerts in any marketing copy or screen text — that's a factual claim about interruption behavior that would be false). Any LLM/AR/ML feature — already excluded at the spec level and I don't see a case for reopening it. Role-as-permissions or any per-role view — role stays a plain label, this was already flagged as a scope-creep risk that reintroduces multi-device coordination through the back door.

My biggest objection to my own scoring: I've pushed Live Activities and App Intents down to should even though product_research called Live Activity the single highest-leverage native feature and the original spec lists both under "iOS-native features" without ranking. Someone could reasonably argue this undersells the "premium, delightful" ambition the parent portfolio prompt demands. I'd defend it anyway — a should-bucket item that ships because there was time is a better outcome than a must-bucket item that gets rushed and ships broken, and a broken Live Activity (showing stale cue state to someone who trusts it over opening the app) is actively worse than the feature not existing. The one thing the final output has to preserve precisely: notifications are must and Live Activities/widgets/App Intents are should, and that's not an oversight, it's a deliberate reading of prompt_contract's own decision rule 6 that already drew this exact line — this phase should ratify it explicitly rather than let a generic "native features" bucket blur a distinction the project already made for itself.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**
