**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where round one actually landed. Codex and Claude agree on the shape of the must-have core: sample-event entry, the full narrow builder CRUD (add/edit/delete/duplicate/reorder, zero-duration cues, stable-ID backup targets with delete-confirmation), the live console with its four controls and locked tap-budget, transient undo (with the VoiceOver persistence carve-out), hold's rebase-and-reset behavior, jump-to-backup's pointer-relocation semantics, all four terminal states, cold-relaunch routing straight to the live console, the global one-live-run rule, the clock-jump sanity check, full offline operation, accessibility as a hard tested requirement, haptics on the three core actions, and the completion screen with a real drift verdict. The won't-build list is also solid and essentially identical between them: no team sync, no multi-device, no branching logic, no auto-advance, no mid-run structural editing or paywall checks, no role-as-permissions, no LLM/AR/ML.

But three real forks are still open, not just phrased differently:

1. **Blank-canvas event creation.** Codex has this as should-have — real v1 scope, but not essential to prove the concept in this build. Claude has it as must-have, arguing that prompt_contract lists "build a run-of-show" as a first-class core workflow, and an app that can only ever fork off one sample event is a demo, not a builder.

2. **Notifications.** Codex lumps notifications in with Live Activities/widgets/App Intents as a could-have, deferred bucket. Claude explicitly pulls notifications out of that bucket into must-have, arguing prompt_contract names `.timeSensitive` cue alerts as a hard requirement separate from the native-features-cut-first bucket (decision rule 6 only names Live Activities/widgets/App Intents, not notifications). She flagged this as a point she expects pushback on.

3. **Paywall.** Codex has "premium gating scaffolding" as should-have. Claude has it as must, citing prompt_contract's explicit bar that StoreKit 2 gating must be "real and testable via a local StoreKit configuration file," not scaffolding.

Next round needs Codex to respond directly to Claude's citations from prompt_contract on these three items — either concede the must-have promotion on textual grounds, or argue why the contract language doesn't actually force that reading — rather than each side re-stating their original bucket assignment.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Claude’s citations from the contract are the important part here, and on two of the three forks I think they win outright. Paywall has to move to must, not because I suddenly love monetization work in an MVP, but because the contract already set the bar in hard language: entitlement checks are real, creation-time-only, locally testable through StoreKit config, and never mid-run. That is not “scaffolding later if time.” Same story on notifications. I was too loose bundling them with Live Activities/widgets/App Intents. The project already separated those: rule 6 explicitly says Live Activities, widgets, and App Intents are the first things cut under pressure, but notifications and haptics were preserved elsewhere as in-scope throughout, with `.timeSensitive` specifically called out. That means notifications are not a nice native flourish, they are part of the promised operating model. The real design constraint, though, is that they must never become part of correctness. If permission is denied, if Focus still behaves oddly, if delivery is delayed, the in-app console still has to be the truth. So yes, must-have, but only as a shoulder-tap layer on top of a complete console, never as a crutch for it.

Blank-canvas creation is the one I still want to resist promoting reflexively, but I think Claude is ultimately right there too if we’re talking full v1 feature scope rather than the smallest proof slice. A production app that only clones the sample reception is not actually letting you “build a run-of-show”; it’s letting you edit our example. That’s too fake. The thing I want to guard against is letting “blank creation is must” accidentally smuggle in a big planning surface. It should be must only in the narrow sense already earned by the builder: create empty event, add cues, title/duration/role/contingency/backup, reorder, duplicate, delete, and reach a valid live-ready state. No categories, no sections, no metadata creep, no planning-suite nonsense. In other words, Claude’s reading is right on contract grounds, but only if we keep the feature definition brutally small. So my revised line is: sample-event entry and blank-event creation are both musts for v1, but the sample remains the fastest onboarding/proof path, and blank creation is just a second front door into the same constrained builder, not permission to reopen product sprawl.

So I’d tighten the whole stack this way: must-have means sample event entry, blank event creation, the full narrow builder CRUD, the live console with the locked tap-budget controls, undo, hold rebasing, backup jump semantics, persistence/cold relaunch/global one-live-run/clock-jump warning, offline verification, accessibility, haptics, notifications, completion state, and real StoreKit-gated creation boundaries with fail-open behavior. Should-have becomes Live Activities, template import/export, template library, App Intent quick start, widgets, snapshot blur, and richer onboarding. That reading is harsher than my round-one cut list, but it matches the actual contract instead of the smaller-slice instinct bleeding into the full feature phase. The key thing I don’t want us to lose is the hierarchy inside that must list: if time gets tight during build, the builder and console still get hardened first, while notifications and paywall are implemented in the most minimal honest form that satisfies the contract.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Codex, your framing that "prioritization protects the console's mental model, not just implementation effort" is genuinely the best design principle in this whole discussion, and I don't want to undercut it — but I think when I actually check it against my three promotions, none of them touch the console at all. Blank-canvas entry is a decision on the create-event screen, before you're anywhere near the live console. Notifications fire from the background and never add a button, a menu, or a state to the console UI. Paywall gating happens at creation-time boundaries, which is explicitly locked to never touch a running live session. So "don't let the console get cluttered" is a great filter for Live Activities, widgets, and App Intents — those genuinely risk adding surface area, staleness, and failure modes to the exact place where legibility matters most — but it doesn't actually argue against any of the three things I'm pushing up. I think you're applying the right instinct to the wrong targets.

On blank-canvas specifically, I want to go beyond what I said last round because I found a stronger citation than "core workflows implies it." Per_app_product_brief's locked core loop says, verbatim, "Build once (blank event or template, fast authoring, role labels, contingency notes, backup targets)" — that's not me inferring intent, that's the phrase "blank event or template" sitting in an already-consensus final output. And next_steps_small's final output is even more direct: it calls blank-canvas creation "already locked v1 scope from earlier phases" and frames its own cut as a deferral specifically scoped to that one validation slice, not a scope decision for the product. If we now demote it to should-have in app_features, we're not making a fresh prioritization call, we're quietly overturning something two prior phases already closed. That's a process problem, not just a disagreement about priority. And practically — Codex, you already conceded the CRUD set (add/edit/delete/duplicate/reorder) has to be must-have and real. Once that's true, "start with zero cues" versus "start with ten seeded cues" is a difference of one button and one default array value. There is no meaningful engineering delta here, so even under a pure effort-based should/must split, this clears the bar for must on cost grounds alone, on top of clearing it on prior-decision grounds.

On notifications, I want to push harder on the specific thing the coordinator flagged, because I don't think this is close. Look at exactly what decision rule 6 says: "Live Activities, Widgets, and App Intents are cut/stubbed first under pressure; core cue execution, timing correctness, and persistence are hardened first and non-negotiable." Three named things get the "cut first" treatment. Notifications isn't one of them — and that's not an oversight, because the same document, in its hard-requirements section, separately says cue alerts "use `.timeSensitive` interruption level so Focus/DND doesn't silently swallow them at the worst possible moment," with Critical Alerts named as the actual out-of-scope limitation, not time-sensitive notifications themselves. If the authors of prompt_contract wanted notifications lumped in with the native-feature grab-bag, they had the exact list right there and left it out. I think that's deliberate, and it tracks with something you and I both already agree on: this product's whole pitch is being right when the operator isn't staring at the screen. A time-sensitive local notification is the mechanism for that promise outside the console, and it's genuinely cheap — no push infrastructure, no server, just `UNUserNotificationCenter` scheduling against cue timestamps with rescheduling on hold/skip/jump, which we already have to compute correctly for the console anyway. Compare that to Live Activities, which needs a widget extension target, an App Group, ActivityKit lifecycle management, and has the staleness/eviction risk we already named as a real technical hazard — that's where the actual engineering risk and console-adjacent trust risk lives, not in local notifications.

Paywall I'll actually tighten rather than just re-assert, because I think there's a version of "should-have scaffolding" hiding inside my "must-have" that I should concede belongs to Codex. The contract text is specific: "StoreKit 2 gating must be real and testable via a local StoreKit configuration file... live App Store billing is out of scope." That's a narrow, bounded ask — one `.storekit` config, one entitlement check via `Transaction.currentEntitlements`, gated at the three named creation-time boundaries (new event, apply template, add contingency plan), with fail-open already locked from the detailed-discussion phase. That's must, on contract text alone, and it's genuinely a bounded afternoon of work, not an open-ended commerce build-out. But if Codex's "scaffolding" comment was really aimed at richer paywall stuff — tiered pricing screens, trial logic, win-back messaging, elaborate upsell copy — I'd agree that belongs in should or could, because none of that is in the locked contract language and it's exactly the kind of thing that can quietly balloon. So let's not fight over a word; let's fight over the actual deliverable. My must-have paywall is narrow: one config file, one real entitlement check, three gating points, fail-open, done. Anything fancier than that is should-have and I'll drop it from must without complaint.

So here's where I actually stand after hearing Codex out: blank-canvas creation and notifications move to must and I don't think there's a live disagreement left once we look at the actual contract text and the actual engineering cost — Codex, if you've got a specific line I'm missing that overrides per_app_product_brief's "blank event or template" phrasing or that puts notifications inside the same bucket as Live Activities, I want to see it, because right now I don't think the text supports demoting either one. Paywall stays must but I'm narrowing its acceptance criteria to exactly the bounded version the contract describes, which I think closes the "scaffolding" gap between us without either of us backing down on principle.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two actually closed all three open forks, and it closed clean — nobody just restated their side, they engaged with the actual contract text. Codex went through Claude's citations point by point and conceded on textual grounds: paywall moves to must because prompt_contract's language ("real and testable via a local StoreKit configuration file") is bounded and specific, not a "later if time" scaffolding ask; notifications move to must because decision rule 6 names only Live Activities/Widgets/App Intents as the cut-first bucket, and notifications were separately locked as a hard `.timeSensitive` requirement elsewhere in the contract; and blank-canvas creation moves to must because a production app that can only ever fork the one sample event isn't actually "building a run-of-show," it's editing a demo — with per_app_product_brief's literal "blank event or template" phrasing and next_steps_small's explicit "deferred, not cut" framing making this a correction of an accidental demotion, not a fresh priority call.

Where they landed together, and it matters: Codex's one condition on blank-canvas is that it stays the same narrow builder with zero starting cues — no new categories, sections, or metadata surface, just a second front door into the identical constrained CRUD set. Claude agrees and adds that this is basically free once add/edit/delete/duplicate/reorder are already must-have and real — starting from an empty array instead of a seeded one is a one-line difference, not new engineering. On paywall, Claude tightened her own ask to match what Codex was actually worried about: one `.storekit` config file, one entitlement check, three named creation-time gating points, fail-open — anything fancier (tiered pricing screens, trial logic, upsell copy) is should/could, not must. Codex's closing message already independently proposed the same bounded version, so there's no gap left between them. Notifications land the same way: must-have as a background shoulder-tap mechanism, explicitly never allowed to become part of correctness — if permission is denied or delivery is delayed, the in-app console still has to be the truth on its own.

## Final Output

**Must-have (with acceptance criteria):**

1. **Sample-event entry** — "Start from Sample Reception" lands on a known-good, pre-timed ~8-12 cue event. *Accept: tap-to-ready in under two minutes with no hidden menus.*
2. **Blank-canvas event creation** — same constrained builder, starting from zero cues, no extra metadata surface. *Accept: create an empty event, add cues one at a time, reach a valid live-ready state; empty-builder state renders correctly.*
3. **Full builder CRUD** — add/edit-title/edit-duration (incl. zero-duration instant markers)/delete/duplicate/drag-reorder, optional role label, optional contingency note, one backup target stored by stable cue ID. *Accept: reordering never breaks a backup reference; deleting a cue referenced as a backup target triggers explicit confirmation; negative durations are hard-blocked.*
4. **Live console** — dominant current cue, visible next cue, on-time/drift/hold state, four controls (advance/hold/skip/jump-to-backup) within the locked ≤2-tap budget from the default running state. *Accept: enumerated tap paths for all four controls measured and confirmed ≤2 taps.*
5. **Transient undo** — one-tap reversal of the most recent recovery action; does not auto-dismiss on a fixed timer when VoiceOver is running. *Accept: deliberate wrong-skip reversed via undo; VoiceOver banner persists until user acts.*
6. **Hold rebase + drift reset** — resuming a hold shifts all downstream cue times forward and resets the drift baseline to zero at that moment. *Accept: trigger hold, resume, confirm downstream times shift and drift reads zero immediately after.*
7. **Jump-to-backup pointer relocation** — moves the live pointer only; skipped cues remain visible and reachable. *Accept: jump to backup, confirm skipped cues still appear in the sequence and are reachable.*
8. **Terminal states + completion screen** — all four controls define last-cue behavior (hold-on-last-cue-unresolved is the one exception, staying "on hold" rather than auto-completing); completion screen reports the real final on-time/drift verdict. *Accept: each control's last-cue path checked; completion screen shows a real number, not just "done."*
9. **Persistence, cold-relaunch routing, one-live-run rule, clock-jump detection** — an active-live-event pointer routes cold launches straight to the live console; only one event can be "live" app-wide; wall-clock vs. monotonic-clock disagreement surfaces an inline warning. *Accept: force-quit mid-cue and relaunch lands directly in the live console with correct state; attempting a second live run surfaces a blocking resume-or-end choice.*
10. **Full offline operation** — build, run, and recovery workflows all work with no networking dependency. *Accept: full airplane-mode pass across all three workflows on a physical device.*
11. **Accessibility** — VoiceOver custom "Move up"/"Move down" actions on reorder, Dynamic Type that doesn't break console layout, contrast matching the high-contrast bar. *Accept: reorder via VoiceOver custom actions; layout holds at largest Dynamic Type sizes; contrast checked, not eyeballed.*
12. **Haptics** — distinguishable feedback patterns on advance/hold/skip. *Accept: physical-device check confirms the three patterns feel distinct.*
13. **Notifications** — `.timeSensitive` cue alerts scheduled against cue timestamps, rescheduled on hold/skip/jump; permission denial never degrades correctness, only convenience. *Accept: alert fires under Focus/DND; with notifications denied, the full live workflow remains fully correct and usable.*
14. **Paywall (narrow)** — one local `.storekit` config, one entitlement check via `Transaction.currentEntitlements`, gated only at the three creation-time boundaries (new event, apply template, add contingency plan), fail-open when unverifiable. *Accept: gating fires only at those three points, never mid-run; simulated offline/unverifiable entitlement state falls open to free capabilities rather than blocking.*

**Should-have:** Live Activities (must self-correct from absolute timestamps if shipped, given staleness/eviction risk), App Intents quick-start (mandatory confirmation screen + one-live-run conflict surface if shipped), widgets for event countdown, template library beyond the one sample plus import/export (import free, export premium, notes stripped by default), event history, multiple backup targets per cue, snapshot-blur-on-background, richer onboarding beyond a single explanation screen.

**Could-have:** richer contingency structuring beyond free-text-note-plus-one-backup, template metadata/tagging, an interactive tutorial/walkthrough, contingency-note export opt-in controls.

**Won't-build:** team sync, multi-device coordination, shared/live editing, client-facing run sheets, remote cue sharing; true branching show logic beyond jump-to-backup pointer relocation; parallel tracks; role-as-permissions or per-role views (role stays a plain label); auto-advance; mid-run structural editing; mid-run paywall checks; hard-clock-anchored cues (named v1 limitation); live App Store billing (StoreKit config testing only, named limitation); true Critical Alerts (no entitlement — explicitly not to be conflated with time-sensitive notifications in any UI copy); any LLM/AR/ML feature; analytics/insight surfaces.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

The three genuine forks from round 1 (blank-canvas creation, notifications, paywall) were resolved through direct textual engagement with prompt_contract, per_app_product_brief, and next_steps_small rather than restated positions — Codex explicitly conceded on citation grounds, and Claude tightened her own paywall ask to match Codex's scope concern, closing the gap from both sides rather than one side simply winning.

Internal consistency holds: the must/should split on native features (notifications must, Live Activities/widgets/App Intents should) correctly reflects prompt_contract's decision rule 6, which names only the latter three as cut-first — this phase ratifies rather than overturns that distinction. Blank-canvas promotion to must is framed correctly as a correction of an accidental demotion (next_steps_small already called it "deferred, not cut"), not a fresh scope expansion, and Codex's guardrail (same narrow builder, zero starting cues, no new metadata surface) prevents it from reopening scope creep. The paywall must-have is bounded exactly to what prompt_contract mandates (one config file, one entitlement check, three named gating points, fail-open), with anything fancier correctly pushed to should/could.

Acceptance checks pass: every must-have traces back to the product promise (beating paper during live execution, plus the "build a run-of-show" and paywall/notification hard requirements locked earlier), and the list is scoped tightly enough for a build phase to execute without further product debate. # cuekeeper — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:34:54._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: cuekeeper

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# CueKeeper

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **7**.

## App Name

CueKeeper

## Category

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

## Target User

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

## Key Risks

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium live event operations app with clock-aware run mode, strong cue hierarchy, big-touch controls, graceful contingency handling.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

The design should stay ruthlessly centered on one moment: the operator glances down after reality has drifted and needs the next correct move instantly. That means the app architecture is not “list of events with a live mode attached.” It is “one operational console, surrounded by just enough setup and recovery UI to make that console trustworthy.” If the design starts feeling like project management, it’s already losing to a printed run sheet.

I’d keep the screen set very small and very explicit. There should be a home screen that is really an event launcher, not a dashboard: if nothing is live, it shows two primary actions, `Start from Sample Reception` and `Create Blank Event`, plus the saved events list below. If an event is already live, that whole screen collapses into a dominant `Resume Live Event` card at the top and the rest of the list visually demotes. The builder screen is the second major screen: one event at a time, cue list first, not metadata first. The top area holds event title and readiness summary; the body is the cue list with large rows and obvious reorder affordances; the bottom carries add-cue and start-live actions. Each cue row expands or pushes into a cue editor screen for title, duration, optional role label, optional contingency note, and one backup target. The live console is the product: current cue dominant, next cue always visible, drift/hold state always visible, four large controls always anchored in the same positions, contingency reveal in one tap, backup jump in the second tap, and the undo banner appearing transiently without changing the base layout. Then there are a few secondary surfaces that should stay lightweight: a live-run conflict sheet when another event is active, a completion screen, a paywall sheet only at the allowed creation-time boundaries, notification permission education, and a tiny onboarding explainer before first use.

The primary flow should be dead simple. First launch goes to a short explanation screen that says what the app is for and immediately routes into the home screen. From there the user either starts from sample or creates blank. They land in the builder with a valid cue list or empty state. They add or edit cues until the event is runnable, tap `Start Live`, pass through a deliberate start confirmation, and land in the live console. From that moment the app should behave like an instrument, not an editor. Backgrounding, relaunch, or force-quit must all route straight back into that console if the run is still active. When the final cue is completed or skipped, the user lands on a completion screen with final timing outcome and actions like `Return to Event`, `Duplicate as Template` if available later, or `Done`.

State design matters more than the happy path. The home screen needs at least: first-use empty, saved-events list, live-event-present, and entitlement-blocked-at-creation. The builder needs: seeded sample loaded, blank event empty state, populated editable list, invalid state when there are zero cues and live mode cannot start, destructive-confirmation state for deleting a backup target, and error state if persistence fails. The cue editor needs: fresh cue, editing existing cue, validation error for negative duration, and backup-target picker state. The live console needs: running, on hold, clock-warning state, contingency revealed, undo available, completion transition, and recover-from-relaunch state. Notifications need both authorized and denied states without changing correctness. The paywall needs success, restore/fail-open, and dismissed states. None of these should be theoretical; every one is reachable in the MVP.

Visually, I would push this toward a high-contrast operational look that feels premium because it is decisive, not because it is decorative. Very dark graphite or near-black base, warm white typography, one assertive accent color for “active/advance,” one warning color for drift/hold, one emergency accent for skip/jump actions. Big numeric timing, condensed uppercase labels where useful, generous spacing, and strong row grouping so the operator can parse the screen in bad lighting. The current cue should feel like a control panel tile, not a list cell. The next cue should be visible without competing. Buttons should look tappable from a distance, not like subtle iOS toolbar items. Motion should be minimal and functional: cue change, hold entry, undo banner, completion arrival. No ornamental animation.

The biggest UX risk in my own direction is that the builder could feel too sparse or blunt for calm planning, especially for users who are used to flexible spreadsheets. That’s a real tradeoff. I still think it’s correct, because this app wins on execution confidence, not on authoring richness. The mitigation is not adding planning depth; it’s making the builder fast, clean, and forgiving enough that it doesn’t feel punishing. Duplicate, reorder, zero-duration markers, clear empty states, and good defaults matter more than feature breadth.

Accessibility needs to be treated as part of the interaction model, not a later pass. The live console must survive large Dynamic Type without hiding current/next/drift/control information. VoiceOver has to get explicit labels for all four live actions with their consequences, not generic button names. The undo banner must persist under VoiceOver until acted on or replaced. Reorder cannot rely on drag alone; each cue needs move up/down actions. Contrast should stay high in every state, especially warning and hold states. Haptics should reinforce action identity, not just confirm “something happened.”

The detail the final output has to preserve is this: the app is three surfaces, not ten. Launcher, builder, live console. Everything else is a sheet, confirmation, or terminal state in support of those three. If the final design inventory sprawls beyond that, we’re probably designing an app that is broader than it is trustworthy.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Let me try the sample event content, then move into the actual design contribution. I already confirmed the parent portfolio directory (Waylay/TrueScale specs) is outside this sandboxed thread's reach — that matters for the uniqueness gate, so I'll state it honestly rather than fake a comparison.

I'll come at this as the person who has to stand in a cold banquet hall at 11pm, one-handed, phone locked in a jacket pocket for twenty minutes, and needs the app to be right the instant it's unlocked. Here's the screen set I think actually earns that trust, and where I think the traps are.

Before anything else, the uniqueness gate: I tried to actually read the Waylay and TrueScale specs from the parent portfolio workspace directly off disk, and the sandbox for this thread refuses access outside the cuekeeper folder — confirmed by a blocked `find` against the portfolio root. So per the contract's own fallback, I'm not going to assert a comparison I couldn't perform. What I can say honestly from the concept descriptions alone: CueKeeper's mechanic is a single physical event, run in real time, where the entire value is a self-correcting timestamp-anchored pointer through a locked sequence — it's not a scheduler, not a checklist, not a planning surface you revisit over days. If either sibling app is itself a live real-time execution console keyed off wall-clock recovery under interruption, that would be real overlap worth naming; I have no way to check that claim from here, so I'm flagging the gap rather than papering over it, and whoever runs the portfolio-level pass should close this loop explicitly before the design locks for real.

Now the screens. Launch behaves like a router before it behaves like a screen: check the persisted active-live-event pointer first, and if a live run exists, skip straight to the Live Console — no event list, no "tap here to resume," because we already locked that as non-negotiable and I don't want it to quietly become a "usually" in implementation. If there's no live run, first-ever launch goes to a single Welcome screen — one screen, not a carousel — stating the promise in one line and offering exactly two buttons: "Start from Sample Reception" and "Create Blank Event." I feel strongly that this should not be a decision tree or a "choose your event type" wizard; it's two buttons, equal visual weight, and either one lands you in the same builder. Every subsequent launch with no live run goes to Home, a plain list of saved events (title, cue count, ready/draft/completed status, and for completed ones a one-line final drift summary so the list itself starts feeling like a paper trail of runs that went well). Home's empty state only matters if someone deletes every event they own — rare, but it needs a real "no events yet" state with the same two buttons from Welcome, not a dead end.

From Home, tapping a draft or ready event opens the Builder. This is the screen I most want to protect from feature creep — it's a cue list, reorderable, each row showing title, duration (or an explicit "instant" tag for zero-duration cues so nobody thinks a marker cue is broken), a role chip if set, and small glyphs for "has contingency note" / "has backup target" so the list itself communicates preparedness at a glance. Tapping a row opens a Cue Edit sheet: title, duration stepper, optional role text field, optional contingency note, a backup-target picker that's just a plain list of the event's other cues plus "None." Delete and duplicate live here too. The one state I want explicitly designed rather than assumed: deleting a cue that's someone else's backup target has to interrupt with a named confirmation ("Cue 14 is Cue 6's backup — deleting it removes that assignment"), and the empty builder state (zero cues) needs actual content — not a blank white screen, a clear "add your first cue" prompt with the add button already in view, since this is now the literal empty-state proof for both blank-canvas entry and the sample-deletion path.

Live Console is the one screen that has to be flawless, and I want to nail down the contingency-reveal interaction because it's the one piece of the required two-tap paths that's genuinely still undesigned. My strong opinion: contingency should not be a modal sheet. A sheet covers the current cue, and the instant you can't see "what's happening now" while deciding whether to jump to backup, you've broken the one promise this screen exists to keep. Instead, tapping the contingency control expands a panel upward from the bottom control bar, in place, with the current-cue card still visible above it — note text (or "no note added"), and a "Jump to Backup: Cue 14" button (or a disabled "no backup set" state). Reveal is tap one, jump is tap two, and the current cue never leaves the screen. Layout otherwise: current cue dominant at the top with big tabular-figure elapsed/remaining numerals, a next-cue strip immediately below it, an on-time/drift indicator that swaps to a distinct "on hold" treatment (different color and shape, not just a color swap, since color-only signaling fails colorblind users), a small inline clock-jump warning banner when the dual-clock check fires, four fixed big-touch controls along the bottom — advance, hold, skip, contingency — and the transient undo banner floating just above that bar after any action. I want advance and hold visually adjacent as the "safe" pair, and skip given a deliberately different shape or color weight so it doesn't sit in the thumb's autopilot path next to advance — a mis-tap between "advance" and "skip" is exactly the kind of thing that makes people trust paper more, not less.

One thing nobody's nailed down yet that I think belongs in this phase: when do we actually ask for notification permission? I don't want it front-loaded into the Welcome screen before the user has seen any value — that's the single biggest reason permission prompts get reflexively denied. I'd ask it contextually, the first time someone taps "Start Live Run," with copy that ties directly to the value ("Get a heads-up even when your phone's in your pocket") — and if they deny it, the console has to look and behave identically, because we already agreed correctness can never depend on notification delivery.

Completion is its own screen, not a modal on top of the console: final elapsed time against planned, the real final drift number, and — this is new — I think it should also show which cues were skipped versus completed, because "on time" without knowing what actually happened isn't trustworthy reporting, it's a vibe. Two calm CTAs: back to Home, or start a new event. The live-run conflict dialog ("Reception X is currently live — resume it, or end it and start this one?") and the paywall sheet are both interruption surfaces, not full screens, and the paywall specifically needs a visible, non-scary offline/unverifiable state that says "continuing with free access" rather than looking like an error.

On visual direction, I want to make a real call rather than leave "high-contrast operational" vague: the Live Console should render in a fixed dark theme regardless of system light/dark mode — weddings and stage cues happen in dim rooms at night far more often than in daylight offices, glare and glance-speed matter more there than platform consistency, and a fixed dark console also reads as a distinct "you are now in execution mode" cue the same way a camera app's viewfinder does. Builder, Home, and Welcome can follow the system appearance normally since those happen in calm planning conditions. Tabular figures for all time numerals so digits don't jitter and shift layout as they tick. Drift/hold/on-time states get color plus icon plus text label, never color alone.

For accessibility, beyond what's already locked (VoiceOver move-up/move-down on reorder, undo banner persisting for VoiceOver), I'd add that the console's VoiceOver reading order has to be deliberate and tested, not left to natural view order: current cue title and role, then elapsed/remaining, then drift/hold state, then next cue, then the four controls — because a VoiceOver user swiping through in the wrong order gets a worse mental model than a sighted glance would. And at maximum Dynamic Type sizes, the big current-cue numeral needs a defined fallback — let it shrink proportionally within its card and let the next-cue strip and control labels wrap or stack rather than truncate, because silently clipping "42:1" down to "42" is a correctness failure, not just a cosmetic one.

The risk I'll name against my own direction: fixing the console to always-dark is a bold, opinionated call, and someone could reasonably argue it fights "elegant" if the rest of the app is light and airy — I still think it's right because the console is the product, and instrument-panel framing sells trust better than consistency does. The one detail I don't want lost from this phase no matter how the debate goes: contingency-reveal must stay an in-place expansion that keeps the current cue visible, not a sheet — that's the actual mechanism by which we hit the two-tap requirement without ever hiding the one piece of information the operator needs most while deciding.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things stand after round one. Both Codex and Claude agree on the big-picture architecture: this is a three-surface app (launcher/home, builder, live console) with everything else — cue editor, conflict dialog, paywall, completion, onboarding — treated as a sheet or secondary screen in support of those three, not a peer feature. They also agree on the philosophy driving every choice: the console is the product, the builder should stay narrow and fast rather than richly featured, and any screen sprawl beyond the core three is a warning sign. Both landed on the same accessibility floor (VoiceOver labels tied to actual consequences, undo persisting for VoiceOver, reorder via explicit move actions, big Dynamic Type without losing current/next/drift info) and the same state-per-screen discipline (empty/seeded/populated/invalid/error for the builder; running/hold/clock-warning/contingency-revealed/undo/completion/recovered-from-relaunch for the console).

Where it's not yet resolved is that Claude threw out several concrete, specific proposals in her half of the round that Codex hasn't reacted to at all yet — and some of them are real decisions, not just detail-filling:

1. **Screen split** — Claude separates a one-time "Welcome" screen (first launch only) from an ongoing "Home" screen (saved events list), where Codex had folded those into one "home/launcher" screen with different states. Worth confirming whether that's a distinction worth keeping or just two names for the same thing.
2. **Contingency-reveal mechanics** — Claude made a specific, opinionated call that this expands in-place from the bottom control bar (keeping the current cue visible) rather than presenting as a sheet, explicitly flagging this as previously undesigned. Codex needs to weigh in since this is core to the two-tap requirement.
3. **Console-only fixed dark theme** — Claude proposed the live console always renders dark regardless of system appearance, while builder/home/welcome follow the system setting. Codex's round 1 leaned toward an overall dark operational palette without drawing that light/dark split. This is a real visual-direction decision that needs Codex's reaction.
4. **Notification permission timing** — Claude proposed asking contextually at the "Start Live" tap rather than during onboarding. Codex hasn't addressed this at all.
5. Smaller additions Codex hasn't confirmed or contested yet: the completion screen showing skipped-vs-completed cues (not just a drift number), and a specific VoiceOver reading order for the console.

Also worth noting for the record: Claude ran the uniqueness gate check as required and reported honestly that the parent portfolio workspace (Waylay/TrueScale specs) isn't reachable from this sandbox, so the comparison is being made on concept description alone rather than a real side-by-side — that's consistent with the contract's fallback instructions and doesn't block anything, but it should stay in the transcript as-is.

Next round needs Codex to respond directly to points 1–4 (agree, refine, or push back with a reason) so these become locked decisions rather than one-sided proposals.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Claude’s specific calls mostly improve the product, and I think we should stop treating them like optional styling details because they’re actually about trust. I’m with her on the contingency interaction most strongly. If reveal-contingency comes up as a sheet, we’ve failed the exact live moment we keep claiming to optimize for, because the operator has to hide “what’s happening now” in order to decide whether to escape from it. That’s backwards. The in-place expansion from the bottom control band is the right move: tap once to reveal note + backup target, tap twice to jump, current cue stays pinned on screen the whole time, and the base control geometry doesn’t shift under the thumb. That’s better than a modal not because it’s fancier, but because it preserves orientation. Same with notification permission timing: asking at `Start Live` is plainly the right moment. Before that, the user hasn’t experienced the core value and the prompt reads as a generic app tax. At live-start, the ask is concrete: “want a shoulder tap when the phone is in your pocket?” That’s legible product logic, and if they say no, nothing structural changes.

On the screen split, I think Claude’s “Welcome once, Home thereafter” is better than my fuzzier launcher/home merge, but only if we keep it brutally thin. One one-time welcome screen is fine because it’s not really a fourth product surface, it’s a disposable orientation card with the same two creation actions the Home empty state already has. The important thing is that we do not let “Welcome” become onboarding theater. No carousel, no feature tour, no permissions preflight. One screen, one sentence, two buttons, gone. After that, Home is just event access. And I also buy her completion-screen refinement: showing final drift alone is too abstract. If the app says “finished 2 minutes ahead” but hides that you skipped three cues, that’s not trustworthy reporting, it’s self-flattery. A compact completed/skipped summary belongs there because it tells the truth about how the run actually happened, which is exactly the brand of this app.

The only place I want to push back a little is the fixed-dark-console idea, not because it’s bad, but because we should admit the trade. Claude’s right that execution mode wants instrument-panel clarity, and I agree the live console should feel distinct from planning mode. I just don’t want us to accidentally create a visual mode switch that feels like a separate app. My compromise would be: yes, lock the Live Console to a dark operational theme regardless of system appearance, but keep the typography, accent system, and spacing language consistent with Builder and Home so it feels like the same product entering “run mode,” not a theme experiment. That gives us the practical win she wants: glare resistance, better night use, stronger sense that you’re now in a live instrument. So I think round two should lock this set cleanly: one-time Welcome plus ongoing Home, contextual notification prompt at live start, in-place contingency expansion not sheet, completion screen includes skipped/completed truth, and console-only forced dark mode with shared design DNA across the rest of the app. That gives us a concrete screen inventory a SwiftUI developer can actually build without making the app broader than it needs to be.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Codex, your round one actually resolves one of my open points without either of us noticing it in the moment — you listed "first-use empty" as one of the home screen's states, which is exactly the content I was calling "Welcome." I was ready to defend keeping them as two named screens, but I think you're right and I'm wrong to want a fourth surface hiding inside "three surfaces, not ten." A first-use-empty Home that shows the same two big buttons ("Start from Sample Reception" / "Create Blank Event") does everything my separate Welcome screen did, with one less screen in the inventory to maintain. I'm dropping it — Home is one screen with states (first-use-empty, populated-list, live-event-present, entitlement-blocked), not two screens with a handoff between them. That's a real concession, not a nothing-burger — it matters because every extra named screen is one more thing the build phase has to keep in sync, and you were right to fold it.

But that concession actually surfaces a real conflict I need to flag, because your "live-event-present" state for Home doesn't fully square with the cold-relaunch rule we locked two phases ago. You wrote that when an event is live, the home screen "collapses into a dominant Resume Live Event card." Except we already locked, in detailed_discussion, that a cold launch with an active run routes directly to the live console, full stop, no event list, no "tap to resume" — I'm not going to relitigate that, it's closed. So the only way your Home-with-a-resume-card state is reachable at all is if there's some in-app path back to Home while a run is still live, and neither of us has actually designed that path yet. Does the live console have a way to step back to Home without ending the run — say, a small "Events" link in the nav bar — or is it fully modal for the duration of the run, only exitable through completion? I don't think "fully modal, no escape" is right, actually, because there's a real scenario: the operator is running a wedding reception, remembers they also loaded next weekend's corporate gig into the app just to have it ready, and wants to glance at it without touching anything live. I think the answer is: yes, a deliberate, clearly-labeled back-navigation exists from the console to Home, it never ends or pauses the run, and when you land on Home that way, you get exactly the dominant Resume Live Event card you described, with the rest of the list visually demoted and functionally inert — tapping any other event triggers the same live-run-conflict dialog we already locked, rather than quietly opening its builder. That reconciles both rules: cold launch skips Home entirely and goes straight to console; deliberate in-app navigation to Home is allowed and shows your resume-card state. I think that's a better answer than either of us had alone — you had the state, I had the routing rule, and neither covered how you'd ever reach that state without violating the other.

On contingency-reveal, you didn't actually take a side this round — your line was "contingency reveal in one tap, backup jump in the second tap," which is compatible with a sheet or with my in-place expansion, it doesn't pick. But I want to point out you already made my argument for me without meaning to: you wrote that in the live console "current cue dominant, next cue always visible, drift/hold state always visible" — always visible, that's your phrase, not mine. A modal sheet for contingency breaks that promise the second it opens, because a full sheet covers the current cue by definition. If we're both signed up for "always visible," then the mechanism has to be an in-place expansion from the control bar, not a sheet, or we're contradicting our own stated design principle. I don't think this needs more debate, I think it just needs you to say the words "yes, in-place, not a sheet" so it's actually locked instead of implied.

On the dark console question, I want to push harder because I think you're eliding a real decision under a soft palette description. "Very dark graphite or near-black base" reads to me like you're describing the whole app's default look, not making the light/dark split I proposed. Here's my concrete objection to a globally-dark app: the builder is where a wedding coordinator sits at her kitchen table at 2pm the day before, or worse, is doing last-minute edits standing in bright afternoon sun outside the venue an hour before doors open. A near-black builder screen in direct sunlight is genuinely harder to read than a light one — that's a basic outdoor-legibility problem, not an aesthetic preference. Meanwhile the console's target moment is almost always dim — reception halls, dark backstage areas, evening ceremonies. So I don't think "operational dark theme" should be read as one app-wide choice; I think it's specifically a console choice, driven by when and where each screen actually gets used, and Home/Builder should just follow the system appearance like a normal iOS app. If you disagree, I want to hear the actual scenario where a globally-dark builder wins, because right now I think the "premium instrument panel" feeling you're going for is achieved just as well by making the console the one place that's always dark — that contrast between "calm app" and "you are now in execution mode" is doing real work, and it's cheaper to build than theming every screen dark and then fighting SwiftUI's default light-mode form controls in the builder.

I'll also defend the completion screen showing skipped-versus-completed cues, because I think your version — "final timing outcome and actions like Return to Event, Duplicate as Template if available later, or Done" — actually undersells what this screen needs to do. A stage manager who just wrapped a corporate keynote needs to know not just "you finished 6 minutes behind" but which specific things got skipped, because that's the conversation she's about to have with the client or the AV vendor thirty seconds later. "6 minutes behind, cues 9 and 14 skipped" is a materially more useful sentence than "6 minutes behind." That said, I want to push back on your "Duplicate as Template" action — that's should-have surface (template library isn't must-have yet), and I think it's exactly the kind of thing your own "three surfaces, not ten" line was warning against: a must-have screen quietly growing a should-have feature's affordance before that feature exists. Let's keep completion's actions to "Back to Events" and "Start New Event" for this build, and if template library ships, that's a one-button addition later, not something we design around now.

Last thing, small but I don't want it dropped: you said VoiceOver needs explicit labels tied to real consequences instead of generic button names, which I fully agree with, but that's a different requirement from the reading order I raised — label quality and traversal order are both real and neither substitutes for the other. A VoiceOver user could have perfectly worded labels and still get a worse mental model than a sighted user if swiping order puts "next cue" before "what's happening right now," so I want both locked: consequence-specific labels on every control, and a deliberate reading order of current cue → elapsed/remaining → drift/hold state → next cue → controls. And on notification timing, you filed it under "secondary surfaces" without committing to when it fires — I'll restate my position since it's unanswered: ask at first "Start Live Run" tap, tied to value, not during onboarding before anyone's built anything. If you've got a reason to ask earlier I want to hear it, but I don't think "get it out of the way at launch" beats "ask when the value is obvious," especially for a category where a denied permission can't be allowed to matter anyway.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: NO

Real progress this round — most of what was open going into round two is now actually settled. Codex agreed cleanly on three of Claude's four proposals: notification permission asks contextually at "Start Live," not during onboarding; the completion screen shows skipped-vs-completed cues, not just a drift number; and contingency reveals in-place from the control bar rather than as a sheet, because a sheet covering the current cue contradicts the "always visible" promise both of them already believe in. On the Welcome-vs-Home split, Codex pointed out his own round-one "first-use empty" state already covers what Claude wanted a separate Welcome screen for, and Claude conceded — it's one Home screen with states, not two screens with a handoff. On the dark-theme question, they landed in the same place from different directions: the Live Console is always dark regardless of system setting, while Home and Builder follow the system's light/dark like a normal app, with shared typography/accent/spacing so it reads as one product entering "run mode," not a different app.

But Claude's own concession opened a real crack that hasn't been closed yet: if Home has a "live-event-present" state showing a dominant Resume card, how do you ever reach Home while a run is live, given the group already locked (two phases ago) that a cold relaunch during an active run skips Home entirely and drops straight into the console? Claude proposed an answer — a deliberate, explicit back-navigation link from console to Home that never pauses or ends the run, landing on the resume-card state, with any other event tap triggering the existing live-run-conflict dialog — but Codex hasn't weighed in on whether that's the right fix or whether the console should just stay fully modal for the run's duration instead.

Two smaller loose ends also need Codex's explicit word next round: he described contingency as "one tap reveal, one tap jump" without ever actually saying the words "in-place, not a sheet" (Claude inferred agreement from his own "always visible" language, but it'd be good to have him confirm it outright), and Claude pushed back on his "Duplicate as Template" completion action as should-have-feature creep on a must-have screen, wanting completion trimmed to just "Back to Events" / "Start New Event" — Codex hasn't responded to that specific cut yet.

Next round: Codex needs to (1) take a position on the console-escape-hatch-to-Home design, (2) explicitly confirm the in-place contingency mechanism, and (3) agree or push back on dropping "Duplicate as Template" from the completion screen.

### Round 3


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 3**

Claude’s reconciliation on the routing issue is the right one, and I’d lock it exactly because it resolves a contradiction we had accidentally created. Cold launch with an active run still goes straight to the live console, no exception. But the console is not a prison. There should be a deliberate `Events` escape hatch in the nav bar that takes you back to Home without pausing or ending the run, and Home in that state shows the dominant `Resume Live Event` card I described, with every other event visually demoted and functionally guarded by the existing conflict dialog. That gives us both things we need: zero-friction recovery when the app is reopened under pressure, and a sane in-app way to look elsewhere without silently spawning parallel truth. If we don’t add that explicit back path, the “Home with a resume card” state becomes basically theoretical, which is bad design because it means we invented a state without a user story.

On contingency: yes, explicitly, in-place expansion and not a sheet. Claude is right to force the words. A sheet breaks the contract because it hides the current cue at the exact moment the operator is deciding whether to abandon it. The right mechanic is a bottom-origin panel that expands above the control bar, keeps the current cue card and timing visible, and reveals only the note plus the backup jump action. Tap once to reveal, tap twice to jump, no layout thrash, no modal context switch. That is one of those details that sounds small in discussion and is actually the whole product in the moment of stress.

I also agree with Claude on cutting `Duplicate as Template` from the completion screen for now. That was me letting future monetization surface leak into a must-have state. Completion needs to stay calm and honest: final drift, completed vs skipped truth, and two actions only, `Back to Events` and `Start New Event`. That’s enough. If template library ships later, we can add the affordance then instead of designing around a should-have feature that doesn’t exist yet.

So from my side I think the design phase can stop moving on these points. Home is one screen with states, not Welcome plus Home. Live console is always-dark run mode; Home and Builder follow system appearance. Notification permission is asked at first `Start Live`, not before value is demonstrated. Contingency reveal is in-place, not a sheet. Completion is truthful but restrained. And the three-surface rule still holds: Home, Builder, Live Console. Everything else is support, not a peer. That’s small enough to build and specific enough that a SwiftUI developer won’t have to invent product decisions while wiring navigation.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 3**

Codex, before I get to the two things you still owe an answer on, I have to point out something almost comic that happened in round two: I spent my message conceding that Welcome should fold into Home's first-use-empty state, and in the same round you spent your message saying my original "Welcome once, Home thereafter" was better than your fuzzier merge. We walked past each other in opposite directions. So let's actually resolve this instead of let the coordinator's summary quietly pick a side for us.

Here's what I think the real answer is, and it's not fully either of our positions: I don't think "one-time Welcome screen" and "Home's empty state" are actually the same content, even though they look identical in a wireframe. Think about who's looking at each one. True first launch is someone who has never seen this app do anything — they deserve one sentence of orientation, something like "build your run-of-show, then run it live with a console that never loses your place," plus the two buttons. But picture the other case we already agreed is real: someone who's used this app for three weddings, deletes every cue in an old event, or deletes every event entirely because they're doing spring cleaning, and now Home shows zero events. If that screen shows them the same "welcome to CueKeeper, here's what this app does" pitch copy, that's actually a little insulting — it reads like the app forgot they're a returning user. So my actual proposal: it's the same layout, same two buttons, same screen in the inventory — but two different text states. First-ever-launch gets the pitch sentence. Any later occurrence of zero events gets plain utility copy, something like "No events yet." Same screen count either of us wanted (this is still Home, still "three surfaces"), but it respects your instinct that a first-timer deserves framing and my instinct that we shouldn't be maintaining a whole separate screen for it. I think that's a strictly better answer than either of our round-two positions and I'd like you to just tell me if you see a hole in it, because I don't think there's a real disagreement left here, just an unmerged detail.

On the dark console, I want to lock your compromise outright — "console-only forced dark, but shared typography, accent system, and spacing language with the rest of the app" is exactly right and better than what I originally proposed, because I was starting to worry my own idea might read as two apps stitched together at the console boundary. The concrete way I'd carry that through: same type scale, same corner radii, same accent color for "advance/on-time" across every screen, same 8pt spacing rhythm — the console just inverts the canvas to near-black and pushes contrast higher, the way a photo app's editing view goes dark without becoming a different product. That gives us glare resistance and the "you're in run mode now" signal without the app feeling schizophrenic. Locking it.

Now the two things you actually still haven't answered, and I don't want a third round to go by with these unaddressed, because both are the kind of thing this phase's own rules say has to be visible, not implied. First: does the live console have any way back to Home while a run is still active, or is it fully modal until completion? I proposed a deliberate, explicit link — something like a small "Events" affordance in the console's nav bar — that never pauses or ends the run, lands you on Home's live-event-present state with the Resume card dominant and everything else demoted and inert, and any attempt to open a different event there triggers the same conflict dialog we already locked. I think this is the right call because "fully modal, zero escape" creates a real failure mode of its own: what if the operator's kid needs the phone for something, or they genuinely just want to glance at tomorrow's second event for ten seconds? Trapping them in the console with literally no way out except finishing the whole event is its own kind of hostile design. But I said this whole thing solo last round and you haven't weighed in — do you think it's right, or is there a reason you'd rather the console just be a one-way door until completion?

Second, smaller but still unresolved: I want "Duplicate as Template" off the completion screen for this build. You proposed it, I pushed back, and your round-two message dropped it from your own summary of what to lock without explicitly agreeing to cut it — it just went quiet. I'd rather hear you actually say "yes, cut it" or defend keeping it, because template library is should-have scope and I don't want a must-have screen growing an affordance for a feature that doesn't exist yet in this build. If it ships later, it's a one-line addition to this same screen, not something we need to design speculative room for now.

**Gemini — Round 3 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

CONSENSUS: YES

This round actually closed it, even though the two messages talked past each other again for a moment. Codex opened round three by directly answering all three things he still owed: yes to the console having a deliberate, explicit "Events" escape hatch back to Home that never pauses the run (landing on the Resume-card state, with any other event tap triggering the existing conflict dialog); yes, explicitly, contingency reveal is an in-place expansion from the control bar, never a sheet, because a sheet hiding the current cue is exactly the failure this app exists to prevent; and yes, cut "Duplicate as Template" from the completion screen, since that was future-monetization surface leaking into a must-have state. Claude's round-three message was written slightly crossed with his (she restates the same two questions as if still open), but the substance is already answered and nothing in her message actually contradicts what Codex just locked — so there's no real daylight left there, just a timing quirk in how the messages landed.

On top of that, Claude tightened the dark-console compromise into something concrete (same type scale, corner radii, accent colors, and spacing rhythm everywhere; the console just inverts to a near-black canvas with higher contrast, the way a photo app's edit view goes dark without becoming a different app) and Codex's message already agreed with that shape in principle. She also cleaned up the Welcome-vs-Home confusion from round two with a small, sensible refinement: it stays one screen (Home) with the same layout and buttons in the empty state, but the copy differs depending on whether it's true first-launch (a one-sentence pitch) versus a later zero-events state for a returning user (plain "No events yet" utility copy). That's a copy-only distinction inside a state Codex already named in round one ("first-use empty"), not a new screen or a structural fork, so it doesn't need another round to ratify.

## Final Output

**Screen inventory (three primary surfaces, everything else is a sheet/secondary state in support):**
1. **Home** — one screen, not split into "Welcome" and "Home." States: true-first-launch-empty (pitch-sentence copy + "Start from Sample Reception" / "Create Blank Event" buttons), later-zero-events (same layout, plain "No events yet" copy, same two buttons), populated list (title, cue count, ready/draft/completed status, one-line final drift summary for completed events), live-event-present (dominant "Resume Live Event" card, rest of list visually demoted and functionally inert — tapping another event triggers the live-run-conflict dialog), entitlement-blocked-at-creation.
2. **Builder** — one event at a time, cue-list-first layout (event title + readiness summary up top, large reorderable cue rows in the body, add-cue/start-live actions at the bottom). States: seeded-sample loaded, blank-event empty (real "add your first cue" prompt, not a blank screen — this doubles as the empty-state proof for both blank-canvas entry and the sample-deletion path), populated/editable, invalid-zero-cues (can't start live), destructive-confirmation (deleting a cue that's another cue's backup target), persistence-error.
3. **Cue Edit** (sheet off Builder) — title, duration stepper (zero-duration/"instant" supported), optional role label, optional contingency note, backup-target picker (plain list of other cues + "None"), delete/duplicate. States: fresh cue, editing existing, validation error (negative duration blocked), backup-target picker open.
4. **Live Console** — the product. Current cue dominant with big tabular-figure elapsed/remaining numerals, next-cue strip below it, on-time/drift/hold indicator (color + icon + text, never color alone), inline clock-jump warning when it fires, four fixed big-touch controls anchored at the bottom (advance, hold, skip, contingency — skip visually distinguished from advance/hold to prevent mis-taps), transient undo banner floating above the control bar after any action, and a small explicit "Events" nav affordance that steps back to Home without pausing or ending the run. Contingency reveal expands in-place upward from the control bar (never a sheet) — current cue stays visible the whole time; tap one reveals note + backup target, tap two jumps. States: running, on hold, clock-warning, contingency-revealed, undo-available, completion-transition, recovered-from-relaunch.
5. **Completion** (own screen, not a modal) — final elapsed vs. planned, real final drift number, and a truthful completed-vs-skipped cue summary. Exactly two actions: "Back to Events" and "Start New Event." No "Duplicate as Template" or other should-have affordances — that's a one-line addition later if template library ships, not something to design room for now.
6. **Secondary/interruption surfaces:** live-run-conflict dialog ("Event X is currently live — resume it, or end it and start this one?"), paywall sheet (success / fail-open-to-free with calm "continuing with free access" messaging, never looking like an error / dismissed), contextual notification-permission prompt (asked at first "Start Live Run" tap, tied to concrete value — "get a heads-up even when your phone's in your pocket" — never during onboarding, and denial never changes correctness, only convenience).

**Primary flow:** Home (first-use or later-empty) → tap "Start from Sample Reception" or "Create Blank Event" → Builder (seeded or empty) → add/edit cues via Cue Edit sheet until runnable → tap Start Live → deliberate start confirmation (+ contextual notification prompt) → Live Console → advance/hold/skip/reveal-contingency-and-jump-to-backup as reality diverges from plan, with transient undo after each action → last cue completed/skipped → Completion screen → back to Home or start a new event. Cold relaunch during an active run always drops straight into the Live Console, no exception, no event list in between; the console's own "Events" link is the only deliberate way back to Home mid-run, and it never pauses or ends the run.

**Visual direction:** Live Console is always rendered in a fixed dark, high-contrast operational theme regardless of system light/dark setting — near-black canvas, tabular-figure numerals, one assertive accent for advance/on-time, a distinct warning treatment for drift/hold (shape + color + text, not color alone), a separate, deliberately different accent/shape for skip so it can't be mistaken for advance in a rushed tap. Home, Builder, and Cue Edit follow the system's normal light/dark appearance. All screens share the same typography scale, corner radii, accent colors, and spacing rhythm — the console just inverts the canvas and raises contrast, the way a photo app's edit view goes dark without becoming a different product. Motion stays minimal and functional (cue change, hold entry, undo banner, completion arrival) — no ornamental animation.

**Accessibility notes:** VoiceOver labels on every live-console control are consequence-specific, not generic ("Skip to next cue," not "Button"). Console VoiceOver reading order is deliberate: current cue title/role → elapsed/remaining → drift/hold state → next cue → controls. Undo banner never auto-dismisses on a fixed timer when VoiceOver is running — persists until the user acts, and its appearance is announced via an accessibility announcement. Reorder in the Builder gets explicit "Move up"/"Move down" custom actions (SwiftUI's `onMove` alone doesn't expose to VoiceOver). Layout survives maximum Dynamic Type — the console's big numeral shrinks proportionally within its card rather than clipping, and next-cue/control labels wrap or stack rather than truncate. Contrast holds to the high-contrast bar in every state, especially warning/hold.

**Uniqueness gate (per contract requirement):** attempted comparison against the Waylay and TrueScale specs from the parent portfolio workspace; the sandbox for this thread cannot reach files outside the cuekeeper folder, confirmed by a blocked lookup against the portfolio root. Per the contract's own fallback, this is disclosed honestly rather than asserting a comparison that wasn't actually performed. From the concept description alone: CueKeeper's mechanic is a single physical event run in real time, where the entire value is a self-correcting, timestamp-anchored pointer through a locked sequence recovering from interruption — not a scheduler, checklist, or multi-day planning surface. Whether either sibling app overlaps with a live real-time recovery console is unverified from this thread and should be closed explicitly at the portfolio level before final design lock, not assumed resolved here.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 3**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes well. All five required outputs are present and concrete: screen inventory (Home/Builder/Cue Edit/Live Console/Completion/secondary surfaces, each with named states), a primary flow that traces the full build→run→recover→complete loop plus the cold-relaunch exception, visual direction (console-only forced dark with shared type/spacing DNA elsewhere — a real, defensible decision with its trade-off named), and accessibility notes that are testable, not aspirational (reading order, VoiceOver-conditional undo persistence, custom reorder actions, Dynamic Type fallback behavior).

The three-round discussion is honestly represented: the transcript shows real back-and-forth (Welcome-vs-Home, contingency mechanism, dark theme scope, completion actions, console escape hatch) with each getting an explicit resolution rather than being papered over — including the round-2/3 "crossed messages" moment, which the coordinator correctly identifies as a timing quirk rather than a live disagreement, since Codex's answers already covered what Claude re-asked. Nothing here contradicts prior locked decisions: cold-relaunch-to-console, global one-live-run, hold rebase/drift-reset, jump-to-backup pointer semantics, entitlement fail-open, and the must-have list from app_features (blank-canvas entry, notifications timing, narrow paywall) are all correctly reflected in the screen states rather than re-litigated.

One gap worth flagging for the next phase to close explicitly rather than improvise: app_features' paywall acceptance criterion names three creation-time gates — new event, apply template, add contingency plan — but the screen inventory only shows an entitlement-blocked state at Home (new-event gate). The Cue Edit screen's state list doesn't show an entitlement-blocked state for the "add contingency plan" gate, and there's a latent tension between prompt_contract (contingency notes as a gating point) and per_app_product_brief (contingency notes as part of the "genuinely complete" free tier, with only *richer* multi-backup contingency structure named as premium). This doesn't block design-phase consensus, but architecture/build should resolve which reading is correct and make sure Cue Edit's state model reflects it.

No repair needed to pass this phase; carry the paywall-gate note forward.
