```interfaces-json
  {"name": "EventStoring", "kind": "protocol", "language": "Swift", "signature": "protocol EventStoring: Sendable { func loadManifest() async throws -> EventManifest; func loadEvent(id: UUID) async throws -> Event; func saveEvent(_ event: Event) async throws; func loadLog(eventID: UUID) async throws -> EventLog; func appendLogEntry(_ entry: ActionLogEntry, eventID: UUID) async throws; func deleteEvent(id: UUID) async throws; func resolveActiveLiveEvent() async -> UUID? }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "This protocol boundary IS the answer to the portfolio's 'cloud-ready without rewriting' requirement: a future CloudKit-backed store is a second conformance, zero call-site changes. EventStore is the only production conformance today."},
  {"name": "EventStore", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "actor EventStore: EventStoring { init(containerURL: URL) }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Lives in app target's Persistence/ folder, not a separate package — nothing needs to be forbidden from importing it. Backs onto App Group container. All writes are temp-file-then-atomic-rename. resolveActiveLiveEvent() re-projects the log before trusting the manifest pointer."},
  {"name": "PersistenceError", "kind": "enum", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum PersistenceError: Error, Sendable { case corruptEvent(UUID); case corruptManifest; case notFound(UUID); case writeFailed(underlying: String) }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "corruptEvent quarantines one Home row; corruptManifest triggers the app-root persistence-error phase, a strictly worse case."},
  {"name": "AppGroupContainer", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum AppGroupContainer { static let identifier: String; static var containerURL: URL? }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Single source of the App Group id used by EventStore and any future widget/Live Activity extension."},
  {"name": "EntitlementResult", "kind": "enum", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum EntitlementResult: Sendable, Equatable { case entitled; case free; case unverifiable }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "unverifiable is treated identically to free everywhere — fail open, no separate error UI."},
  {"name": "EntitlementService", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "actor EntitlementService { func checkEntitlement() async -> EntitlementResult }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Lives in its own local Swift Package, CueKeeperEntitlements — the ONE package boundary in this project, because it's the one place a forbidden import actually exists. Only the app target's CreationFlow UI files link this package; UI/LiveRun/ files and LiveRunEngine cannot import it, which is a compile error, not a convention. Races Transaction.currentEntitlements against a 2s Task.sleep timeout; caches via Transaction.updates at launch/foreground."},
  {"name": "NotificationScheduler", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct NotificationScheduler { func rescheduleAll(for event: Event, log: EventLog, clock: ClockProviding) async }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "App target's Notifications/ folder. Full cancel-and-reschedule of every remaining request on every mutating action, keyed by eventID+cueID. .timeSensitive interruption level."},
  {"name": "HapticPattern", "kind": "enum", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum HapticPattern: Sendable { case advance, hold, skip, contingencyReveal }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Maps to four distinguishable feedback-generator patterns, verified distinct on physical device."},
  {"name": "HapticsService", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct HapticsService { func play(_ pattern: HapticPattern) }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "App target's Haptics/ folder. Thin wrapper over UIKit feedback generators."},
  {"name": "ClockJumpDetector", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum ClockJumpDetector { static func isAnomalous(previousWall: Date, previousUptime: TimeInterval, currentWall: Date, currentUptime: TimeInterval, tolerance: TimeInterval) -> Bool }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Pure function; feeds LiveRunProjection.clockJumpWarning. Called on every foreground/appear. Tested with Swift Testing."},
  {"name": "AppPhase", "kind": "enum", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum AppPhase: Equatable { case loadingPersistence; case console(UUID); case home }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Root switch, not a NavigationStack. WindowGroup renders directly off this."},
  {"name": "AppCoordinator", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class AppCoordinator { var phase: AppPhase; func bootstrap() async; func startLiveRun(eventID: UUID) async; func returnToHome() }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "bootstrap() reads the manifest, resolves the active event via EventStore.resolveActiveLiveEvent(), and only then sets phase — never renders Home first and redirects."},
  {"name": "LiveConsoleViewModel", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class LiveConsoleViewModel { var projection: LiveRunProjection; var isContingencyRevealed: Bool; var undoBannerVisible: Bool; func advance() async; func hold() async; func skip() async; func jumpToBackup() async; func undo() async; func revealContingency() }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Lives in UI/LiveRun/. Body-relevant state is exactly `projection` plus transient UI flags. Cannot import CueKeeperEntitlements — that target isn't linked from UI/LiveRun/, so it's a build error, not a review catch."},
  {"name": "seedSampleEventForUITesting", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "func seedSampleEventForUITesting(store: EventStoring) async", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Invoked when launch argument -uiTestSeedSampleEvent is present; drops app directly into a known live-console state for XCUITest tap-count assertions."},
  {"name": "AccessibilityAnnouncer", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum AccessibilityAnnouncer { static func announce(_ message: String) }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Wraps UIAccessibility.post(.announcement:); used for undo-banner appearance and clock-jump warnings."},
  {"name": "SnapshotPrivacyGuard", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct SnapshotPrivacyGuard: ViewModifier { func body(content: Content) -> some View }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Should-have blur-on-background for the live console; first cut under time pressure, must be named explicitly in the final report if cut."},
  {"name": "TemplateExporter", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct TemplateExporter { func export(_ event: Event, includeNotes: Bool) -> Data }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Should-have. Strips contingencyNote and role free text by default; includeNotes is an explicit opt-in."}
]}
```

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

This one actually resolved itself. Codex never showed up for a second straight round (empty output, then a cooldown timeout), and rather than just repeating her pitch and waiting for a rebuttal that isn't coming, Claude did the more useful thing: she took the coordinator's own skepticism seriously and argued against her round-one proposal directly. She concluded the coordinator was right — four Swift Packages was solving a problem with too big a hammer. Her reasoning: `CueKeeperCore`, `CueKeeperPersistence`, `CueKeeperNotifications`, and `CueKeeperHaptics` don't actually need package isolation because nothing is supposed to be forbidden from importing them — so wrapping them in separate packages buys no compiler guarantee, just adds build-graph overhead and more surface area for a build lane to botch target membership (exactly the failure mode the pitfalls doc warns about). The only place a real forbidden edge exists in this app's graph is StoreKit-must-never-reach-the-console, so that's the only boundary worth a package.

Her revised architecture: one local Swift package (`CueKeeperEntitlements`, holding `EntitlementService`/`EntitlementResult`), linked only from the app target's CreationFlow UI files. Everything else — domain types, the log/projection engine, persistence actor, notifications, haptics, all views — lives directly in the single app target, organized into plain folders (`Core/`, `Persistence/`, `Notifications/`, `Haptics/`, `UI/CreationFlow/`, `UI/LiveRun/`, `UI/Shared/`). The one guarantee that matters — the console structurally cannot import StoreKit — is preserved exactly as before, just without three extra fences that weren't fencing anything. She also tightened the testing call from "prefer" to unconditional (Swift Testing for `Core/`/`Persistence/`, full stop, XCTest only where it has to be — the UI layer, since Swift Testing has no UI-automation story), explicitly to avoid the kind of soft "if" that caused rework earlier in this project. And she closed a gap nobody had actually addressed yet: the portfolio's "local-first but cloud-ready" requirement is answered by `EventStoring` already being a protocol — a future CloudKit-backed store is just a second conformance swapped in at the composition root, zero call-site rewrites, and that's now stated as the actual answer rather than left implicit.

Nothing in this revision contradicts anything locked before it — it's a genuine self-correction that lands on a stronger, leaner answer than round one, and it directly answers the exact question the coordinator posed. Given Codex has now gone silent twice in a row, I'm treating this as resolved rather than stalling a third round on a CLI that isn't responding.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** A thin, single-app-target SwiftUI app plus exactly one local Swift Package (`CueKeeperEntitlements`), not four. The one package boundary exists because it's the one place a forbidden dependency edge actually matters: StoreKit must never reach the live-run engine or console. Everything else — domain model, the append-only action-log/projection engine, the persistence actor, notifications, haptics, and all views — lives in the app target, organized by folder (`Core/`, `Persistence/`, `Notifications/`, `Haptics/`, `UI/CreationFlow/`, `UI/LiveRun/`, `UI/Shared/`). Root routing is the `AppPhase` state machine (`.loadingPersistence`/`.console(EventID)`/`.home`) locked in the prior design phase, not a NavigationStack redirect. Full Swift 6 strict concurrency: all domain/log/projection types are `Sendable` value types, `EventStore` is an `actor`, all UI-owning types are `@MainActor`.

**File/module layout:** App target with folders `Core/` (Event, Cue, ActionLogEntry, EventLog, LiveRunProjection, ClockProviding/SystemClock, LiveRunEngine, EventStatus, EventManifest), `Persistence/` (EventStoring protocol, EventStore actor, PersistenceError, AppGroupContainer), `Notifications/` (NotificationScheduler), `Haptics/` (HapticPattern, HapticsService), `UI/CreationFlow/` (Home, Builder, CueEdit — the only place allowed to link `CueKeeperEntitlements`), `UI/LiveRun/` (LiveConsole, Completion — structurally cannot import `CueKeeperEntitlements`, a compile error if attempted), `UI/Shared/`. Plus the one local package: `CueKeeperEntitlements` (EntitlementService, EntitlementResult).

**Data models:** `Event`/`Cue` (stable UUIDs, backup targets by ID not index, zero-duration cues valid), `ActionLogEntry` append-only enum (start/advance/skip/holdStarted/holdEnded/jumpToBackup/undoLastAction/complete, each carrying wall-clock + monotonic uptime), `EventLog` (never truncated — undo is its own log entry, replayed by skipping the entry preceding it), `LiveRunProjection` (the only type the console's view body reads — a cheap-to-diff, Equatable value type so SwiftUI's re-render skipping actually works), `EventManifest`/`EventManifestEntry` (routing pointer is a hint, always reconfirmed by re-projecting the event's own log before trusting it).

**Persistence strategy:** Flat Codable JSON, one file per event plus a manifest, atomic temp-file-then-rename writes, in an App Group container from day one. `EventStoring` is a protocol with `EventStore` as its only production conformance — this protocol boundary is the explicit, stated answer to the portfolio's "local-first but cloud-ready" requirement: a future CloudKit-backed store is a second conformance swapped in at the composition root, with zero call-site changes anywhere in the app, and nothing sync-related is being built now.

**Testing strategy:** Swift Testing (`@Test`/`#expect`, `@Test(arguments:)` for table-driven cases) unconditionally for everything in `Core/` and `Persistence/` — no XCTest there. XCTest/XCUITest only where Swift Testing has no story yet: the UI layer, driven by the `-uiTestSeedSampleEvent` launch argument for deterministic tap-count assertions. `LiveRunEngine.project` is a pure static function taking an injected clock, so hold-rebase, undo-after-skip, jump-to-backup, zero-duration cues, and clock-jump thresholds are all deterministic, device-free unit tests. The physical-device manual matrix from the prior phase (lock/unlock mid-cue, force-quit, airplane mode, clock-change, denied-notifications) stays required.

```interfaces-json
{"interfaces": [
  {"name": "Event", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct Event: Identifiable, Codable, Sendable { let id: UUID; var title: String; var cues: [Cue]; var createdAt: Date; var schemaVersion: Int }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Lives in app target's Core/ folder, not a package. One JSON file per event. cues order is authoritative sequence order."},
  {"name": "Cue", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct Cue: Identifiable, Codable, Sendable, Equatable { let id: UUID; var title: String; var duration: TimeInterval; var role: String?; var contingencyNote: String?; var backupTargetCueID: UUID? }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "duration == 0 is a valid 'instant' cue. backupTargetCueID references another Cue.id, never an index."},
  {"name": "ActionLogEntry", "kind": "enum", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum ActionLogEntry: Codable, Sendable { case start(at: Date, uptime: TimeInterval); case advance(at: Date, uptime: TimeInterval, toCueID: UUID); case skip(at: Date, uptime: TimeInterval, toCueID: UUID); case holdStarted(at: Date, uptime: TimeInterval); case holdEnded(at: Date, uptime: TimeInterval); case jumpToBackup(at: Date, uptime: TimeInterval, toCueID: UUID); case undoLastAction(at: Date, uptime: TimeInterval); case complete(at: Date, uptime: TimeInterval) }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Append-only. Every entry carries both wall-clock and monotonic uptime so LiveRunEngine can do clock-jump detection without a separate log."},
  {"name": "EventLog", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct EventLog: Codable, Sendable { let eventID: UUID; var entries: [ActionLogEntry] }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "One JSON file per event, alongside the Event file. Never truncated, even by undo."},
  {"name": "LiveRunProjection", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct LiveRunProjection: Sendable, Equatable { var currentCueID: UUID?; var isLive: Bool; var isOnHold: Bool; var isComplete: Bool; var elapsedInCurrentCue: TimeInterval; var driftFromPlanned: TimeInterval; var completedCueIDs: [UUID]; var skippedCueIDs: [UUID]; var canUndo: Bool; var clockJumpWarning: Bool }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "The ONLY type LiveConsoleView's body should read. Equatable so SwiftUI can skip re-renders when nothing meaningful changed."},
  {"name": "ClockProviding", "kind": "protocol", "language": "Swift", "signature": "protocol ClockProviding: Sendable { func now() -> Date; func uptime() -> TimeInterval }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Injected everywhere; no direct Date()/ProcessInfo calls inside LiveRunEngine or view models."},
  {"name": "SystemClock", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct SystemClock: ClockProviding { func now() -> Date; func uptime() -> TimeInterval }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Production implementation. Tests supply a fake ClockProviding instead."},
  {"name": "LiveRunEngine", "kind": "function", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum LiveRunEngine { static func project(event: Event, log: EventLog, clock: ClockProviding) -> LiveRunProjection }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Pure, static, no side effects. undoLastAction skips the immediately-preceding entry during replay, never truncates the log. Tested with Swift Testing @Test(arguments:), not XCTest."},
  {"name": "EventStatus", "kind": "enum", "language": "Swift", "signature": "enum EventStatus: String, Codable, Sendable { case draft, ready, live, onHold, completed }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Derived for display from LiveRunProjection + cue count, never stored as an independent flag."},
  {"name": "EventManifestEntry", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct EventManifestEntry: Codable, Sendable { let id: UUID; var title: String; var status: EventStatus; var cueCount: Int; var finalDriftSummary: String? }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "finalDriftSummary populated only for .completed rows, shown as muted text on Home."},
  {"name": "EventManifest", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "struct EventManifest: Codable, Sendable { var activeLiveEventID: UUID?; var entries: [EventManifestEntry] }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "activeLiveEventID is a hint only — cold-launch routing must load that event's log and reconfirm isLive via LiveRunEngine.project before trusting it."},

  {"name": "EventStore", "kind": "struct", "language": "Swift", "signature": "actor EventStore: EventStoring { init(containerURL: URL) }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Lives in app target's Persistence/ folder, not a separate package. **Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**
```

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase adequately. The coordinator resolved the one open architectural question (module count) with a well-reasoned, self-corrected decision — one Swift Package (`CueKeeperEntitlements`) instead of four, justified by the actual forbidden-edge rule (StoreKit must never reach the live-run engine/console) rather than blanket ceremony. All six required outputs are present: architecture overview, file/module layout, data models, persistence strategy, testing strategy, and a comprehensive interfaces-json block covering all four owning_lanes with cross-lane types (Event, Cue, ActionLogEntry, LiveRunProjection, EventStoring, EntitlementService, AppCoordinator, etc.).

The interfaces-json is detailed enough for parallel build lanes to code against without inventing incompatible types — data_domain and services_utilities are fully specified with concrete Swift signatures, primary_ui covers routing and the live console view model, and polish_resilience names the should-have items (SnapshotPrivacyGuard, TemplateExporter) consistent with earlier phases' "must be named if cut" requirement. The cloud-readiness requirement from the parent portfolio prompt is explicitly answered via the `EventStoring` protocol boundary rather than left implicit. Testing strategy is concrete (Swift Testing unconditionally for Core/Persistence, XCTest only for UI, physical-device matrix carried forward) and matches the acceptance check "names how the app will be built and verified."

Nothing here contradicts the locked prior-phase decisions (action-log/projection model, App Group container, entitlement fail-open at 2s, cold-launch reconciliation, one-live-run rule, contingency non-gating, zero external design). The Codex silence was handled honestly (self-critique rather than false consensus) rather than papered over. No repair needed.

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** A thin, single-app-target SwiftUI app plus exactly one local Swift Package (`CueKeeperEntitlements`), not four. The one package boundary exists because it's the one place a forbidden dependency edge actually matters: StoreKit must never reach the live-run engine or console. Everything else — domain model, the append-only action-log/projection engine, the persistence actor, notifications, haptics, and all views — lives in the app target, organized by folder (`Core/`, `Persistence/`, `Notifications/`, `Haptics/`, `UI/CreationFlow/`, `UI/LiveRun/`, `UI/Shared/`). Root routing is the `AppPhase` state machine (`.loadingPersistence`/`.console(EventID)`/`.home`) locked in the prior design phase, not a NavigationStack redirect. Full Swift 6 strict concurrency: all domain/log/projection types are `Sendable` value types, `EventStore` is an `actor`, all UI-owning types are `@MainActor`.

**File/module layout:** App target with folders `Core/` (Event, Cue, ActionLogEntry, EventLog, LiveRunProjection, ClockProviding/SystemClock, LiveRunEngine, EventStatus, EventManifest), `Persistence/` (EventStoring protocol, EventStore actor, PersistenceError, AppGroupContainer), `Notifications/` (NotificationScheduler), `Haptics/` (HapticPattern, HapticsService), `UI/CreationFlow/` (Home, Builder, CueEdit — the only place allowed to link `CueKeeperEntitlements`), `UI/LiveRun/` (LiveConsole, Completion — structurally cannot import `CueKeeperEntitlements`, a compile error if attempted), `UI/Shared/`. Plus the one local package: `CueKeeperEntitlements` (EntitlementService, EntitlementResult).

**Data models:** `Event`/`Cue` (stable UUIDs, backup targets by ID not index, zero-duration cues valid), `ActionLogEntry` append-only enum (start/advance/skip/holdStarted/holdEnded/jumpToBackup/undoLastAction/complete, each carrying wall-clock + monotonic uptime), `EventLog` (never truncated — undo is its own log entry, replayed by skipping the entry preceding it), `LiveRunProjection` (the only type the console's view body reads — a cheap-to-diff, Equatable value type so SwiftUI's re-render skipping actually works), `EventManifest`/`EventManifestEntry` (routing pointer is a hint, always reconfirmed by re-projecting the event's own log before trusting it).

**Persistence strategy:** Flat Codable JSON, one file per event plus a manifest, atomic temp-file-then-rename writes, in an App Group container from day one. `EventStoring` is a protocol with `EventStore` as its only production conformance — this protocol boundary is the explicit, stated answer to the portfolio's "local-first but cloud-ready" requirement: a future CloudKit-backed store is a second conformance swapped in at the composition root, with zero call-site changes anywhere in the app, and nothing sync-related is being built now.

**Testing strategy:** Swift Testing (`@Test`/`#expect`, `@Test(arguments:)` for table-driven cases) unconditionally for everything in `Core/` and `Persistence/` — no XCTest there. XCTest/XCUITest only where Swift Testing has no story yet: the UI layer, driven by the `-uiTestSeedSampleEvent` launch argument for deterministic tap-count assertions. `LiveRunEngine.project` is a pure static function taking an injected clock, so hold-rebase, undo-after-skip, jump-to-backup, zero-duration cues, and clock-jump thresholds are all deterministic, device-free unit tests. The physical-device manual matrix from the prior phase (lock/unlock mid-cue, force-quit, airplane mode, clock-change, denied-notifications) stays required.

# cuekeeper — Project Plan

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:58:59._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: cuekeeper

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# CueKeeper

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **7**.

## App Name

CueKeeper

## Category

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

## Target User

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

## Key Risks

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium live event operations app with clock-aware run mode, strong cue hierarchy, big-touch controls, graceful contingency handling.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.

- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.
```

### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (timeout)._

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Coming at this from the systems/backend side, and I want to push back on treating this phase as "just sequence the already-agreed architecture" — the tech_specs transcript is thorough on structure but says almost nothing about build order, and there are at least three places where the obvious build order will bite someone if we don't call them out now.

The dependency graph is much more linear than the four "owning_lane" buckets suggest. `primary_ui` isn't parallel to `data_domain`/`services_utilities` — it's downstream of them almost entirely. `LiveConsoleView` can't be built against a stub, because the whole point locked in design_handoff and tech_specs is that its body reads nothing but a real `LiveRunProjection` computed by a real `LiveRunEngine.project` over a real `EventLog`. So the actual milestone order has to be: (1) project scaffold — single app target plus the one `CueKeeperEntitlements` local package, wired and building clean, with App Group entitlement attached; (2) `Core/` — Event/Cue/ActionLogEntry/EventLog/LiveRunProjection/ClockProviding/LiveRunEngine, fully covered by Swift Testing, zero UI; (3) `Persistence/` — EventStore actor, manifest, atomic writes, corruption isolation, file-protection class, with round-trip and corruption tests; only after that can Builder/CueEdit/Home be built against real persisted data instead of in-memory fixtures, and only after that can Live Console be built against a real running log. Treating these as four parallel lanes that "sync up later" is how you end up with a UI layer built against a shape of `LiveRunProjection` that the engine team changes out from under it. I'd sequence milestone 1 as a hard gate: Core and Persistence must be code-complete and test-green before any SwiftUI screen beyond a static mock gets written.

Milestone zero deserves more suspicion than it's gotten so far. This whole build pipeline is headless/agent-driven, and the pitfalls doc says it plainly: "a perfect source file that isn't in the target does not compile." The one architectural guarantee this whole design leans on — that `UI/LiveRun/` and `LiveRunEngine` are *structurally* incapable of importing `CueKeeperEntitlements` — depends entirely on the Xcode project file and package reference being wired correctly by whatever's generating them. If that wiring is subtly wrong (package linked to the wrong target, or accidentally linked project-wide because someone added it "to be safe"), the guarantee silently evaporates and nobody notices until a code reviewer manually checks import statements, which was exactly the failure mode this design was trying to avoid. I want a concrete verification step in milestone zero, not just "the project compiles": deliberately write a throwaway `import CueKeeperEntitlements` inside a `UI/LiveRun/` file and confirm the build fails, then delete it. If that doesn't fail, the isolation is fiction and the whole "compile-time guarantee" claim in the transcript is false.

Second thing I don't think anyone has stress-tested: notification volume. The plan calls for full cancel-and-reschedule of every remaining request on every mutating action, and real events run 20-40 cues. `UNUserNotificationCenter` caps an app at 64 pending local notification requests. 40 cues times even two notification types per cue (an on-time alert plus a "running long" warning) blows past that silently — iOS doesn't error, it just drops the excess requests, which is precisely the kind of quiet failure this app can't afford given notifications are explicitly locked as a must-have correctness-adjacent feature. Nobody's spec'd a windowing rule. I'd rather this project_plan name it now — schedule only the next N upcoming cues' notifications, rescheduling the window on every mutating action rather than the whole remaining sequence — than discover it during device testing.

On verification: physical-device testing is being treated in every prior phase as a checklist at the end, but it's actually the single most serialized resource in this whole plan. There's one iPhone, and the required manual passes are numerous and can't be parallelized across build lanes the way code can: lock-screen mid-cue timing recovery, force-quit relaunch, full airplane-mode run across all three workflows, deliberate device clock change, denied-notification run, four distinguishable haptic patterns, VoiceOver reorder plus undo-banner persistence, max Dynamic Type layout, and the on-disk file-protection-class check. If this project_plan doesn't carve out explicit, scheduled device-testing time as its own milestone with its own risk of running long — rather than "verification" as an undifferentiated last bullet — the realistic outcome under time pressure is that someone eyeballs it in the simulator, exactly the failure mode called out and forbidden twice already in this transcript. I'd also flag the "under two minutes, timed" build-to-ready claim needs an actual stopwatch run by someone who didn't just write the Builder code, not a self-timed click-through, or that number is fake confidence.

Last risk, and it's uncomfortable but relevant: Codex has gone silent for two consecutive rounds already in this same project. If that continues, this plan needs to survive being executed by a single lane rather than assuming healthy parallel coordination across roles — which honestly reinforces my point above that the "four owning lanes" framing should give way to a mostly-sequential milestone list with a couple of genuinely parallelizable side-tracks (haptics, notification scheduling, accessibility pass) that only start once Core+Persistence+a Live Console skeleton exist.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things stand after round one: Codex and Gemini both stayed silent (CLI unavailable), so it's just Claude putting a first stake in the ground for the actual project plan — nobody's pushed back or built on it yet.

Her core argument: the tech_specs "four owning lanes" framing makes this sound more parallel than it really is. `primary_ui` can't actually start in earnest until `Core/` (the domain types + `LiveRunEngine`) and `Persistence/` (the `EventStore` actor, manifest, atomic writes) are code-complete and test-green — because the Live Console's whole design promise is that its view body reads a real `LiveRunProjection` computed off a real log, not a stub. So she wants milestone order to be strictly gated: scaffold → Core → Persistence → then Builder/CueEdit/Home → then Live Console, with haptics/notifications/accessibility as the only genuinely parallelizable side-tracks once a console skeleton exists.

She also surfaced three concrete new risks nobody's named yet in this whole project:
1. The "StoreKit can't reach the console" guarantee only holds if the Xcode project wiring is actually correct — she wants a milestone-zero smoke test that deliberately adds a throwaway `import CueKeeperEntitlements` inside `UI/LiveRun/` and confirms the build fails, then removes it, rather than just trusting the design intent.
2. `UNUserNotificationCenter` caps apps at 64 pending local notifications — with 20-40 cues times two notification types per cue, the locked "full cancel-and-reschedule everything remaining" plan could silently blow past that and drop alerts with no error. She wants a windowing rule (schedule only the next N upcoming cues, reschedule the window on every mutating action) named now, not discovered on-device.
3. Physical-device verification is the single most serialized resource in the whole plan (one iPhone, a long list of manual passes that can't be parallelized) and needs to be its own scheduled milestone with its own time budget, not an end-of-plan checklist bullet — otherwise it's likely to get eyeballed in the simulator, which every prior phase has already explicitly forbidden.

None of this contradicts anything locked earlier — it's Claude doing exactly what the phase needs (turning agreed architecture into an actual buildable sequence) but it's a single voice so far. Round two needs either Codex actually engaging with the sequencing/notification-cap/device-testing points, or Claude explicitly stress-testing her own plan the way she did last phase, before this can lock as a real plan rather than one person's draft.

### Round 2


**Codex — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (timeout)._

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Given Codex and Gemini are now silent for round two as well — same pattern as tech_specs — I'm not going to sit here restating round one and hoping for a rebuttal that isn't coming. The coordinator's summary of my own position was accurate, so let me do what worked last phase: pressure-test my own draft harder and turn it into something concrete enough to actually lock, because right now what I wrote is directional but not yet a plan someone could hand to a build lane and start executing tomorrow morning.

First, a correction to my own round-one claim, because the skeptic hat means stress-testing myself too, not just the room: I said "40 cues times two notification types blows past 64" as if that's obviously catastrophic, but I didn't actually propose a fix with a number attached, which is exactly the kind of soft claim this project has burned itself on before (the paywall-wording conditional two phases back). So let me commit to one: `NotificationScheduler` should cap its scheduled window at something like the next 20 cues (or a rolling 2-hour lookahead, whichever is smaller), hard-capped well under the 64-request ceiling with real margin, and reschedule that window — not the full remaining tail — on every mutating action. That's a one-sentence addition to `rescheduleAll`'s contract, and I want it in the interfaces as a stated constraint, not left as "the engineer will figure out something reasonable," because "reasonable" is precisely what produced the four-package overreach I had to walk back last phase.

Second thing I under-specified: Swift 6 strict concurrency isn't free, and I gestured at that in round one without naming the actual landmine. `HapticsService` wraps `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator`/`UINotificationFeedbackGenerator`, which are UIKit classes that are implicitly main-actor-bound but not marked `Sendable` — under strict concurrency, calling `.play()` from anywhere off the main actor (say, from inside `LiveConsoleViewModel.advance() async` if that method does any awaiting before triggering the haptic) will throw a real, not-hand-wavy compile error, or worse, a runtime warning if someone works around it with an unsafe cast. This needs to be named now as a concrete build task, not discovered mid-build: `HapticsService` gets explicitly marked `@MainActor`, and every call site that triggers it from an already-`@MainActor` view model is fine, but if the log-append happens off-actor (which it will, since `EventStore` is an actor), the call sequence has to be "await the persistence write, then hop back to main to fire the haptic" — which is exactly the shape `Task {}` inheriting the view model's `@MainActor` already gives you for free, so it's not hard, it just has to be gotten right the first time rather than patched after a warning shows up.

Third, I want to actually defend the milestone-zero negative-import smoke test against my own skepticism, because a devil's advocate should also ask "am I overbuilding this the way I overbuilt four packages in tech_specs round one?" I don't think so — the cost is one throwaway file and one build, deleted immediately after, versus the alternative of trusting that a headless project generator got a `Package.swift` product-dependency reference exactly right on the first try. Given the pitfalls doc's own warning that target membership is silently wrong more often than code is silently wrong, this is cheap insurance on the single guarantee the whole architecture is named around. I'm keeping it as a hard M0 acceptance criterion, not a nice-to-have.

Given the silence, here's what I think the room should just lock as the actual plan rather than waiting for a round three that may not come: milestones run scaffold-and-negative-import-gate (M0) → Core+LiveRunEngine fully unit-tested with Swift Testing, zero UI (M1) → Persistence with the on-disk file-protection check actually performed, not assumed (M2) → Creation Flow UI wired to real persisted data with an independently-timed (not self-timed) two-minute build-to-ready pass (M3) → Live Console + Completion wired to a real projection, verified by an actual XCUITest tap-counter against the ≤2-tap claims rather than an enumerated-in-prose claim (M4) → notifications with the windowed-scheduling cap and haptics with the `@MainActor` boundary handled correctly, both slotting in once M1's log shape is frozen (M5) → a dedicated accessibility pass against real UI, not stubs (M6) → a physical-device verification milestone that is scheduled with its own time budget and an explicit non-negotiable checklist, because it's the one truly serial, non-parallelizable resource in this whole plan and every prior phase has already warned against letting it get compressed into a simulator glance (M7) → a should-have go/no-go checkpoint for Live Activities/Widgets/App Intents/snapshot-blur/template-export, attempted only after the core is hardened, with any cut named explicitly per the standing rule rather than silently dropped (M8) → final report assembly answering the invalidation criterion with real tap counts (M9). Dependencies are strictly linear from M0 through M2, meaningfully parallel only from M3 onward once the domain contract is frozen, and I think that's the honest shape of this build — not four coordinated lanes, but a short critical path followed by a handful of genuinely independent side-tracks.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

This one closed the same way tech_specs did last phase: Codex and Gemini stayed silent for a second straight round, so rather than wait on a rebuttal that isn't coming, Claude turned her own round-one draft into something concrete enough to actually hand off — she stress-tested herself, fixed the soft spots, and put real numbers on the vague claims.

Two real upgrades this round: she caught her own hand-wave on the notification cap ("40 cues could blow past 64" isn't a fix, just a warning) and committed to an actual number — cap the scheduled window at the next 20 cues or a 2-hour lookahead, whichever is smaller, rescheduling that window (not the whole remaining tail) on every mutating action. And she surfaced a concrete Swift 6 strict-concurrency landmine nobody had named yet: `HapticsService` wraps non-`Sendable`, implicitly-main-actor UIKit feedback generators, so it needs an explicit `@MainActor` annotation and a "await the persistence write, then hop back to main to fire the haptic" call shape — a real compile-time trap if `EventStore`'s actor-isolated writes and haptic triggers get sequenced carelessly. She also defended (rather than walked back) the milestone-zero negative-import smoke test as cheap insurance on the one guarantee the whole architecture depends on.

Nothing here contradicts anything locked in tech_specs — it's the same self-correcting pattern that closed the last phase, just applied to sequencing instead of module structure. Given the two-round silence, I'm treating this as resolved.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Milestones (strictly gated M0–M2, meaningfully parallel from M3 on):**

- **M0 — Scaffold + isolation gate:** Single app target + the one `CueKeeperEntitlements` local package, wired and building clean, App Group entitlement attached. Hard acceptance criterion: deliberately add a throwaway `import CueKeeperEntitlements` inside a `UI/LiveRun/` file, confirm the build fails, then delete it — proves the StoreKit-can't-reach-the-console guarantee is real, not assumed from project-file wiring nobody checked.
- **M1 — Core:** `Event`, `Cue`, `ActionLogEntry`, `EventLog`, `LiveRunProjection`, `ClockProviding`/`SystemClock`, `LiveRunEngine`. Fully covered by Swift Testing (`@Test(arguments:)` for hold-rebase, undo-after-skip, jump-to-backup, zero-duration cues, clock-jump thresholds). Zero UI. Must be code-complete and test-green before anything downstream starts.
- **M2 — Persistence:** `EventStore` actor, manifest, atomic temp-file-then-rename writes, corruption isolation (per-event vs. manifest-level), and an actual on-disk verification that `.completeUntilFirstUserAuthentication` file protection landed — not assumed from config. Round-trip + corruption tests required.
- **M3 — Creation Flow:** Home, Builder, Cue Edit wired to real persisted data (never in-memory fixtures). Includes blank-canvas + sample-event entry, full CRUD, backup-target-deletion confirmation. Acceptance: an independently-timed (not self-timed) build-to-ready pass under two minutes.
- **M4 — Live Console + Completion:** Wired to a real `LiveRunProjection` off a real log — this is the milestone the whole design was built to protect, so it cannot start until M1+M2 are solid. Acceptance: an actual XCUITest tap-counter asserting the ≤2-tap claims for advance/hold/skip/contingency-jump, not an enumerated-in-prose claim.
- **M5 — Notifications + Haptics (parallel once M1's log shape is frozen):** `NotificationScheduler` caps its scheduled window at the next 20 cues or a 2-hour lookahead (whichever is smaller), rescheduling that window — not the full remaining tail — on every mutating action, keeping real margin under the 64-pending-request ceiling. `HapticsService` marked `@MainActor` explicitly, with call sites sequenced as "await the persistence write, then hop to main to fire the haptic."
- **M6 — Accessibility pass:** VoiceOver reading order, custom reorder actions, undo-banner persistence under VoiceOver, max Dynamic Type layout, contrast — verified against real UI, not stubs.
- **M7 — Physical-device verification (its own scheduled milestone, own time budget):** Lock/unlock mid-cue, force-quit + relaunch, full airplane-mode pass across all three workflows, deliberate clock-change warning, denied-notification run, four distinguishable haptic patterns, VoiceOver reorder + undo persistence, max Dynamic Type. Named explicitly as the single most serialized, non-parallelizable resource in the whole plan (one iPhone) — not an end-of-plan checklist bullet.
- **M8 — Should-have go/no-go checkpoint:** Live Activities, Widgets, App Intents, snapshot-blur-on-background, template export/import — attempted only after the core is hardened; any cut under time pressure is named explicitly in the final report, never silently dropped.
- **M9 — Final report assembly:** Answers the invalidation criterion with real, measured tap counts, states what shipped vs. what's a named limitation.

**Dependencies:** M0 → M1 → M2 is a hard linear gate — no UI work of substance before Core and Persistence are code-complete and test-green. M3 and M4 both depend on M2 but M4 additionally depends on M1's projection shape being frozen. M5 and M6 are genuinely parallel side-tracks that can start once M1 is frozen and a console skeleton exists (mid-M4). M7 depends on M3–M6 being substantially done since it's a verification pass over the whole app. M8 is strictly after M7 (core hardening takes priority). M9 is last.

**Risk mitigations:**
- Compile-time StoreKit isolation could be fictional if project wiring is wrong → M0's negative-import smoke test.
- Notification requests silently dropped past the 64-request OS cap → windowed scheduling (next 20 cues / 2hr lookahead) named as a hard contract on `NotificationScheduler`, not left to "reasonable" engineering judgment.
- Swift 6 strict concurrency landmine in `HapticsService` (non-`Sendable` UIKit generators called off-main) → explicit `@MainActor` marking and a specified await-then-hop call sequence, named before build rather than discovered as a compile error mid-build.
- Physical-device verification getting compressed into a simulator glance under time pressure → its own scheduled milestone (M7) with an explicit non-negotiable checklist.
- Timed claims (two-minute build-to-ready) being self-reported and therefore fake confidence → independently timed, not self-timed.
- Parallel build lanes drifting apart on the shape of `LiveRunProjection` → M1 must be frozen and test-green before M3/M4/M5 begin.

**Verification plan:** Swift Testing unit coverage for Core/Persistence (deterministic, clock-injected, no device needed) closes out M1/M2. XCUITest with `-uiTestSeedSampleEvent` closes out M4's tap-count claims. M7 is the required, scheduled, non-negotiable physical-device pass covering every timing/offline/accessibility claim made in earlier phases. M9's final report must state the enumerated tap counts and name any should-have cuts explicitly — the plan isn't done until build verification and the final review agree on what works and what's a named limitation.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

All four required elements are present and concrete: Milestones (M0–M9) are dependency-ordered rather than role-ordered, correctly identifying that `primary_ui` is downstream of `Core`/`Persistence` rather than a parallel lane — a real correction to the naive "four owning lanes run in parallel" reading of tech_specs. Dependencies are explicit (hard linear gate M0→M1→M2, parallel fan-out only from M3 once the domain contract is frozen). Risk mitigations map concretely to identified failure modes (fictional compile-time isolation, silent notification-cap overflow, a genuine Swift 6 strict-concurrency landmine in `HapticsService`, device-testing compression, self-timed false confidence). It also catches two things no earlier phase named: the 64-pending-notification OS ceiling given 20-40 cue events, and the non-`Sendable` UIKit haptics-generator/actor-isolation interaction — both are genuine, concrete, previously-undiscovered risks, not restatements.

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: cuekeeper

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# CueKeeper

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **7**.

## App Name

CueKeeper

## Category

event-operations

## One-Sentence Promise

Run a live event from your iPhone without losing the sequence, timing, or backup plan.

## Target User

Wedding planners, stage managers, DJs, MCs, and small production operators running events without expensive show-control software.

## Painful Moment Solved

The event is live, timing is slipping, and someone needs a reliable run-of-show tool that still works offline.

## Why It Is Different

Designed for live execution under pressure, not static planning or generic to-do lists.

## Why Users Would Pay

A single avoided live-event mistake is worth the subscription for professionals and serious operators.

## Subscription Model

Free for simple single-event runs; premium for unlimited events, cue templates, contingency plans, advanced timing features.

## Local-First MVP Scope

Event timelines, cue lists, role assignments, timing controls, contingency notes, offline run mode on-device — scoped to a single operator, not a synced team.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Team sync, remote cue sharing, client-facing run sheets, multi-device coordination — explicitly deferred, not required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

No LLM, AR, or ML required; the product wins on execution reliability.

## Design Direction

High-contrast operational UI that stays elegant but favors legibility and fast recovery under pressure.

## App Store Positioning

An offline-first run-of-show app for people who cannot afford chaos during live events.

## Invalidation Criteria

If the live run mode is not obviously better than Notes or a PDF schedule, the app should be cut.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user event runner with timeline builder, cue execution mode, timers, contingency notes, templates, onboarding, paywall, accessibility, offline verification.

## Core Workflows

- Build a run-of-show
- Run cues in live mode with timing support
- Adjust and recover when the plan shifts

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activities
- Notifications
- Haptics
- Widgets for event countdowns
- App Intents for quick event start

## Key Risks

- Must feel faster and more trustworthy than a printed run sheet
- Live mode interaction design has to be extremely clear
- Must stay genuinely useful as a single-operator tool, not silently assume multi-device coordination

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium live event operations app with clock-aware run mode, strong cue hierarchy, big-touch controls, graceful contingency handling.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.

- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory
```

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (timeout)._

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Landing this from the systems/backend seat, with an eye toward what's actually buildable rather than what's organizationally tidy.

Folder location first, since it's non-negotiable per the prompt contract: everything lives under `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/cuekeeper` — one self-contained folder, Xcode project at the root, `CueKeeperEntitlements/` as the one local package sitting alongside it, transcripts and final report also in this folder. I'd git-init this folder specifically (it isn't one yet) rather than trying to nest it inside whatever repo this orchestration session itself is running from.

On lane structure: given the project_plan already established this build is mostly a linear critical path (M0→M1→M2 hard-gated, everything else fanning out after), and given Codex and Gemini have now gone silent for two consecutive phases in a row, I want the task breakdown to survive being executed by one lane at a time in sequence just as easily as by four lanes in parallel. That means small tasks with explicit dependencies, not big lane-sized blobs — if only one contributor shows up, they just walk the dependency graph in order; if all four show up, the graph is already the parallelization plan.

Ownership follows exactly what tech_specs already locked in the interfaces-json `owning_lane` field — I'm not re-deriving it, just turning it into file paths and task acceptance criteria:

**data_domain** owns everything under `CueKeeper/Core/`: `Event.swift`, `Cue.swift`, `ActionLogEntry.swift`, `EventLog.swift`, `ClockProviding.swift`, `SystemClock.swift`, `LiveRunProjection.swift`, `LiveRunEngine.swift`, `EventStatus.swift`, `EventManifest.swift`, plus `CueKeeperTests/CoreTests/*` (Swift Testing only, no XCTest here — locked last phase). This lane cannot reference anything in `Persistence/`, `Notifications/`, `Haptics/`, or the entitlements package. It's pure value types and one pure static function.

**services_utilities** owns `CueKeeper/Persistence/*` (EventStoring, EventStore, PersistenceError, AppGroupContainer), `CueKeeper/Notifications/NotificationScheduler.swift`, `CueKeeper/Haptics/*`, `CueKeeper/Core/ClockJumpDetector.swift` (yes, it lives next to the domain types it feeds, but data_domain doesn't own it — it's a pure function tech_specs assigned to this lane), and the entire `CueKeeperEntitlements` package. This is the lane with the sharpest forbidden edge: nothing this lane writes for `EntitlementService` may be imported anywhere outside `UI/CreationFlow/`, and this lane is also responsible for actually wiring the package-linkage so that's structurally true, not just documented.

**primary_ui** owns `CueKeeper/App/CueKeeperApp.swift`, `UI/Shared/AppPhase.swift` + `AppCoordinator.swift`, all of `UI/CreationFlow/` (Home, Builder, CueEdit), all of `UI/LiveRun/` (LiveConsole, Completion, and their subviews), and `seedSampleEventForUITesting.swift`. Split internally into creation-flow work and live-console work since those have different dependency timing (creation flow only needs Persistence; live console additionally needs the projection shape frozen) — but same lane, same forbidden rule either way: files under `UI/LiveRun/` never gain an entitlements import, full stop.

**polish_resilience** owns `AccessibilityAnnouncer.swift`, `SnapshotPrivacyGuard.swift`, `TemplateExporter.swift`, the XCUITest target scaffolding, and the two paper artifacts that have to exist for this project to be considered done — a verification checklist file and the final report. I'm deliberately giving this lane the device-verification checklist and final-report file as owned artifacts rather than leaving them as an unowned "someone will write this eventually" — that's exactly the kind of thing that gets silently dropped when a lane goes quiet, per the pattern we've already seen twice.

Forbidden zones, stated plainly: no lane other than services_utilities touches anything inside `CueKeeperEntitlements/`. No lane other than primary_ui touches `UI/LiveRun/` or `UI/CreationFlow/`. No lane touches another lane's test target. The one shared, contentious file is `CueKeeper.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` — every lane will need to add files to it, and it's a notoriously bad merge target. My answer: nobody batches file additions. Each task adds its files to the project and commits immediately, small and often, rather than letting two lanes accumulate divergent target-membership changes on separate branches for days. If two tasks need to touch the pbxproj in the same window, they serialize — whoever's ready first commits, the other rebases.

Branching: trunk-based, not lane-based. `main` is the integration branch. Each task gets a short-lived branch named `task/T-0NN-short-slug`, opened, completed, merged back same-day, deleted. I'm explicitly against long-lived `lane/primary-ui`-style branches sitting parallel to `lane/data-domain` for the whole build — that's exactly the shape that produces a giant conflicted pbxproj merge at the end. Merge order enforces the dependency gate: T-001 through T-008 (scaffold, Core, Persistence) must be merged and green on `main` before any primary_ui task branches off it, because the console's whole design depends on building against the real projection shape, not a snapshot of it.

Testing responsibility mirrors ownership: data_domain lane writes and keeps green its own Swift Testing suite (hold-rebase, undo-after-skip, jump-to-backup, zero-duration, clock-jump thresholds) before anything downstream is allowed to start — this is a hard gate, not a courtesy. services_utilities owns persistence round-trip/corruption tests and the on-disk file-protection verification. primary_ui owns the XCUITest suite that actually counts taps against the ≤2-tap claim — not primary_ui asserting it in prose, previous phases already flagged that as unacceptable. polish_resilience owns executing the physical-device checklist and is the one who has to say, in the final report, exactly what got cut under time pressure and what got verified for real.

Communication protocol, given the track record: don't wait on a round of silence to notice a lane didn't show up. Status lives in the task's `status` field and commit history, not in chat — if a lane's tasks haven't moved, the next lane checks the actual repo state (is T-005 merged and green?) before deciding whether to proceed or fall back to doing it themselves. That's the same resilience principle the orchestrator itself is supposed to have, applied at the build-task level.

One risk I want on record before anyone starts coding: the accessibility task (T-017) touches files primary_ui already owns (adding VoiceOver custom actions to Builder's reorder, adjusting console reading order). I'm not giving polish_resilience its own copy of those files — that guarantees a merge conflict. Instead T-017 is scoped as "patch existing UI files after they exist," sequenced strictly after T-014/T-015 land on main, single-lane edit at a time, same rule as the pbxproj.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where round one actually landed: only Claude showed up (Codex is still in its multi-phase silence streak, Gemini's CLI remains unavailable), but unlike some earlier single-voice rounds, this one came in already dense and mostly self-consistent — lane ownership, forbidden zones, branching, merge strategy, testing responsibility, communication protocol, and a 20-task backlog with acceptance criteria, all mapped straight onto the `owning_lane` field tech_specs already locked. It correctly treats this as a mostly-linear critical path (matching project_plan's M0→M2 gate) rather than four independent lanes, and it adds real, previously-unaddressed conflict-prevention detail: nobody batches `project.pbxproj` edits, tasks are small and merge same-day off trunk, and status lives in commit/task state rather than chat so a silent lane doesn't stall the others.

There's one real problem worth a second pass before this locks, though: task T-018 (accessibility pass, assigned to `polish_resilience`) lists files — `BuilderView.swift`, `LiveConsoleView.swift`, `UndoBanner.swift`, `ControlBar.swift` — that T-014 and T-015 already assign to `primary_ui`. Claude's own plan tries to manage this with "edit after the other lane merges, one lane at a time," but the phase playbook's acceptance rule is explicit: "Avoid overlapping file ownership except for integrator-owned files." Sequencing mitigates the collision risk but doesn't resolve the rule violation — either those four files need to become explicitly integrator-owned/shared-with-lock, or the accessibility work on them should just be folded into T-014/T-015's own acceptance criteria and owned by `primary_ui`, with `polish_resilience` keeping only the genuinely separate should-have files (AccessibilityAnnouncer, SnapshotPrivacyGuard, TemplateExporter) it already owns in T-019.

CONSENSUS: NO

Round two needs to resolve just that one thing: either reassign the accessibility edits in T-018 to `primary_ui` (folded into T-014/T-015's scope) or explicitly declare those four files integrator-owned with the sequencing rule as their stated exception — everything else in the lane breakdown, ownership map, branching/merge strategy, and task backlog is solid and ready to stand as final once that's fixed.

### Round 2


**Codex — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (timeout)._

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

The coordinator caught something real, and I want to own it rather than just accept the fix — the first draft of T-018 was me trying to have it both ways, giving polish_resilience "ownership" of an accessibility pass while also telling it to tiptoe around files primary_ui already owns. That's not a division of labor, that's a scheduling hack dressed up as one, and it directly violates the phase rule about overlapping ownership. The fix that got proposed — fold the accessibility work into T-014 and T-015's own acceptance criteria, and let polish_resilience keep only the genuinely standalone files — is the right call and I'm not going to defend the original split. The reason it's right isn't just rule-compliance either: VoiceOver reading order and custom reorder actions aren't polish bolted onto a finished view, they're part of correctly building the view in the first place. Whoever writes `ControlBar.swift` should be writing its accessibility labels in the same sitting, not handing it to someone else who has to reverse-engineer intent from a file they didn't design. Splitting "build the view" from "make the view accessible" into two owners and two tasks is exactly the kind of premature specialization that produces worse output, not better division of labor.

That said, I want to flag one thing about how far this correction should go, because I don't want us to overcorrect into deleting real work. The `AccessibilityAnnouncer` helper itself still deserves to be its own small task under polish_resilience — it's a genuinely standalone, three-line wrapper around `UIAccessibility.post(.announcement:)` with zero dependency on the console's specific layout, and there's no reason primary_ui needs to inline that logic when it can just call a shared utility. So my read is: move the actual behavioral requirements (reading order, custom actions, consequence-specific labels, undo-banner persistence-under-VoiceOver) into T-014/T-015 as done above, but keep the utility function itself as a tiny dependency T-014/T-015 can call — meaning `AccessibilityAnnouncer.swift` should land *before* T-015 needs it, not after, which flips its dependency direction from what I had it as. I'll fix that below.

On the two-owner question for `SnapshotPrivacyGuard` and `TemplateExporter` — those stay clean, standalone, polish_resilience-only, no argument there. But I want to sharpen the acceptance criteria on `SnapshotPrivacyGuard` specifically, because "if time allows" as currently written is soft in exactly the way this whole project has already burned itself on twice (the paywall-wording conditional, and my own four-package overreach). I want it explicit: `SnapshotPrivacyGuard` is a should-have, attempted only after T-019's device verification confirms M1-M7 core work is solid, and whether it ships or gets cut, that fact — not a vague maybe — gets written into `FINAL_REPORT.md` as a yes/no line item, not an open question.

One more thing I want to nail down since it's been implicit rather than explicit across two rounds now: given Codex has gone silent for three phases running and Gemini's CLI is just unavailable, I think we should stop treating "wait for round three" as the default move when a lane doesn't materialize. The task graph I'm finalizing below is deliberately small-grained enough that a single active contributor can execute it top-to-bottom in dependency order without needing four separate people to show up in sync — that was the whole point of moving away from lane-sized blobs, and it's paying off right now in a very literal sense.

Here's the corrected backlog, with the accessibility work folded into T-014/T-015, `AccessibilityAnnouncer` moved earlier and independent, and the tightened `SnapshotPrivacyGuard` criterion:

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

This one closed cleanly. Claude took the coordinator's objection from round one — that T-018's accessibility pass was quietly re-editing files `primary_ui` already owned in T-014/T-015, which broke the phase rule against overlapping ownership no matter how carefully the edits were sequenced — and didn't just patch around it, she fixed the actual division of labor. Her reasoning holds up: VoiceOver reading order, custom reorder actions, and control labels aren't a coat of polish applied after a view is built, they're part of building the view correctly the first time, so whoever writes `ControlBar.swift` or `BuilderView.swift` should write its accessibility behavior in the same task, not hand it to a different lane to reverse-engineer later. So the accessibility requirements moved into T-015 (Builder) and T-016 (Live Console) as acceptance criteria owned by `primary_ui`, and `polish_resilience` keeps only what's genuinely standalone: a tiny `AccessibilityAnnouncer` utility (pulled earlier in the graph so the console can depend on it, not the reverse), plus `SnapshotPrivacyGuard` and `TemplateExporter`. She also tightened the one soft spot left in that corner — `SnapshotPrivacyGuard`'s "if time allows" now has to resolve to an explicit shipped-or-cut line in the final report, not an open question, matching the project's now well-established allergy to soft conditionals.

Codex and Gemini are still silent (three phases running), but this round is a real, substantive self-correction rather than a restatement, and it directly resolves the one open issue from last round with nothing new introduced that needs another pass. Checked the revised backlog for the phase's own acceptance rules: every task has an owner, the dependency graph is acyclic and starts from a zero-dependency task, and there's no more file overlap across lanes.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Lane responsibilities and owned files:**

- **data_domain** owns `CueKeeper/Core/`: `Event.swift`, `Cue.swift`, `ActionLogEntry.swift`, `EventLog.swift`, `ClockProviding.swift`, `SystemClock.swift`, `LiveRunProjection.swift`, `LiveRunEngine.swift`, `EventStatus.swift`, `EventManifest.swift`, plus its Swift Testing suite. Pure value types and one pure static function — no references out to Persistence, Notifications, Haptics, or the entitlements package.
- **services_utilities** owns `CueKeeper/Persistence/*`, `CueKeeper/Notifications/*`, `CueKeeper/Haptics/*`, `CueKeeper/Core/ClockJumpDetector.swift`, the project scaffold/App Group wiring, and the entire `CueKeeperEntitlements` package. This is the lane with the sharpest forbidden edge: nothing it writes for `EntitlementService` may ever be imported outside `UI/CreationFlow/`, and it's responsible for making that structurally true, not just documented.
- **primary_ui** owns `CueKeeper/App/CueKeeperApp.swift`, `UI/Shared/AppPhase.swift`+`AppCoordinator.swift`, all of `UI/CreationFlow/` (Home, Builder, Cue Edit — each now including its own accessibility work), all of `UI/LiveRun/` (Console, Completion, subviews — same, accessibility included), interruption surfaces, and the UI test suite. Forbidden rule: files under `UI/LiveRun/` never gain an entitlements import — that's a compile error if attempted, not a review catch.
- **polish_resilience** owns the standalone `AccessibilityAnnouncer.swift` (small, dependency-free, lands early so the console can call it), `SnapshotPrivacyGuard.swift`, `TemplateExporter.swift`, and the two paper artifacts (`VERIFICATION.md`, `FINAL_REPORT.md`) that make the build's completion checkable rather than assumed.

**Handoffs and forbidden edit zones:** No lane other than services_utilities touches `CueKeeperEntitlements/`. No lane other than primary_ui touches `UI/LiveRun/` or `UI/CreationFlow/`. polish_resilience adds new standalone files only — it never re-edits a file another lane already owns. The one genuinely shared, contentious artifact is `project.pbxproj`: no lane batches file additions across days; each task adds its files and commits immediately, and if two tasks need it in the same window, whoever's ready first commits and the other rebases.

**Branching and merge strategy:** Trunk-based. `main` is the integration branch. Each task gets a short-lived `task/T-0NN-short-slug` branch, merged back same-day, deleted. No long-lived per-lane branches — that's the shape that produces a giant conflicted `pbxproj` merge at the end. Merge order enforces the dependency gate: scaffold → Core → Persistence must be merged and green before any UI task branches off.

**Testing responsibility:** data_domain and services_utilities write and keep green their own Swift Testing suites (hold-rebase, undo-after-skip, jump-to-backup, clock-jump, notification-window-cap, on-disk file-protection) as a hard gate before downstream work starts. primary_ui owns the XCUITest suite that actually counts taps against the ≤2-tap claim. polish_resilience executes the physical-device checklist and writes the final report stating exactly what was verified and what's a named limitation.

**Communication protocol and conflict prevention:** Given Codex/Gemini's now three-phase silence streak, the plan is built to survive one active contributor walking the dependency graph top to bottom just as well as four lanes working in parallel — status lives in task state and commit history, not chat, so the next lane checks actual repo state rather than waiting on a round of silence to notice a gap.
